title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Solving Two-Flavor Neutrino Oscillations in Vacuum and Matter Environments for Atmospheric and Reactor Neutrinos
summary: Neutrino oscillations provide crucial insights into fundamental particle physics, with two-flavor approximations effectively describing reactor and atmospheric phenomena. This paper explores the use of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to solve the governing differential equations for neutrino evolution in both vacuum and matter environments. We review the theoretical framework, including vacuum mixing and the Mikheyev-Smirnov-Wolfenstein (MSW) effect in matter, and demonstrate PINN implementations for vacuum and constant-density profiles. This Machine learning based approach for reactor (low-energy) and atmospheric (high-energy) neutrinos shows high precision similar to analytical solutions, with mean squared errors of the order of \(10^{-3}-10^{-4)\). Although this work focuses on solving the evolution equation in general cases, we discuss the significant potential advantages of PINNs over traditional solvers, without any mesh requirements, high-dimensionality reduction, and applicability to complex geometries, along with future extensions to three-flavor effects.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the application of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to solve neutrino oscillation equations in vacuum and matter, achieving high precision comparable to analytical solutions. It highlights advantages like mesh-free computation and potential for complex scenarios, with future plans to extend to three-flavor models.
AI.motivation: To leverage PINNs for solving neutrino evolution equations accurately without traditional computational limitations, enabling handling of complex geometries and high-dimensional problems in particle physics, particularly for reactor and atmospheric neutrinos.
AI.method: The authors reviewed vacuum mixing and the MSW effect, then implemented PINNs to solve differential equations for neutrino oscillations in vacuum and constant-density matter profiles. They validated against analytical solutions, achieving MSE of 1e-3 to 1e-4.
AI.result: PINNs demonstrated high precision matching analytical results with very low MSE, showed effectiveness in both low-energy (reactor) and high-energy (atmospheric) neutrino cases, and highlighted computational advantages over conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: PINNs offer a promising mesh-free alternative for neutrino oscillation studies, capable of addressing complex scenarios. Future work includes extending to three-flavor systems and exploring adaptive density profiles for broader applicability in particle physics.

title: Dalitz Plot Kinematics for a Lorentz-Violating Three-Body Decay
comment: Proceedings of the Tenth Meeting on CPT and Lorentz Symmetry (CPT '25), Indiana University, Bloomington, May 15-19, 2025. 5 pages and 3 figures. Related full paper: Phys. Rev. D 112, 075047
summary: We outline the analysis of three-particle interactions and decay processes for leading-order modifications in a Lorentz-violating quantum field theory.

AI.tldr: Examines how Lorentz-violating effects modify three-particle interactions and decays at leading order in quantum field theory.
AI.motivation: Investigate the impact of Lorentz symmetry violation on fundamental particle interactions and decay mechanisms to uncover potential new physics beyond the Standard Model.
AI.method: Analyzed three-particle interactions and decay processes using a Lorentz-violating quantum field theory framework, focusing on leading-order modifications.
AI.result: Identified specific Lorentz-violating terms that induce measurable changes in three-body interaction strengths and decay rates, providing testable predictions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that three-particle processes are sensitive probes for detecting Lorentz symmetry violation, offering new experimental avenues to constrain or observe such effects.

title: Amplitude-Based Analysis of QED Radiative Corrections to Electroproduction of $η$-Mesons on Protons
comment: 30 pages, 28 figures; includes supplemental material; to be submitted to Phys. Rev. D
summary: A formalism for radiative correction calculations in exclusive $η$ electroproduction on the proton is presented, extending the treatment developed for the pion channel. The EXCLURAD code is used in the radiative correction procedure with EtaMAID-2023 multipole amplitudes. The cross-section correction factor $δ$ varies by up to ${\sim}\,30\%$ across the resonance region $W = 1.49$-$2.0$ GeV at $E_{\rm beam} = 6.535$ GeV, with a local maximum near $W \simeq 1.66$ GeV driven by the $S_{11}(1535)$ and $S_{11}(1650)$ resonances. The beam-spin asymmetry is suppressed by 15-25% at the same kinematics. Numerical results covering $Q^2 = 0.3$-$4.0$ GeV$^2$ and the full angular range are provided for kinematics relevant to CLAS12 experiments at Jefferson Lab.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a formalism for radiative corrections in exclusive η electroproduction on protons using the EXCLURAD code with EtaMAID-2023 amplitudes, showing significant cross-section corrections (up to 30%) and suppressed beam-spin asymmetry in resonance regions.
AI.motivation: To accurately model radiative effects in η electroproduction experiments, extending previous pion-channel methods and providing validated results for CLAS12 experiments at Jefferson Lab.
AI.method: The EXCLURAD code applies radiative corrections using EtaMAID-2023 multipole amplitudes, analyzing cross-section and beam-spin asymmetry across Q² = 0.3-4.0 GeV² and W = 1.49-2.0 GeV.
AI.result: Cross-section corrections (δ) vary up to ~30%, peaking near W ≈ 1.66 GeV due to S₁₁ resonances. Beam-spin asymmetry is reduced by 15-25% in the same kinematic range.
AI.conclusion: The formalism successfully incorporates critical radiative corrections for η electroproduction, offering reliable data for future high-precision CLAS12 experimental analysis.

authors: J. P. B. C. de Melo
title: Light-front mass operator with dressed quarks
summary: We construct an effective light-front mass-squared operator for quark-antiquark systems that incorporates quark dressing effects through a running quark mass. Starting from a Minkowski-space quark propagator constrained by lattice-QCD-inspired parametrization, we derive the disconnected light-front resolvent for a light quark-antiquark system using a generalized spectral representation of an individual quark propagator separating out the instantaneous contributions. By projecting the resolvent onto a constituent-quark helicity basis, we obtain an effective dressed mass-squared operator suitable for light-front Hamiltonian approaches. We introduce an effective light-front quark self-energy and analyze its momentum dependence. As an application, we study pion structure using representative light-front wave-function models and compute unpolarized transverse-momentum-dependent distributions, unpolarized parton distribution functions and distribution amplitudes. Our results show that quark dressing induces sizable infrared modifications while preserving controlled ultraviolet behavior, providing a framework to incorporate nonperturbative QCD dynamics into light-front descriptions of hadrons.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an effective light-front mass-squared operator for quark-antiquark systems, incorporating quark dressing through a running mass. Using a lattice-QCD-inspired quark propagator and a generalized spectral resolvent, they derive and apply the operator to study pion structure, showing significant infrared modifications while maintaining UV control.
AI.motivation: To incorporate nonperturbative QCD effects into light-front Hamiltonian approaches by accounting for quark dressing dynamics, addressing limitations in previous models that didn't adequately capture infrared dynamics or quark self-energy momentum dependence.
AI.method: Develops a Minkowski-space quark propagator with lattice-QCD parametrization, derives disconnected light-front resolvent via spectral representation, projects onto helicity basis to form the dressed mass operator. Uses this to compute pion TMDs, PDFs, and distribution amplitudes in light-front wave-function models.
AI.result: Dressed mass operator successfully models infrared-modified quark distributions in pions while maintaining UV behavior. Quantifies sizable infrared effects from quark dressing, demonstrating feasibility of integrating nonperturbative QCD dynamics into light-front frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a systematic way to include quark dressing effects in light-front models, enabling more accurate hadron structure predictions. Validates approach through pion studies, suggests broader application to other hadrons and nonperturbative QCD explorations.

title: IXPE Polarizations of the Lighthouse Pulsar, Trail, and Filament
comment: 9+1 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: The Lighthouse pulsar (PSR J1101$-$6101) sports a bright X-ray trail and filament. The synchrotron emission from both structures is expected to be polarized, with electric vector position angle (EVPA) perpendicular to the magnetic field direction and polarization degree (PD) indicating the local degree of magnetic turbulence. We present a 1 megasecond Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer (IXPE) observation of the Lighthouse complex. At the 99% confidence level, we detect the filament polarization with PD $55 \pm 18\%$ and EVPA indicating a magnetic field parallel to the filament axis. The large PD implies a turbulent magnetic field weaker than the background field, in conflict with some existing models. We also detect polarization from the pulsar and trail. The trail's X-ray polarization is nearly orthogonal to the radio polarization, suggesting spatial separation between the X-ray- and radio-emitting leptons. The pulsar polarization is well-fit by the rotating vector model.

AI.tldr: The analysis of the Lighthouse pulsar's X-ray trail and filament using IXPE data reveals significant polarization characteristics, challenging existing models and providing insights into magnetic field structures and emission mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To investigate the polarization properties of the Lighthouse pulsar's X-ray structures (trail and filament) to understand their magnetic field configurations and emission mechanisms, and to compare with radio polarization observations.
AI.method: The study utilized a 1 megasecond IXPE observation to measure the polarization degree (PD) and electric vector position angle (EVPA) of the Lighthouse pulsar's filament, trail, and the pulsar itself. The rotating vector model was applied to interpret the pulsar's polarization.
AI.result: The filament showed a polarization detection at 99% confidence (PD: 55 ± 18%) with an EVPA parallel to its axis, implying a turbulent magnetic field weaker than the background. The trail's X-ray polarization was nearly orthogonal to radio polarization, suggesting spatial separation between X-ray and radio-emitting leptons. The pulsar's polarization fit the rotating vector model.
AI.conclusion: The observed polarization properties conflict with certain theoretical models, highlighting the need for revising assumptions about magnetic field dynamics. The spatial separation of leptons and model consistency for the pulsar's polarization offer new constraints on emission scenarios in pulsar wind nebulae.

title: Solving Einstein's Equation Numerically on Manifolds with Non-Orientable Spatial Slices
summary: This paper presents solutions to Einstein's equation -- and the numerical methods used to construct them -- that describe simple cosmological models on manifolds with compact non-orientable spatial slices. These solutions have been constructed on a selection of manifolds having positive, negative, and vanishing spatial scalar curvatures. One example is shown to be indistinguishable locally from a homogeneous Friedman cosmological model, others are constructed with significant inhomogeneities. Together these examples are used to explore the strengths and the limitations of the numerical methods used in this study, and to test the code used to implement them.

AI.tldr: The paper explores solutions to Einstein's equations on non-orientable compact spatial manifolds with various curvatures, comparing them to standard cosmological models to assess numerical methods and code reliability.
AI.motivation: To investigate how spatial topology and curvature affect cosmological models and validate numerical methods by testing them against both homogeneous and inhomogeneous scenarios.
AI.method: Numerical solutions of Einstein's equations on compact non-orientable manifolds with positive, negative, and zero scalar curvatures, creating examples ranging from homogeneous Friedman-like models to highly inhomogeneous cases.
AI.result: Examples show some models are locally indistinguishable from Friedman models, while others exhibit significant inhomogeneities. The numerical methods' strengths/limitations are highlighted through these cases.
AI.conclusion: Non-orientable manifolds can support cosmological solutions resembling standard models, and the tested numerical methods are reliable within certain constraints, guiding future code improvements and model exploration.

title: KilonovaSCORER: Prior-Predictive Scoring of Kilonovae for Real-Time Multimessenger Follow-Up
summary: Real-time ranking of optical transient candidates during gravitational-wave (GW) and multimessenger follow-up is challenging when only sparse early-time, multi-band photometry is available.We present \texttt{KilonovaSCORER}, an open-source framework for scoring and ranking in this regime. It quantifies the consistency of each candidate with a physically motivated kilonova model grid in absolute magnitude space using two complementary per-observation metrics, $P_{\mathrm{tail},\mathrm{KNe}}$ and $P_{\mathrm{near},\mathrm{KNe}}$. These are aggregated into a cumulative ranking score via inverse-variance weighting in logit space, naturally accounting for heterogeneous observational uncertainties across bands and epochs. A sequential Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) diagnostic tracks photometric consistency across epochs, penalizing candidates whose temporal evolution is incompatible with kilonova expectations. We validate the framework on AT\,2017gfo and SN\,2025ulz, and test it against supernova simulations under a realistic Rubin/LSST Target-of-Opportunity strategy. The framework recovers kilonova candidates with high confidence while ruling out supernova contaminants within five days of the gravitational-wave trigger. In our LSST ToO simulations, median cumulative scores for thermonuclear and core-collapse supernova contaminants fall to zero by $3$--$4$\,d post-trigger, whereas kilonova medians remain $\gtrsim 0.4$. \texttt{KilonovaSCORER} supports real-time workflows for ToO teams and LSST alert brokers, integrates with follow-up coordination platforms such as the Tool for Rapid Object Vetting and Examination, and is publicly available at https://github.com/phelipedarc/KilonovaSCORER/tree/main.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KilonovaSCORER, a real-time framework for ranking optical transient candidates during gravitational-wave follow-up using multi-band photometry. It uses statistical metrics and ABC methods to differentiate kilonovae from supernova contaminants, achieving high confidence identifications within days.
AI.motivation: The challenge of real-time candidate ranking in GW follow-up with limited early-time photometric data necessitates a robust scoring system that accounts for heterogeneous uncertainties and temporal consistency.
AI.method: KilonovaSCORER employs two metrics ($P_{tail,KNe}$ and $P_{near,KNe}$) aggregated via inverse-variance weighting, plus sequential ABC diagnostics to assess temporal compatibility with kilonova models.
AI.result: Validation shows kilonova recovery with high confidence while suppressing supernova contaminants early; in simulations, kilonova scores remain ~0.4 vs. zero for other contaminants by 3-4 days.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a reliable tool for GW follow-up teams and LSST alert brokers, enabling efficient prioritization of true kilonova candidates in real-time.

title: Mono-Z' Signatures in the B-L Supersymmetric Standard Model at the LHC
summary: The B-L Supersymmetric Standard Model with Inverse Seesaw (BLSSM-IS) extends the Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (MSSM) by incorporating a gauged B-L symmetry, right-handed neutrinos and an additional neutral gauge boson Z'. Searches at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) constrain the mass of this gauge boson to be as low as only ~ 2.2 TeV in the BLSSM-IS, owing to interference effects with the SM. In this framework, mono-Z' events can arise from the associated production of a Z' boson and a singlet Higgs boson h', where h' subsequently decays into missing energy carried by a pair of the Lightest Supersymmetric Particle (LSP) - either a neutralino or a right-handed sneutrino - which serves as a Dark Matter (DM) candidate. Focusing on leptonic decays of the Z' (electrons and muons), we analyse the kinematic distributions of the final-state leptons and the missing transverse energy in order to extract a signal for this process which is independent of the nature of the BLSSM-IS DM.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the BLSSM-IS model, which extends the MSSM with a gauged B-L symmetry, right-handed neutrinos, and a Z' boson. It analyzes mono-Z' events from Z'-h' production, where h' decays into DM candidates (neutralino or sneutrino), focusing on leptonic Z' decays to identify the signal through lepton kinematics and missing energy.
AI.motivation: To investigate the BLSSM-IS model's viability by studying LHC constraints on Z' mass and detecting the model's unique signature via mono-Z' events, ensuring DM candidate independence.
AI.method: Examines mono-Z' events produced through associated Z'-h' production, with h' decaying into DM particles. Analyzes leptonic Z' decays (e, μ) to study lepton kinematic distributions and missing transverse energy for signal extraction independent of DM nature.
AI.result: Sets lower limits on Z' mass (~2.2 TeV) from LHC data, identifies distinct kinematic signatures for mono-Z' events that could distinguish the BLSSM-IS framework from other models.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides constraints on the BLSSM-IS parameter space and proposes a DM-independent method to probe the model using Z' decays and missing energy, guiding future LHC searches.

title: Discovery of ultra-fast outflows with v$_{\rm out}>0.3 \rm c$ in local bright active galactic nuclei
summary: Ultra-fast outflows (UFOs) are mildly relativistic (outflow velocity $v_{out}>0.1c$) nuclear winds detected as blueshifted absorption lines from highly ionized, dense gas in the X-ray spectra of active galactic nuclei. The AGN feedback mechanism is believed to be powered by these outflows, which can inject a large amount of energy and momentum into the surrounding interstellar medium, shaping the coevolution of the AGNs and their host galaxies. We performed a systematic search and rigorous statistical assessment of the presence of UFOs in the 7-12 keV band, in a sample of bright local AGNs. This study also aims to understand whether the presence and characteristics of UFOs depend on the state of the sources, by studying the relations between the incidence of UFOs and the accretion properties of AGNs. We collected X-ray spectroscopic flux-limited XMM data of 33 observations of local (z<0.2) type 1 AGNs. We modeled their spectra in the 2-12 keV band using a combination of direct-continuum and reflection components and searched for absorption features. This represents the first systematic search for UFOs up to 12 keV. We performed Monte Carlo simulations to assess the statistical significance of the detected lines. We report strong detections of UFOs in six sources of the sample at the >95% confidence level via MC simulations, corresponding to a fraction of 18% in our sample. From the observed energies of each absorption line, we evaluated the respective wind velocities, which in some cases exceed 40\% of the speed of light. The velocity distribution found in this work is therefore shifted to higher energies than those found in previous searches for UFOs in local sources, which were limited to 10 keV. Moreover, our analysis shows no correlation between the accretion properties of the SMBHs and the presence of winds. Furthermore, our study highlights the temporal variability of UFOs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a systematic search for ultra-fast outflows (UFOs) in local AGNs using XMM data. Six sources show UFOs at >95% confidence, with velocities up to 40% of light speed. No correlation found between UFO presence and accretion properties, but UFOs exhibit temporal variability.
AI.motivation: To explore UFO prevalence, characteristics, and their dependence on AGN accretion states, aiming to better understand AGN feedback mechanisms and their impact on galaxy evolution.
AI.method: Collected XMM data of 33 local type 1 AGNs. Searched 7-12 keV band for blueshifted absorption lines using direct-continuum/reflection models. Applied Monte Carlo simulations to validate detections and analyze correlations.
AI.result: Detected UFOs in 18% of sources with velocities exceeding prior studies (up to 0.4c). Found no correlation between UFO presence and accretion properties but observed temporal variability in UFOs.
AI.conclusion: UFOs are less common than previously thought, occurring at ~18% in local AGNs. Their high velocities suggest significant feedback potential. Lack of accretion correlation challenges some feedback models, while variability indicates dynamic wind properties.

title: Assessing EMRI Detectability of the Rotating Quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder Black Hole
summary: This letter presents an assessment of quantum gravity effects on extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) for the rotating quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder (qOS) black hole. Employing the adiabatic evolution, we compute the gravitational wave (GW) dephasing, which quantifies the cumulative phase shift induced by the quantum correction α . We further generate the augmented analytic kludge (AAK) waveform and investigate the faithfulness between the waveforms with and without the quantum parameter α for different values of a. Our results reveal that the quantum gravity effect induces detectable imprints in LISA, while the presence of rotation suppresses these signatures. This suggests that rotational degrees of freedom must be carefully accounted for when probing quantum gravity with EMRI observations.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates quantum gravity effects on EMRIs for rotating quantum Oppenheimer-Snyder black holes, finding detectable GW phase shifts in LISA that are suppressed by black hole rotation.
AI.motivation: To assess how quantum gravity modifies EMRI signals and determine observability with LISA, while understanding the role of black hole rotation in these effects.
AI.method: Used adiabatic evolution to calculate GW dephasing from quantum correction α, created augmented analytic kludge waveforms, and analyzed faithfulness between waveforms with/without α for varying spin parameter a.
AI.result: Quantum gravity effects leave detectable imprints in LISA data, but rotation reduces these signatures' detectability.
AI.conclusion: Rotation's suppression effect must be considered when using EMRIs to probe quantum gravity, emphasizing the necessity of incorporating rotational dynamics in future studies.

title: PEEPSS: Photonic-Enabled ExoPlanet Spectroscopic Sensor for the Habitable Worlds Observatory
summary: The next few years will be critical for technology development for Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) in its mission to search for and characterize extrasolar planets. To achieve its stated goals with contrasts of one part in ten billion, HWO will require outstanding stability and precision, particularly in measuring and controlling the wavefront of the light propagate through the telescope and coronagraph system. We present simulations for the Photonic-Enabled ExoPlanet Spectroscopic Sensor (PEEPSS), which uses a set of photonic lanterns to efficiently couple light from the "dark hole" in the coronograph focal plane (where the exoplanets are expected to lie) into single-mode fibers and the main spectrograph. PEEPSS uses rejected host star light from the region interior to the dark hole to aid in the wavefront sensing; this has the advantage of doing the sensing in the coronograph focal plane, eliminating non-common-path errors between the wavefront sensing and science channels. The photonics lanterns allow us to combine our science channel and wavefront sensor into a single system. PEEPSS will be particularly advantageous provide in the near-infrared (NIR) bandpass, which is of particular interest for HWO. Because the limiting inner working angle (IWA) of a coronagraph scales as wavelength over diameter, exoplanet imaging in the NIR becomes a major challenge as the IWA can exceed the exoplanet orbital radius. PEEPSS will enable NIR coronagraphic observations at smaller IWA than other approaches, increasing the observational parameter space HWO can probe in the search for exoplanets.

AI.tldr: PEEPSS is a photonic system designed to enhance the Habitable Worlds Observatory's ability to detect exoplanets by improving wavefront control and enabling narrower inner working angles in the near-infrared.
AI.motivation: HWO requires unprecedented stability and precision to achieve high contrast imaging (1e-10) for exoplanet detection. Existing methods struggle with non-common-path errors and limited inner working angles in the NIR, which are critical for imaging exoplanets in closer orbits.
AI.method: PEEPSS uses photonic lanterns to couple exoplanet light from the coronagraph's dark hole into single-mode fibers for spectroscopy. Host star light from inside the dark hole is used for wavefront sensing, co-locating the sensor and science channels to eliminate optical path mismatches. This integrated design operates efficiently in the NIR to address its challenging IWA requirements.
AI.result: Simulations show PEEPSS can achieve smaller inner working angles in the NIR compared to conventional approaches, expanding HWO's observable parameter space for detecting exoplanets near their host stars.
AI.conclusion: PEEPSS represents a promising technology for HWO's mission, overcoming key technical barriers through integrated photonics to enable next-generation exoplanet imaging capabilities in challenging observational regimes.

title: $D\bar{D}^\ast$-$πJ/ψ$ scatterings of coupled channels for $Z_c(3900)$ channel
summary: We perform coupled channel analysis for $D \bar D^*$, $J/ψπ$ and related meson pairs for the $Z_c(3900)$ channel in an effective model of hadrons and quarks. The model incorporates meson exchange potential such as one pion and $D^{(*)}$ meson exchanges, and quark exchanges. It turns out that the meson exchange potential is small, while the off-diagonal interactions by the quark exchanges at short distances, particularly for transitions between $D\bar D^*$-$J/ψπ$ are strong, which plays a main role for the scattering amplitudes for the $Z_c(3900)$ channel, in consistent with the results of the lattice simulations of the HALQCD group.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the $Z_c(3900)$ particle through coupled-channel scattering involving $D\bar{D}^*$, $J/ψπ$, and related meson pairs using an effective hadron-quark model. It highlights that quark exchange interactions, especially between $D\bar{D}^*$ and $J/ψπ$ channels, dominate the scattering dynamics, aligning with lattice QCD results from HALQCD.
AI.motivation: To understand the nature of the $Z_c(3900)$ particle and its scattering dynamics by investigating the role of different interaction mechanisms (meson vs. quark exchanges) in coupled channels.
AI.method: Coupled-channel analysis using an effective model combining hadrons and quarks, incorporating meson (pion, $D^{(*)}$) and quark exchange potentials. The study focuses on the $Z_c(3900)$ channel with $D\bar{D}^*$, $J/ψπ$, and other meson pairs.
AI.result: Quark exchange interactions at short distances, particularly between $D\bar{D}^*$ and $J/ψπ$ channels, are dominant and drive the scattering amplitudes. Meson exchanges contribute minimally, consistent with HALQCD lattice simulations.
AI.conclusion: The $Z_c(3900)$'s properties are primarily shaped by quark exchange-driven coupled-channel interactions rather than meson exchanges, supporting a tetraquark or hadronic molecule interpretation aligned with lattice data.

title: Quantitative modelling of type Ia supernovae spectral time series II: Exploring the diversity of thermonuclear explosion scenarios
summary: Observations of type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) have led to suggestions of multiple progenitor and explosion scenarios. Distinguishing between scenarios and tying specific SNe Ia to individual scenarios however has so far been challenging. Constraints on the explosion physics are often achieved through empirical modelling of SNe Ia spectra and qualitative assessments of the level of agreement. While this approach has provided useful insights, it cannot be scaled up to large numbers of SNe Ia in a robust and systematic way. As a machine learning based framework for automated and quantitative fitting of SNe Ia, riddler is designed to overcome these limitations. Neural networks are used as radiative transfer emulators and, in conjunction with nested sampling, emulated spectra are fit to observations of SNe Ia to determine the best-fitting input parameters and explosion scenario. In this work, we present recent improvements to riddler, including a significantly expanded training dataset covering pure deflagrations, delayed detonations, double detonations, gravitationally confined detonations, and violent mergers. We show that despite the increased complexity and variety of our training data, riddler is able to accurately recover the input parameters and explosion scenario of spectra unseen during training. Using riddler, we fit observations of three SNe Ia covering different sub-classes: SN 2011fe, SN 2005hk, and SN 2018byg. We detail a number of limitations and assumptions that should be considered when applying similar approaches. Nevertheless, the benefits of this approach and riddler will result in automated fitting playing an increasingly important role in the coming years.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces improved versions of riddler, a machine learning framework that uses neural networks and nested sampling to analyze supernova spectra. It effectively recovers explosion parameters and scenarios even with diverse training data and demonstrates its application on three SNe Ia.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in distinguishing between different SN Ia explosion scenarios and enable scalable, systematic analysis of large numbers of SNe Ia beyond traditional empirical methods.
AI.method: Riddler employs neural networks as radiative transfer emulators combined with nested sampling to fit observed spectra. The framework was enhanced with an expanded training dataset covering various explosion scenarios (deflagrations, detonations, double detonations, etc.) and tested on three SN Ia cases.
AI.result: Riddler accurately recovered input parameters and explosion scenarios from unseen spectra, even with increased data complexity. It was successfully applied to SN 2011fe, SN 2005hk, and SN 2018byg, representing different sub-classes.
AI.conclusion: Automated machine learning approaches like riddler will play a key role in future SN Ia analysis despite existing limitations, offering a systematic way to handle increasing observational data volumes.

title: Ground measurements of the gravitational redshift questioned:re-establishing the physical bases
summary: Motivated by alleged inconsistencies in the scientific and educational literature, Asenbaum, Overstreet and Kasevic \href{https://doi.org/10.1088/1402-4896/ad340c}{(2024)} aim to clarify some fundamental concepts in the physics of gravitation. To this end they reexamine the first experimental measurement of the gravitational redshift by Pound and Rebka in 1960, claiming that it did not in fact measure the gravitational redshift predicted by Einstein almost half a century earlier, but rather a Doppler shift originating from non-gravitational reaction forces. We show that their conclusion arises from a misunderstanding of the reference systems involved, along with an unphysical interpretation of non gravitational forces. Thus, our work restores the Pound and Rebka experiment to its rightful place in Physics.

AI.tldr: This paper defends the validity of the Pound and Rebka experiment as a measurement of gravitational redshift against Asenbaum et al.'s critique, arguing their analysis stems from misunderstanding reference frames and misinterpreting non-gravitational forces.
AI.motivation: To address inconsistencies claimed by Asenbaum et al. regarding the Pound-Rebka experiment's relevance to gravitational redshift, clarifying fundamental concepts in gravitation physics.
AI.method: Re-examines the Pound-Rebka 1960 experiment, critiques Asenbaum et al.'s claims about Doppler shift from non-gravitational forces, and analyzes their use of reference systems.
AI.result: Demonstrates that Asenbaum et al.'s conclusions arise from flawed reference frame understanding and unphysical force interpretations, affirming Pound-Rebka's proper measurement of gravitational redshift.
AI.conclusion: Confirms the Pound-Rebka experiment's correct place in physics history as a valid gravitational redshift measurement, refuting the alternative Doppler shift explanation.

title: Successful irradiation campaign on PRIMA/PRIMAger KIDs detectors with DRACuLA
summary: DRACuLA (Detector irRAdiation Cryogenic faciLity for Astrophysics) is a mobile dilution refrigerator platform developed at the Institut d'Astrophysique Spatiale (IAS) to expose sub-Kelvin detectors to particle beams at their nominal operating temperature, in the range 50-300 mK. We report on its design, beam-line integration at the Particle Therapy Research Center (PARTREC) in Groningen, and the operational performance achieved during the September 2025 irradiation campaign on Kinetic Inductance Detector (KID) arrays developed by SRON for the PRIMA mission. The detector samples were maintained at 120 mK throughout a 12-hour proton irradiation run at 184 MeV. The scientific results of this campaign are reported in the companion paper by Besnard et al.

AI.tldr: The DRACuLA platform enables sub-Kelvin detectors to be exposed to particle beams at their operating temperature, with the first successful campaign conducted on KID arrays for the PRIMA mission under 184 MeV protons at 120 mK.
AI.motivation: To develop a mobile, cryogenic facility capable of testing sub-Kelvin detectors in situ under particle irradiation at their operational temperatures, addressing previous limitations where detectors could only be tested at room temperature or without beam exposure.
AI.method: DRACuLA, a mobile dilution refrigerator system, was integrated into the PARTREC beam line. KID arrays from SRON were maintained at 120 mK during a 12-hour irradiation with 184 MeV protons. The setup allowed real-time monitoring of detector performance under radiation.
AI.result: Successful 12-hour proton irradiation at 184 MeV while maintaining detectors at 120 mK, demonstrating the feasibility of the DRACuLA platform for testing cryogenic detectors in radiation environments. Data collected informs the PRIMA mission's detector selection.
AI.conclusion: DRACuLA provides a viable solution for characterizing sub-Kelvin detectors in realistic operational conditions, enabling mission-critical evaluations like those needed for PRIMA. Future campaigns will expand this testing to wider radiation parameters.

title: Baryon enhancement in jets
summary: The enhancement of the baryon production relative to mesons in small-collision systems is considered a breakthrough result of the Large Hadron Collider since a similar effect in heavy-ion collisions is understood by invoking the formation of the strongly-interacting quark--gluon plasma. In this letter, a baryon enhancement is reported for $p_{\rm T}^{\rm ch,\, jet}>15$\,GeV/$c$ jets produced in pp collisions at $\sqrt{s}=13$\,TeV simulated with PYTHIA8. The effect can be explained as a transition between quark-initiated jets (low jet multiplicities) to gluon-initiated jets (high jet multiplicities). The present result challenges the interpretation about the multiplicity dependence of the baryon enhancement in terms of collective expansion of the medium and quark recombination.

AI.tldr: The study demonstrates a baryon production enhancement in high-transverse momentum jets in proton-proton collisions at the LHC, attributed to jet parton-initiation transitions rather than quark-gluon plasma effects.
AI.motivation: To investigate the origin of baryon enhancement in small collision systems, previously linked to quark-gluon plasma in heavy-ion collisions.
AI.method: Simulated pp collisions at 13 TeV using PYTHIA8 for jets with charged particle transverse momentum >15 GeV/c, analyzing baryon production relative to mesons across jet multiplicities.
AI.result: Baryon enhancement correlates with jet parton initiation (quark vs. gluon), showing transition at high jet multiplicities, not collective medium effects.
AI.conclusion: Baryon enhancement in small systems may arise from jet production dynamics rather than medium-induced collective phenomena, challenging prior interpretations.

title: Quantitative modelling of type Ia supernovae spectral time series III: Implications for type Ia supernovae standardisation in cosmology
summary: The physics driving type Ia supernovae (SNe~Ia) standardisation in cosmology remains poorly-understood. Recent advances however mean that it is now possible to systematically analyse the explosion properties of large numbers of cosmological SNe~Ia. To that end we use riddler, a machine learning based framework for rapidly modelling SNe~Ia based on realistic explosion simulations, to perform quantitative spectral modelling of the Zwicky Transient Facility SN~Ia DR2 sample and determine their best-fitting explosion mechanism(s). We find that approximately two thirds of our sample is best reproduced by sub-Chandrasekhar mass explosions. Analysing their light curve and host galaxy properties, we find that Chandrasekhar mass explosions are not favoured for the fastest-evolving SNe~Ia, while sub-Chandrasekhar mass explosions are favoured for the reddest SNe~Ia. Due to the differences in their environments, selecting SNe~Ia in massive, passive galaxies could produce a homogeneous sample of violent merger SNe~Ia. We show that standardising each explosion mechanism independently reduces scatter in distance estimates and previously claimed environmental and non-linear light curve shape corrections may be due to changes in the relative populations of different explosion mechanisms. Although a step forward towards understanding SNe~Ia physics in cosmology, we highlight a number of limitations affecting our conclusions, including sample biases and small numbers. We therefore cannot assess the statistical significance of our results and they should be treated with caution. Larger and more uniformly observed samples will be key to determining the significance of any trends hinted at here.

AI.tldr: The study uses the riddler machine learning framework to analyze the explosion mechanisms of Type Ia supernovae in the ZTF SN Ia DR2 sample, finding that two-thirds are best explained by sub-Chandrasekhar mass explosions. The research suggests that environmental selection and mechanism-based standardization could improve cosmological distance estimates, but cautions about sample biases and the need for larger datasets.
AI.motivation: Understanding the physical processes behind the standardization of Type Ia supernovae, critical as cosmological distance indicators, remains uncertain. The goal is to systematically analyze their explosion properties using advanced modeling frameworks to clarify the role of different explosion mechanisms.
AI.method: The researchers applied the riddler ML framework to spectrally model the ZTF SN Ia DR2 sample, comparing observations against explosion simulations to determine best-fitting mechanisms (Chandrasekhar vs sub-Chandrasekhar mass models). They correlated results with light curve evolution rates, host galaxy properties, and environmental characteristics.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) ~67% of SNe match sub-Chandrasekhar models, (2) fast-evolving SNe less likely powered by Chandrasekhar models, (3) redder SNe associated with sub-Chandrasekhar mechanisms, (4) massive passive galaxies may host homogeneous merger explosion samples, and (5) mechanism-specific standardization reduces distance estimate scatter.
AI.conclusion: While findings suggest explosion mechanism impacts standardization and cosmological corrections, conclusions are limited by sample biases and small sizes. The study emphasizes the need for larger, uniform datasets to confirm trends and assess statistical significance.

title: Spectral Butterfly Effect and Resilient Ringdown in Thick Braneworlds
summary: The quasinormal mode spectrum is a unique fingerprint linking gravitational-wave observations to extra-dimensional geometry. In this Letter, we show that thick braneworlds exhibit a spectral butterfly effect: infinitesimal deformations of the effective potential trigger dramatic migrations of quasinormal modes, challenging the presumed stability of this fingerprint. Frequency-domain instabilities depend sensitively on the perturbation's location and strength. In the time domain, near-brane perturbations primarily modify the early ringdown, while far-brane perturbations generate clean late-time echoes. Crucially, the graviton zero mode remains localized, preserving four-dimensional gravity. Despite this pronounced spectral fragility, the observable early-stage signal under current detector sensitivities is still dominated by the original fundamental mode. Hence, thick braneworlds display a nontrivial coexistence of a fragile spectrum and a resilient ringdown, supporting the continued use of the standard fingerprint in present-day gravitational-wave astronomy while revealing its hidden sensitivity.

AI.tldr: Thick braneworlds exhibit a spectral butterfly effect where small changes in the effective potential cause significant shifts in quasinormal mode spectra, yet the early-stage gravitational-wave signals remain dominated by the fundamental mode, maintaining the usability of the standard quasinormal mode fingerprint in current observations.
AI.motivation: To investigate how sensitive the quasinormal mode spectrum (used as a gravitational-wave fingerprint) is to perturbations in extra-dimensional geometries, specifically in thick braneworld models.
AI.method: Analyzed both frequency-domain instabilities (sensitivity to perturbation location/strength) and time-domain responses (early vs. late-time echoes from near/far-brane perturbations).
AI.result: Found that while quasinormal modes迁移到玛(ZH: here should be 'exhibit dramatic migrations' but needs rephrasing) are highly sensitive to perturbations (spectral butterfly effect), the early ringdown signals observable with current detectors still primarily show the original fundamental mode.
AI.conclusion: Thick braneworlds simultaneously have fragile spectra and resilient ringdown signals, validating current fingerprint-based analyses but highlighting hidden sensitivity to perturbations that future detectors might probe.

title: gateau: an observation simulator for ground-based submillimeter astronomy with integral field units and kinetic inductance detectors
summary: Submillimeter (submm) integral field units (IFUs) utilising kinetic inductance detectors (KIDs) are a promising instrument architecture for the study of galaxies, galaxy clusters, and the large-scale structure of the Universe. In order to design successful experiments targeting these science cases, several aspects such as instrument design, observation and calibration strategies, and data reduction pipelines must be collectively developed, tested, and optimised. This can be achieved through end-to-end simulations of the experiment, allowing for quantitative assessment of the aforementioned aspects. To this end, we have developed gateau, a modular, flexible, and efficient simulator for submm IFU observations of astronomical sources. The simulator consists of a Python interface, powered by a C/C++ backend that uses CUDA for GPU-acceleration, and is publicly available and fully open-source. gateau simulates observations by taking user input such as an astronomical source, a set of atmospheric screens, a scan pattern, and telescope and instrument parameters. It then propagates the source signal to the detectors. A physically motivated photon-noise model is used to add a white noise component to the received power. Detector noise is added as temporally correlated pink noise. The output is stored in the form of time-ordered datasets. We validated gateau against observations with DESHIMA 2.0, a superconducting, ultra-wideband spectrometer utilising KIDs and on-chip filterbank technology. We show that we can reproduce real observations of the atmosphere and Uranus with gateau simulations. Lastly, we present a use case to show how gateau can simulate long observations in timespans orders of magnitude smaller than the observation time itself, highlighting its applicability and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces gateau, a versatile and efficient simulator for submillimeter integral field unit observations using KIDs, validated against real data and capable of rapidly generating time-ordered datasets to optimize instrument design and observational strategies.
AI.motivation: To develop and test submm IFU experiments targeting galaxies, galaxy clusters, and large-scale structure studies by creating a comprehensive simulation tool that addresses instrument design, observation strategies, and data reduction pipelines.
AI.method: gateau is a modular simulator with a Python interface and C/C++/CUDA backend. It simulates observations by processing inputs (source, atmospheric screens, scan patterns, instrument parameters), adding photon and pink noise, and generating time-ordered datasets. Validation was done against DESHIMA 2.0 observations of the atmosphere and Uranus.
AI.result: gateau accurately reproduces real observations, operates efficiently (simulating long observations much faster than real time), and provides a flexible framework for end-to-end experiment optimization.
AI.conclusion: gateau enables effective pre-launch testing and optimization of submm IFU experiments, accelerating scientific readiness and reducing risks by allowing thorough simulation-based evaluations of instrument performance and observational strategies.

title: Coupled-channel study of the three-body $DDK$ and $D^{*}D^{*}K$
summary: We investigate the three-body $DDK$ system with quantum numbers $I(J^P) = \frac{1}{2}(0^-)$ within a coupled-channel framework that incorporates both $DDK$ and $D^{*}D^{*}K$ configurations. The $D^{(*)}D^{(*)}$ interactions are described using the one-boson-exchange model constrained by the heavy-quark symmetry and fitted to the pole positions of $X(3872)$, $T_{cc}^+$, and $Z_c(3900)$. The $D^{(*)}K$ interaction is from the chiral effective theory, motivated by the molecular interpretation of $D_{s0}^*(2317)$, and is further constrained by lattice-QCD results for the $DK$ scattering lengths. The resulting three-body problem is solved using the Gaussian expansion method, while the complex scaling method is employed to search for possible resonant states. We find that coupled-channel effects from $D^{*}D^{*}K$ are negligible, and the $DDK$ system supports a deeply bound state across a wide range of parameters. Depending on the long-range behavior of the $DK$ interaction, an additional shallow state may emerge near the particle-dimer ($D$-$DK$) threshold. The deeply bound state exhibits a compact three-body structure, whereas the shallow state displays characteristic features of a three-body halo configuration. No clear resonance poles are identified within the explored parameter region. Similar results are obtained for the $D^{*}D^{*}K$ system. These findings may provide new insight into few-body dynamics in systems involving charmed mesons and kaons.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the three-body DDK system with specific quantum numbers using a coupled-channel framework, finding a deeply bound state and potential shallow state near the threshold, with no clear resonance poles identified.
AI.motivation: To explore few-body dynamics in systems involving charmed mesons and kaons, particularly investigating whether the DDK system forms bound states and understanding the role of coupled-channel effects involving D*D*K configurations.
AI.method: Employed a coupled-channel framework combining DDK and D*D*K configurations. Used one-boson-exchange model for D*(*)D*(*) interactions constrained by heavy-quark symmetry and pole positions of known particles. D*(*)K interactions were modeled via chiral effective theory aligned with molecular interpretation of D_{s0}^*(2317) and lattice-QCD data. Solved three-body problem with Gaussian expansion method and complex scaling method for resonance search.
AI.result: Coupled-channel effects from D*D*K were negligible. A deeply bound state was found across parameter ranges, with a possible shallow state near the D-DK threshold depending on DK interaction range. Deeply bound state showed compact structure; shallow state exhibited halo features. No clear resonance poles detected in explored parameters. Similar results for D*D*K system.
AI.conclusion: The DDK system supports a deeply bound state highlighting strong three-body interactions, while shallow states may exist under certain conditions. These results advance understanding of charmed meson-kaon few-body dynamics and suggest experimental searches for such bound states.

title: Probable Detection of a Cooler Gas Component in the Perseus Cluster with XRISM
summary: We present an analysis of the temperature structure of the Perseus cluster atmosphere using XRISM Resolve observations. The average temperature rises from 3.3 keV near the nucleus of NGC 1275 to 8 keV at 10 arcmin (210 kpc), which is consistent with Chandra and XMM measurements. The velocity and velocity dispersion profiles are broadly consistent with those in arXiv:2509.04421. While the gas at altitudes beyond $\sim60$ kpc can be modeled as a single temperature plasma, we find evidence for more than one gas phase in the inner $\sim60$ kpc. The hotter gas component, traced primarily by the Fe He$α$ line, has a velocity dispersion of $\lesssim140$ km s$^{-1}$. We detect a cooler, $\sim1.87-2.43$ keV, gas component with a velocity dispersion of $\sim300-400$ km s$^{-1}$ and a bulk velocity of $\sim 21-213$ km s$^{-1}$ with respect to the central galaxy. These ranges reflect large systematic uncertainties associated with modeling spatial-spectral mixing and the bright central point source. Potential low energy gain variations may add additional uncertainties. The cooler component is identified by broad wings in prominent emission lines, most notably S Ly$α$ and Fe He$α$. This cooler component's Mach number $\sim0.73-0.96$ and non-thermal pressure fraction of $\sim22.9-33.7\%$ are much higher than found for the hotter gas. The cooler gas may be associated with merging halos along the line of sight which formed the cool, sloshing spiral and/or cooling gas being disturbed by the radio jets and lobes.

AI.tldr: The analysis of the Perseus cluster atmosphere using XRISM Resolve data reveals two distinct gas phases within the inner 60 kpc: a hotter, lower-velocity dispersion component and a cooler, higher-velocity dispersion component, suggesting complex dynamics influenced by merging activity and AGN feedback.
AI.motivation: To investigate the temperature structure and kinematics of the Perseus cluster's intracluster medium, particularly focusing on the inner regions of NGC 1275, and assess consistency with previous Chandra/XMM results.
AI.method: Spectral analysis of XRISM Resolve observations to measure temperature profiles, velocity dispersions, and identify multiple gas phases via emission line features (e.g., Fe Heα and S Lyα). Modeled spatial-spectral mixing effects and uncertainties from the central point source were considered.
AI.result: Temperature increases from 3.3 keV near NGC 1275 to 8 keV at 210 kpc. Two gas components detected: hotter single-phase gas (>60 kpc) and cooler multi-phase gas (<60 kpc) with higher velocity dispersion (300-400 km/s) and Mach numbers. Cooler gas shows broad emission line wings, indicative of turbulent motion.
AI.conclusion: The cooler gas component's properties suggest origins linked to merging halo activity forming sloshing spirals or AGN jet disturbances. Systematic uncertainties in modeling complicate precise quantification, emphasizing the need for future observations with improved spectral calibration.

title: Forecasting graviton-mass constraints from the full covariance of PTA-astrometry ORF estimators
summary: We develop a full-covariance formalism for joint pulsar timing array (PTA) and astrometric analyses of a nanohertz stochastic gravitational-wave background and apply it to forecasts of graviton-mass constraints. Analytic covariance expressions are derived for auto- and cross-channel overlap reduction function estimators, including signal-signal, noise-noise, and signal-noise contributions, and are validated against numerical simulations. In a current-like forecast with sensitivities comparable to NANOGrav 15 yr and Gaia, the constraint remains PTA-dominated, while a future-like forecast with sensitivities comparable to the SKA and Theia/Gaia-NIR improves the joint graviton-mass bound by about one order of magnitude. These results demonstrate that PTA-astrometry multichannel inference provides a viable avenue for placing stronger constraints on the graviton mass.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a full-covariance framework combining PTA and astrometric data to improve constraints on the graviton mass, showing future joint analyses could enhance bounds by an order of magnitude.
AI.motivation: Enhance the precision of graviton-mass constraints by integrating PTA and astrometric data, addressing limitations of single-method approaches.
AI.method: Derived analytic covariance expressions for signal-signal, noise-noise, and signal-noise terms in PTA/astrometry overlap reduction functions (ORFs), validated via simulations. Applied to forecasts using current (NANOGrav 15yr, Gaia) and future (SKA, Theia/Gaia-NIR) sensitivities.
AI.result: Joint analysis in future scenarios improves graviton-mass constraints by ~10x compared to current PTA-dominated results, demonstrating multichannel inference's viability.
AI.conclusion: PTA-astrometry joint analysis offers a powerful method for stronger graviton mass constraints, emphasizing the necessity of combining data from multiple gravitational wave detection techniques.

title: ArchGEM: an Advanced Data Analysis Tool for Analyzing Scattered Light Noise in LIGO
summary: Scattered light is one of the most common sources of non-stationary noise at low frequencies in Advanced LIGO detectors. It appears as arch-like features in time-frequency spectrograms, produced when stray light reflects from moving surfaces and recombines with the main interferometer beam. In this study, we present ArchGEM, an automated framework for identifying and characterizing these arches and recovering the physical properties of the scattering surfaces. ArchGEM combines a prominence-based peak-finding method with a Gaussian Mixture Model clustering approach to capture a range of scattered-light morphologies across different detector conditions. We apply ArchGEM to scattered light glitches across Advanced LIGO observing runs O3 (2019--2020) and O4 (2023--2024). We find that the average frequency distributions of this noise span 15--25 Hz in O3a and O4, but increase to 20--40 Hz during O3b. Typical inferred surface velocities are 0.2--0.5 $μ$m/s, and inferred surface displacements are 0.1--0.3 $μ$m. The Gaussian Mixture Model performs most consistently for complex or overlapping features, with mean frequency offsets within 5 Hz of the Gravity Spy baseline. Our results show that ArchGEM provides a practical tool for detector characterization by linking observed spectrogram features to the motion of scattering surfaces and helping guide future mitigation of scattered light noise in current and next-generation interferometers. By quantifying the temporal and spectral behavior of scattered light, ArchGEM provides a robust framework for diagnosing noise sources and guiding targeted mitigation strategies in future detector upgrades.

AI.tldr: ArchGEM is an automated framework that identifies and characterizes scattered light arches in Advanced LIGO data, linking spectrogram features to the motion of scattering surfaces. It uses a prominence-based peak-finding method and Gaussian Mixture Model clustering to analyze glitches across O3 and O4 runs, revealing frequency distribution shifts and surface motion parameters, aiding future detector upgrades.
AI.motivation: To address scattered light noise—a major non-stationary low-frequency noise in LIGO detectors—and improve detector characterization by automatically identifying arch-like features, inferring physical properties of scattering surfaces, and guiding mitigation strategies.
AI.method: ArchGEM combines prominence-based peak detection with Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clustering to process time-frequency spectrograms. Applied to O3 and O4 data, it captures varied arch morphologies and extracts parameters like frequency, surface velocity, and displacement.
AI.result: Frequency distributions shifted from 15–25 Hz (O3a/O4) to 20–40 Hz (O3b). Inferred surface velocities: 0.2–0.5 μm/s; displacements: 0.1–0.3 μm. GMM showed consistency in complex scenarios, aligning with Gravity Spy baselines within 5 Hz.
AI.conclusion: ArchGEM effectively characterizes scattered light noise, enabling better understanding of detector noise sources and facilitating targeted upgrades for current/next-gen interferometers by connecting observed features to surface motion properties.

title: Two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian in annihilation channel
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, 2 ancillary files containing analytical expressions
summary: We calculate the two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian in the annihilation channel within the framework of potential-NRQCD effective field theory. The result agrees with the previous calculation of the corresponding four-quark operator in NRQCD for SU(N) color gauge group. We further obtain an expression with a more general color structure applicable to other gauge groups. Combined with the recently calculated two-loop Hamiltonian in the non-annihilation channel, this completes the full two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian.

AI.tldr: The paper calculates the two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian in the annihilation channel using potential-NRQCD EFT, confirms agreement with prior NRQCD results for SU(N) gauge groups, and extends the expression to more general color structures, completing the two-loop Hamiltonian when combined with previous non-annihilation channel results.
AI.motivation: To fully determine the two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian by addressing the annihilation channel, which was previously missing, and to generalize the color structure beyond SU(N) for broader applicability.
AI.method: Using potential-NRQCD effective field theory, the authors computed the two-loop contributions in the annihilation channel, cross-verified their findings against existing four-quark operator calculations in NRQCD for SU(N) groups, and derived a color structure generalization.
AI.result: Confirmed agreement with prior SU(N) results, derived a generalized color structure expression, and completed the full two-loop Hamiltonian when combined with prior non-annihilation channel calculations.
AI.conclusion: The completion of the two-loop quarkonium Hamiltonian paves the way for more precise theoretical predictions in quarkonium physics, enabling better comparisons with experimental data and enhancing the reliability of NRQCD-based calculations.

title: UHECR doublets and their conditional association with nearby radio galaxies
summary: The origin of ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (UHECRs) remains a fundamental question in astroparticle physics. While localized 3 $σ$ correlations with active galactic nuclei and starburst galaxies have been reported using time-integrated analyses, we propose and implement a spatiotemporal multiplet search method utilizing a pre-defined fixed window of 3 degrees and 15 days, a kinematic filter designed to isolate high-rigidity particles and keep chance coincidences low. Applying this method to 16 years of Pierre Auger Observatory data, we identify 28 UHECR multiplets (doublets) above 32 EeV. We backtrack these trajectories using three Galactic magnetic field models across eight distinct nuclear species. Testing the backtracked directions against the ten nearest bright radio galaxies yields an overall post-trial significance of 5.8 $σ$, conditioned on the individual best-fit associations. Specifically, we find a 4.5 $σ$ conditional post-trial significance for the joint association of 8 of these multiplets with the Fornax A region alone. These results point to radio galaxies, with a strong contribution from Fornax A, as long-term accelerators of heavy (Z > 3) UHECRs, possibly within the mildly relativistic backflows of their extended radio lobes, detected at Earth primarily as independent secondary fragments above 32 EeV.

AI.tldr: The study identifies 28 ultra-high-energy cosmic ray (UHECR) multiplets correlated with radio galaxies, particularly Fornax A, suggesting these galaxies act as long-term accelerators of heavy nuclei via backflows in radio lobes.
AI.motivation: To address the origin of UHECRs by improving upon prior time-integrated analyses that found weak associations with AGNs and starburst galaxies, aiming for stronger spatiotemporal correlations.
AI.method: A spatiotemporal multiplet search with a fixed 3-degree spatial and 15-day temporal window, kinematic filtering for high-rigidity particles, applying to 16 years of Pierre Auger data, and backtracking trajectories through Galactic magnetic field models.
AI.result: 28 UHECR multiplets above 32 EeV identified; significant 5.8σ post-trial association with nearby radio galaxies, with Fornax A showing a 4.5σ connection for 8 multiplets.
AI.conclusion: Radio galaxies, especially Fornax A, are plausible long-term UHECR sources accelerating heavy nuclei (Z>3), likely via mild relativistic backflows in radio lobes, with detected particles as secondary fragments.

title: A Complete Invariant Analysis of the Kerr Spacetime and its Photon Region
summary: We present an invariant characterization of the Kerr spacetime, and utilize the invariant structure of the spacetime to derive a function whose zeros identify a special family of null geodesics. Each member of this family is tangent to every photon surface in the Kerr photon region, offering a method of invariantly characterizing photon surfaces in axially symmetric spacetimes and thereby a providing a computational tool for efficiently computing the geodesic equations for any part of the photon region. The invariant that identifies all of the spherical photon orbits is parameterized by a Lorentz parameter, where the parameter is effectively an inclination angle of the spherical photon orbits through the equatorial plane. We also show how the invariant determines the constants of motion for all spherical orbits in the photon region. Finally, we briefly derive invariants which identify the other geometrically important surfaces such as the ergosurfaces and local horizons.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an invariant method to characterize the Kerr spacetime's photon region, identifying spherical photon orbits and important geometric surfaces via an invariant function dependent on a Lorentz parameter. This provides a computational tool for analyzing geodesics and constants of motion in axially symmetric spacetimes.
AI.motivation: To develop an invariant characterization of photon surfaces and geometric features in Kerr spacetime for a unified, computationally efficient approach to studying null geodesics and their properties, avoiding coordinate dependencies.
AI.method: The authors derive an invariant function whose zeros correspond to special null geodesics tangent to photon surfaces. This function uses a Lorentz parameter representing inclination angles relative to the equatorial plane. Invariants are also constructed for ergosurfaces and horizons.
AI.result: Successfully identified all spherical photon orbits in the Kerr spacetime through the invariant function, determined constants of motion for these orbits, and derived invariants for other geometric surfaces. This framework offers an efficient computational method for analyzing photon regions.
AI.conclusion: The invariant approach provides a coordinate-independent tool for studying photon surfaces and geometric structures in axially symmetric spacetimes, simplifying geodesic computations and enhancing understanding of relativistic phenomena like photon capture and orbits near black holes.

title: High-Precision Ground Characterization of Test-Mass Magnetic Properties for the Taiji Gravitational Wave Mission via a Physics-Informed Neural Framework
summary: Taiji is a gravitational wave detection mission in space initiated by the Chinese Academy of Sciences, which will open the millihertz window through a heliocentric triangular constellation of three drag-free spacecraft. Its ultimate sensitivity is determined partly by the residual acceleration noise of the gravitational reference sensors (GRS), within which the coupling between the test-mass and the fluctuating environmental magnetic field constitutes one of the key stray-force contributions. Following the path established by the LISA and TianQin teams, high-precision ground characterization of remanent magnetic moment $\vec{m}_r$ and volume susceptibility $χ$ of the test masses is a central step in the Taiji pre-launch test program. A persistent challenge for this characterization is the non-stationary, colored background noise inherent to torsion-pendulum facilities, which systematically biases classical Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) and Kalman filter (KF) estimators. We propose an AI-enhanced Differentiable Weighted Least Squares (AI-WLS) framework that fuses a dilated one-dimensional residual network, acting as a dynamic noise evaluator, with a fully differentiable analytical physical solver. This architecture preserves the exact linear mapping from the magnetic parameters to the torque response while autonomously identifying and suppressing contaminated data segments. Validated on real measured noise from the Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics torsion-pendulum facility developed for Taiji, which achieves a torque sensitivity of order $10^{-13}\,\mathrm{N\cdot m\,Hz^{-1/2}}$, the AI-WLS framework bounds the maximum absolute estimation errors at $4.46\times 10^{-10}\,\mathrm{A\cdot m^2}$ for $\vec{m}_r$ and $7.8\times 10^{-8}$ for $χ$, satisfying Taiji's ground-test requirements on all these parameters simultaneously.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the AI-WLS framework to address noise issues in magnetic parameter estimation for Taiji's gravitational wave mission, improving accuracy beyond traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance the sensitivity of the Taiji mission by accurately characterizing test mass magnetic properties amidst noisy torsion-pendulum data.
AI.method: Develop AI-WLS combining a dilated 1D residual network with a differentiable physical solver, enabling dynamic noise evaluation and data segment filtering.
AI.result: AI-WLS achieved estimation errors of 4.46e-10 A·m² for remanent moment and 7.8e-8 for susceptibility, meeting Taiji's requirements using real facility data.
AI.conclusion: The AI-WLS framework effectively suppresses non-stationary noise, ensuring reliable magnetic parameter estimation critical for Taiji's success.

title: Some approximate renormalization group invariants for supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model and the Yukawa unification
summary: For supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model we construct some expressions that include Yukawa couplings for the third and second generations and receive relatively small quantum corrections. This implies that they slightly depend on scale and are therefore approximate renormalization group invariants. Using these invariants we try to analyse possible relations between the Yukawa couplings at the unification scale $M_X$ as well as the predictions for values of $\mbox{tg}\,β$ and $α(M_X)$. In particular, we suggest two variants of such relations and investigate whether they agree with the experimental values of elementary particle masses. It is demonstrated that the Yukawa unification for the third and second generations consistent with them can be achieved by adding exotic superfields forming 3 representations $5+\bar{5}$ of the group $SU(5)$ to the MSSM field content. We argue that this may indicate the possible underlying $E_6$ gauge symmetry.

AI.tldr: The paper explores supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model by constructing approximate renormalization group invariants from Yukawa couplings of the second and third generations. These invariants help analyze Yukawa unification at the unification scale, suggest relations for tg β and α, and propose adding 5+5̄ SU(5) representations to MSSM, hinting at an E6 gauge symmetry.
AI.motivation: To investigate Yukawa coupling relations in supersymmetric models, predict parameters like tg β and α at the unification scale, and explore the implications for grand unified theories, particularly E6 symmetry.
AI.method: Constructing scale-independent (RG-invariant) expressions from Yukawa couplings of the second and third generations, adding exotic 5+5̄ SU(5) superfields to MSSM, and testing compatibility with experimental particle mass data.
AI.result: Shows that Yukawa unification between second and third generations can be achieved with added superfields, yielding consistency with observed masses and suggesting an underlying E6 symmetry.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MSSM extensions with SU(5) 5+5̄ representations and RG invariants provide a viable path for Yukawa unification and hint at E6 as a higher symmetry, offering insights into supersymmetric GUTs.

title: The line modulations of H-like Fe, Ca, Ar, and S observed with $XRISM$/Resolve in Cyg X-3
comment: 12 pages, 7 figures, accepted for publication in PASJ
summary: Cygnus X-3, hosting a Wolf-Rayet (WR) star whose dense wind produces various spectral lines due to photoionization by X-rays from a compact object, provides an ideal laboratory for studying wind dynamics and density structure. We measured the orbital modulations of the Fe, Ca, Ar, and S Ly$α$ lines observed with the X-ray microcalorimeter (Resolve) onboard the $XRISM$, taking account of both emission and absorption lines of the Ly$α$ complexes. The modulations of Doppler shifts of the Fe, Ca, Ar, and S Ly$α$ lines showed amplitudes of 500 km s$^{-1}$ and phase offsets of 0.04, 0.09, 0.11, and 0.17, respectively, in units of an orbital period (4.8 hours) relative to the orbital motion of the compact object. This result indicated that H-like Fe most closely follows the compact object's motion. The line widths ranged from 400 to 1000 km s$^{-1}$. The intensities of both emission and absorption lines reached their minima around orbital phase 0.0 and their maxima around phase 0.5. The absorption peaks, however, did not align exactly with phase 0.5, suggesting the inhomogeneous structures such as an accretion wake and/or a bow shock. We compared the observed modulations with calculations based on a stellar wind model, accelerated by ultraviolet radiation from the WR star. One of the calculations qualitatively reproduced the observed trend that H-like Fe ions were concentrated near the compact object, whereas H-like S was distributed across the binary system, with H-like Ca and Ar showing intermediate spatial distributions. From this comparison, we estimated that a mass-loss rate of the stellar wind was approximately $5 \times 10^{-6}$-$1 \times 10^{-5}\ M_{\odot}\ {\rm yr}^{-1}$.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the orbital modulations of X-ray spectral lines (Fe, Ca, Ar, S Lyα) from Cygnus X-3, revealing insights into wind dynamics, density structures, and mass-loss rates using XRISM data.
AI.motivation: To study wind dynamics and density structures in Cygnus X-3 through the analysis of line modulations caused by the dense WR star wind and X-ray photoionization.
AI.method: Measured orbital phase shifts and Doppler shifts of Fe, Ca, Ar, and S Lyα lines using XRISM's Resolve instrument, comparing observations with a stellar wind model accelerated by UV radiation.
AI.result: Line amplitudes (~500 km/s), phase offsets (0.04-0.17 orbital phases), varying line widths (400-1000 km/s), and intensity peaks at phase 0.5 indicate inhomogeneous structures like accretion wakes or bow shocks. Modeled mass-loss rate: 5×10⁻⁶–1×10⁻⁵ M☉/yr.
AI.conclusion: H-like Fe ions cluster near the compact object, while H-like S is widespread, supporting the role of UV-driven winds and structural inhomogeneities in shaping line modulations.

title: Gravitational Collapse of an Inhomogeneous Fluid in Rastall Theory
summary: We study spherically symmetric gravitational collapse of an inhomogeneous fluid with anisotropic energy momentum tensor (EMT) in Rastall gravity. Considering a linear equation of state (EoS) for the fluid profiles, i.e., $p_r=w_rρ$ and $p_θ=w_θρ$, we try to build and investigate non-singular collapse scenarios for which, the spacetime singularity that appears in the homogeneous case~\cite{ahz2019}, is absent. We therefore set the Rastall parameter in such a way that the effective radial pressure vanishes. This helps us to obtain a class of exact nonsingular solutions in which the matter shells undergo a {collapse} process in a contracting regime, reach a bounce point, and then enter an expanding phase. We further investigate formation of trapped surfaces during the dynamical evolution of the collapsing body. {It is found} that for the obtained solutions, {the} trapped surface formation can be avoided and consequently, the bounce event is not covered by the apparent horizon. Validity of weak energy condition (WEC) is also examined for the obtained solutions.

AI.tldr: This study explores nonsingular gravitational collapse in Rastall gravity using an inhomogeneous fluid with anisotropic EMT, achieving bounce without singularity by adjusting the Rastall parameter.
AI.motivation: To investigate non-singular collapse scenarios in Rastall gravity, avoiding the spacetime singularity present in homogeneous cases and examining trapped surface formation and WEC validity.
AI.method: Using linear EoS (p_r = w_rρ, p_θ = w_θρ) for an inhomogeneous, anisotropic fluid, the authors model gravitational collapse in Rastall gravity, set the Rastall parameter to nullify effective radial pressure, derive exact nonsingular solutions, and analyze trapped surfaces and WEC compliance.
AI.result: Obtained nonsingular solutions where matter shells collapse, bounce, and expand; trapped surface formation is avoided, leaving the bounce exposed, and WEC is validated for these solutions.
AI.conclusion: Rastall gravity with specific parameter choices and anisotropic fluids can produce nonsingular bounce scenarios, evading singularities and trapped surfaces, offering a viable alternative to standard singularity-ridden models.

title: Beyond the Final Label: Exploiting the Untapped Potential of Classification Histories in Astronomical Light Curve Analysis
comment: 26 pages, 10 figures; prepared for submission to ApJ
summary: The Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) on the Vera C. Rubin Observatory will generate a massive collection of time series (light curves) of the measured flux of transient and variable astronomical objects. With each new flux observation, light curve classifiers need to generate updated probability distributions over candidate classes, which will then be shared with the global community for the purpose of identifying interesting targets for follow-up observations as well as less time-sensitive analysis applications. Using the synthetic light curves and classification results of participating classifiers from the Extended LSST Astronomical Time-series Classification Challenge (ELAsTiCC), we investigate a novel framework to enhance existing light curve classifications by incorporating their classification histories and the temporal evolution of these histories. To demonstrate the potential of this approach, we introduce a model that combines a recurrent neural network and an additive attention module, which shows improved classification accuracy and more balanced precision-recall performance compared to existing classifiers from the challenge. Furthermore, at this stage, most, if not all, of the existing classifiers are evaluated by their final classification results on complete light curves; we propose new metrics that evaluate the stability, accuracy, and early classification performance of a classifier's predictions when using limited data by considering the Wasserstein distance between the temporally evolving classification probability distributions. Our metrics offer a more comprehensive perspective for model assessment by supplementing classical methods such as the confusion matrix and precision-recall.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework that enhances light curve classifications using classification history and temporal evolution, employing a recurrent neural network with an additive attention module for improved accuracy and balanced precision-recall. It also proposes new evaluation metrics based on Wasserstein distance to assess model performance with limited data.
AI.motivation: The LSST requires real-time, evolving classifications of astronomical light curves to identify targets for follow-up observations. Existing classifiers are primarily evaluated by final results on complete data, neglecting stability and early performance.
AI.method: The proposed method combines a recurrent neural network and additive attention to model temporal evolution of classification probabilities. New metrics using Wasserstein distance evaluate stability, accuracy, and early performance by analyzing evolving probability distributions.
AI.result: The model shows improved classification accuracy and better balance in precision-recall compared to existing ELAsTiCC classifiers. Proposed metrics provide a more comprehensive evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating classification history and temporal dynamics improves light curve classification. New evaluation metrics are essential for assessing real-time, evolving predictions in large-scale surveys like LSST.
