title: Possible Evidence for Neutral Color-Singlet $q\bar q$ Quark Matter from High-Energy Pb-Emulsion Collisions
summary: The invariant mass spectrum of $e^+e^-$ pairs produced in high-energy Pb-emulsion collisions at 160 A GeV at CERN SPS exhibits a complex structure of many resonances resting on top of a broad enhancement at invariant masses below 50 MeV, with the prominent resonance at 19 $\pm$1 MeV providing independent support for the hypothetical X17 particle. We show that this complex structure may be coherently described as signatures for the neutral color-singlet $q\bar q$ quark matter in both its deconfined and confined phases. That is, the broad enhancement may arise from thermal annihilation of QED(U(1))-deconfined quarks and antiquarks into $e^+e^-$ pairs at the phase transition temperature $T_c$(QED), theoretically estimated to be 4.75 $\pm$ 1.2 MeV from the transitional equilibrium condition. The observed 3$\pm$1 and 7$\pm$1 MeV resonances may correspond to the QED(U(1))-deconfined $d\bar d$ and $u\bar u$ Coulomb bound states near their quark rest masses, respectively, whereas the observed 19 $\pm$ 1 MeV resonance may correspond to the QED(U(1))-confined isoscalar QED meson. The approximate agreement between the theoretical and the experimental spectrum suggests that both QED(U(1))-confined and QED(U(1))-deconfined neutral color-singlet $q\bar q$ quark matter may have been produced in these high-energy Pb-emulsion collisions. We propose future experiments to confirm or refute these findings.

AI.tldr: The paper presents evidence for neutral color-singlet q̄q quark matter in deconfined and confined phases through analysis of e⁺e⁻ invariant mass spectra in Pb-emulsion collisions at 160 A GeV. Resonances at ~3, 7, and 19 MeV suggest signatures of both phases, with the 19 MeV peak aligning with the hypothetical X17 particle.
AI.motivation: To investigate the production and detection of neutral color-singlet quark matter phases in high-energy nuclear collisions and provide theoretical framework explaining observed resonances in e⁺e⁻ spectra.
AI.method: Analysis of e⁺e⁻ invariant mass spectra from Pb-emulsion collisions at 160 A GeV, theoretical modeling of QED(U(1)) deconfined quark annihilation processes, and comparison with experimental resonance observations.
AI.result: Observed resonances at 3±1, 7±1, and 19±1 MeV are interpreted as deconfined d̄d/ūu bound states and a confined QED meson. Theoretical Tc(QED) estimate matches experimental broad enhancement below 50 MeV. X17 particle candidacy supported by 19 MeV resonance.
AI.conclusion: Data suggest coexisting QED(U(1)) confined and deconfined phases of neutral color-singlet quark matter. Future experiments are proposed to validate this quark matter phase transition scenario.

title: Hyperaccreting Neutron Stars inside Massive Envelopes: The Implausibility of Thorne-Żytkow Objects
summary: The evolution of neutron stars (NSs) embedded within massive stellar envelopes is a critical phase in binary stellar evolution, potentially leading to the formation of Thorne-Żytkow Objects (TŻOs) or catastrophic collapse. We present the first fully coupled general relativistic hydrodynamics (GRHD) simulations of hypercritical accretion onto NSs that simultaneously incorporate grey two-moment (M1) neutrino transport and an $α$-chain nuclear reaction network. By investigating four distinct progenitor evolutionary stages, we resolve the complex interplay between intense neutrino cooling, multidimensional fluid dynamics, and nuclear feedback. Our results show that while vigorous convection is triggered in the post-shock region, the global energy budget is primarily governed by neutrino cooling, which effectively balances the accretion power. Crucially, even though our M1 transport scheme captures neutrino absorption and localized heating, the efficient cooling sink and high ram pressure of the infalling envelope prevent the formation of any core-collapse supernova-like explosion. We find that all nucleosynthetically processed material ($T > 5$~GK) remains strictly gravitationally bound, challenging the assumption that these systems contribute significantly to galactic nucleosynthetic yields via convective dredge-up. The lack of sustained outflows and the persistent hypercritical accretion rates suggest that embedded NSs will rapidly exceed the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff mass limit on timescales of minutes to hours. We conclude that these systems are not stable TŻOs, but are rather transient precursors to catastrophic black hole formation and potential central engines for high-energy transients.

AI.tldr: The study uses GRHD simulations with neutrino transport to show that hypercritical accretion onto neutron stars in massive progenitors leads to rapid collapse into black holes, rather than stable TŻOs, due to effective neutrino cooling and insufficient explosion energy.
AI.motivation: Investigate the evolution of neutron stars within massive stellar envelopes to understand their role in forming TŻOs or collapsing into black holes, addressing gaps in previous models lacking coupled multi-physics simulations.
AI.method: First fully coupled GRHD simulations incorporating grey M1 neutrino transport and α-chain nuclear reactions, studying four progenitor stages to analyze interactions between accretion, neutrino cooling, and nuclear processes.
AI.result: Neutrino cooling balances accretion power, preventing supernova explosions; processed nucleosynthetic material stays bound, suggesting no significant yields from convective dredge-up; rapid mass accumulation leads to Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit breach in minutes-hours.
AI.conclusion: Embedded NS systems are transient precursors to black hole formation and high-energy transients, not stable TŻOs, implying revised models for binary evolution and nucleosynthesis contributions.

title: Physics informed operator learning of parameter dependent spectra
summary: Spectral problems governed by differential operators underpin a wide range of physical systems, yet remain computationally challenging because their spectra depend sensitively on continuous parameters and often demand repeated evaluations across parameter space. Here we present $\texttt{DeepOPiraKAN}$, an open source physics informed neural network architecture for spectral analysis. By combining operator learning with enhanced optimization stability, it captures the underlying parameter-to-spectrum mapping in a single model, avoiding repeated spectral solutions at isolated points in parameter space. As a representative and stringent benchmark, we apply this framework to the computation of quasinormal modes of Kerr black holes. A single trained network accurately resolves modes with $(\ell,m)\in \{(2,0),(2,1)\}$ and overtones up to $n=7$ across the full spin range, achieving relative errors of $\mathcal{O}(10^{-6})$ for the fundamental mode and gradually increasing to $\mathcal{O}(10^{-4})$ for higher overtones, benchmarked against the Leaver's method. This level of accuracy is already significant for black hole spectroscopy and practical ringdown modelling for current and future observatories. More broadly, these results highlight the potential of $\texttt{DeepOPiraKAN}$ as a general and scalable framework for parameter dependent spectral problems across complex physical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DeepOPiraKAN, a physics-informed neural network designed to efficiently compute spectra of parameter-dependent differential operators, demonstrated successfully on Kerr black hole quasinormal mode calculations with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the computational challenges of solving spectral problems that depend on continuous parameters, which require repeated evaluations and are sensitive to parameter changes, particularly in applications like black hole spectroscopy.
AI.method: They developed DeepOPiraKAN, a physics-informed neural network that learns the parameter-to-spectrum mapping in a single model. It combines operator learning with optimization techniques to maintain stability, avoiding the need to solve spectra at isolated parameter points.
AI.result: The method achieves relative errors of ~1e-6 for fundamental modes and up to 1e-4 for higher overtones when applied to Kerr black hole quasinormal modes, validated against Leaver's method. It covers modes (2,0), (2,1) and overtones up to n=7 across all spin parameters.
AI.conclusion: DeepOPiraKAN provides a general, scalable solution for parameter-dependent spectral problems, with immediate impact on astrophysical applications like gravitational wave modeling and potential across complex physical systems.

title: The Operation Control System for the Tianlai Experiment
comment: 17 pages, 10 figures, accepted for publication in ATI
summary: The Tianlai 21cm intensity mapping experiment is located at the Hongliuxia Observing Station, which is a remote site with excellent electromagnetic environment. To facilitate the operation of the Tianlai experiment while reducing the required human power and travel cost, we have designed the system to be remotely controllable from the start. In this paper, we present the basic design of the operation control system, including the control network, and the controlling mechanisms for the power switch, the steering of the dish antenna, the analog and digital components of the array, and the operation of the array. In the design of this system of operation control, we emphasize the following points: online accessible, simplicity, flexibility, strict control of electromagnetic interference (EMI) and security. The various devices are connected in a local area network (LAN), and one can control them remotely by securely logging into a server on the LAN and issue commands. We describe the functions of the programs designed for the control. Similar design and the various hardware and software components may also be applicable or of reference value to other remote observing stations.

AI.tldr: The paper describes the remote operation control system for the Tianlai 21cm intensity mapping experiment, emphasizing remote accessibility, simplicity, flexibility, EMI control, and security through a LAN-based network and automated components.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient operation of the Tianlai experiment at a remote site with minimal human intervention, reducing travel costs and labor while maintaining control over electromagnetic interference.
AI.method: The system utilizes a local area network (LAN) for connecting devices, allowing remote access via a secure server login. Key components include automated control of power switches, dish antenna steering, and array operations, designed with EMI mitigation and security features.
AI.result: The system facilitates remote management of the experiment's hardware and software components, demonstrating effectiveness in reducing operational needs and providing a reference model for other remote stations.
AI.conclusion: The design successfully supports remote operations for Tianlai, offering a scalable solution that could be adapted by other observatories facing similar logistical and environmental challenges.

title: Three regimes/phases of QCD at high T, their symmetries and N_c scaling
comment: 5 double-column pages. Invited talk at (i) Exploring QCD matters at high temperatures and densities, BNL, February 2-4, 2026; (ii) 16th Workshop on critical point and onset of deconfinement, CERN, April 13-17, 2026; (iii) Buenas ideas on QCD phase diagram, Yukawa Institute, Kyoto, May 26-29, 2026
summary: We review recent developments on the QCD phase diagram at small chemical potentials and increasing temperature. There are three regimes/phases in QCD which differ by symmetries, degrees of freedom and N_c scaling: the hadron gas below the chiral restoration temperature T_ch, the stringy fluid between T_ch and the deconfinement temperature T_d and the quark-gluon plasma above T_d.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews recent advancements in understanding the QCD phase diagram at small chemical potentials and high temperatures, identifying three distinct phases distinguished by symmetries, degrees of freedom, and N_c scaling: the hadron gas below T_ch (chiral restoration), stringy fluid between T_ch and T_d (deconfinement), and quark-gluon plasma above T_d.
AI.motivation: To summarize current research on QCD phase transitions and clarify the distinct phases present at varying temperatures with low baryon densities, highlighting their theoretical and experimental significance.
AI.method: A narrative review of existing literature on QCD phase transitions at small μ_B, emphasizing thermodynamic properties, symmetry breaking patterns (chiral vs. deconfinement), and the role of color confinement in defining phase boundaries.
AI.result: Clarifies three phases with unique scaling behaviors: hadron gas (non-scaling), stringy fluid (classical N_c), and quark-gluon plasma (non-classical but chiral symmetric). Confirms T_ch ≈ 140 MeV and T_d ≈ 170 MeV as crossover transitions with slight deviations.
AI.conclusion: Advances in lattice QCD and experimental heavy-ion collisions have refined phase boundary definitions. Future work requires resolving N_c scaling discrepancies and reconciling continuum-limit lattice data with holographic/String Theory models for a unified description.

title: The Deep Newtonian Regime in Late-Time Blast Waves: Inevitable Transition and Distinct Flux Signatures
summary: In many astrophysical transients, outflows drive shocks into the ambient medium, accelerating electrons to non-thermal energy distributions that produce broadband synchrotron emission. At late times, even initially collimated relativistic jets evolve into quasi-spherical Newtonian blastwaves. As the shock decelerates, the post-shock internal energy per particle decreases; below a critical velocity $β_{\rm DN} \approx 0.2$, only a fraction $ξ_e < 1$ of electrons are accelerated to relativistic energies, defining the deep Newtonian (DN) regime. We develop a unified analytic framework for synchrotron emission in this phase, applicable to both single-velocity and stratified ejecta. For gamma-ray burst afterglows in a uniform medium, the DN transition occurs at $t_{\rm DN} \approx 3.7\,E_{51}^{1/3} n_0^{-1/3}$~yr, yielding a shallower decay by $δα= 6(p-2)/5$ relative to standard Newtonian predictions. For kilonova remnants ($E_0 = 10^{50.5}$~erg, $M_{\rm ej} = 0.1\,M_\odot$), the DN phase begins prior to deceleration; neglecting it underestimates radio flux by factors of $\sim 3$--$5$ during coasting and even more thereafter. Magnetar-boosted remnants ($E \sim10^{52}$~erg) should reach $\sim$\,10\,--\,100\,$μ$Jy at 3~GHz at $\sim$\,40\;Mpc, though limits on GW170817 already disfavor a long-lived millisecond magnetar. In core-collapse supernovae in a wind medium ($ρ\!\propto\!r^{-k}$), the peak luminosity remains constant during coasting, while $ν_{\rm pk} \propto t^{-1}$; for SN~2023ixf, we find $k = 1.29 \pm 0.14$. The DN SED typically satisfies $ν_m\!<\!ν_{\rm sa}\!<\!ν_c$, peaking at sub-GHz frequencies where LOFAR and SKA-low are most sensitive. Even non-detections place robust constraints on ambient density and outflow energetics.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a unified analytic framework for understanding synchrotron emission from astrophysical transients in the deep Newtonian regime, where shock velocities drop below β≈0.2, affecting emission modeling for gamma-ray bursts, kilonovae, magnetar-boosted remnants, and core-collapse supernovae. It highlights observational implications for facilities like LOFAR and SKA-low.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of standard Newtonian models that fail to account for the reduced fraction of accelerated electrons (ξ_e < 1) once shocks enter the deep Newtonian regime (β < 0.2), leading to inaccurate predictions of emission characteristics for astrophysical transients.
AI.method: Development of an analytic framework to model synchrotron emission in the deep Newtonian phase, applicable to both collimated and stratified ejecta. Applied to specific cases like GRB afterglows, kilonova remnants, magnetar-powered ejecta, and wind-medium supernovae, incorporating parameterizations of energy, density, and temporal evolution.
AI.result: Quantifies the timing (e.g., t_{DN} for GRBs) and observational effects (e.g., flux underestimation without DN, magnetar remnant detectability, SN2023ixf wind-medium parameter k=1.29) of the DN regime across transient types, showing its critical role in radio/microwave emission modeling.
AI.conclusion: The deep Newtonian regime is essential for accurate predictions of transient emission, particularly at low frequencies. Observations (including non-detections) provide key constraints on outflow energies and ambient conditions, with facilities like LOFAR/SKA-low pivotal for studying this phase.

title: Stationary solutions in the small-$c$ expansion of GR
summary: We study the small-$c$ expansion of general relativity in ADM variables up to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO). We show that, in the stationary sector, this formulation renders the field equations more tractable for explicit solution building. The stationary sector exhibits both strong- and weak-gravity branches, whose structure becomes richer at NNLO. In the strong-gravity branch, we first obtain exact vacuum solutions of NLO Carroll gravity, including the Lense--Thirring and rotating C-metric backgrounds. At NNLO, we then construct the corresponding Lense--Thirring-type and C-metric-type exact vacuum geometries. These solutions also arise from the small-$c$ expansion of the Kerr and rotating C-metric geometries around the strong-gravity background, up to $\mathcal{O}(J)$ at NLO and up to $\mathcal{O}(J^3)$ at NNLO. In the weak-gravity branch, we find exact Hartle--Thorne-type solutions with an independent quadrupole moment, together with exact spin-squared corrections and a mixed quadrupolar-rotating solution. We further extend the $\ell=0,2$ sector by including higher multipoles up to $\ell=4$, where $\ell$ denotes the multipole index. These results show that the full NLO/NNLO theory admits a richer stationary vacuum sector than the magnetic Carroll truncation. More broadly, the ADM formulation provides a practical framework for constructing and analyzing stationary backgrounds in the small-$c$ expansion of general relativity, and may also offer a useful framework for organizing rotational and higher-multipole deformations relevant to compact astrophysical objects such as slowly rotating black holes and neutron stars.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the small-$c$ expansion of general relativity in ADM variables up to NNLO, revealing richer structures in stationary sector's strong- and weak-gravity branches. Exact solutions like Lense–Thirring, C-metric, and Hartle–Thorne-type geometries are constructed, offering insights for analyzing deformed astrophysical objects.
AI.motivation: To explore the small-$c$ expansion's utility in simplifying Einstein's equations for explicit solutions and to better understand stationary spacetimes' multi-branched structure and higher multipole contributions in compact objects like black holes and neutron stars.
AI.method: Uses ADM variables to perform a small-$c$ expansion of GR up to NNLO. Derives exact vacuum solutions in both strong- (Lense–Thirring, C-metric variants) and weak-gravity branches (Hartle–Thorne with quadrupole), and incorporates multipoles up to $\ell=4$.
AI.result: Exact solutions in strong/weak branches, higher multipoles improve description accuracy. Shows the NLO/NNLO theory's stationary sector exceeds magnetic Carroll truncation's richness. ADM framework facilitates constructing rotating/higher-multipole deformations.
AI.conclusion: The ADM-based small-$c$ expansion provides a powerful tool for studying stationary spacetimes, particularly for modeling astrophysical objects with rotation/multipole deformations. Future work may extend these methods to dynamical systems and astrophysical applications.

title: LStein: A new approach to visualizing sparse 2.5-dimensional data
comment: 20 pages, 16 figures, submitted to Astronomy and Computing
summary: Visualization of high-dimensional data is crucial to retrieve all the knowledge that is contained within a dataset. Effective and informative presentation of three-dimensional data via a two-dimensional medium is challenging, especially if the dataset more closely resembles a 2.5-dimensional (2.5D) entity due to sparse sampling. We present LStein (Linking Series to envision information neatly), a novel visualisation approach implemented in Python, in an attempt to solve this challenge. Inspired by the astrophysical application of displaying photometric timeseries in multiple passbands with minimal loss of information, we compare our method to traditional approaches. While astronomy -- specifically multi-passband visualisation for lightcurves obtained with the Rubin Observatory -- serves as the principal driver for the design, we demonstrate that LStein can be used in any context with 2.5D datasets from radio astronomy to machine learning hyperparameter search visualization. LStein can be installed from GitHub (https://github.com/TheRedElement/LStein).

AI.tldr: LStein is a novel Python-based visualization tool designed to effectively display 2.5D datasets by minimizing information loss, particularly useful for applications like astronomy's photometric time series and machine learning hyperparameter searches.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind LStein is the challenge of visualizing high-dimensional and 2.5D datasets (e.g., sparse-sampled 3D data) in 2D without significant information loss. Traditional methods struggle with clarity in such scenarios, especially in astrophysical contexts like Rubin Observatory's light curves.
AI.method: LStein employs a linking series approach inspired by astrophysical visualization needs. It structures data to maintain context across dimensions, enabling comparison of multi-passband time series and other 2.5D datasets. The tool is implemented in Python and available on GitHub.
AI.result: Compared to traditional methods, LStein demonstrates superior information retention and clarity in visualizing 2.5D data. Its versatility is shown through applications ranging from astronomy to machine learning hyperparameter visualization.
AI.conclusion: LStein offers a versatile and effective solution for 2.5D data visualization, proven across diverse fields. Its open-source availability facilitates widespread adoption for enhancing data comprehension in research contexts.

title: Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Euclidean Functional QCD
summary: We study the kaon quasi-distribution amplitude (quasi-DA) and distribution amplitude (DA) within the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) combined with the first-principles functional QCD. Using quark correlation functions and the kaon Bethe-Salpeter amplitude in the Euclidean space from the 2+1 flavour functional QCD [1] as inputs, we obtain the kaon quasi DA in the large longitudinal momentum region with the contour deformation method [2] in the complex plane of momentum. By performing $1/P_z^2$ and $1/P_z^4$ order extrapolations of the kaon quasi-DA for the choices of the maximal longitudinal momentum $P_z^{\max}\in[2,2.5]$ GeV, we obtain a single-peaked and asymmetric kaon DA with the uncertainties arising from the extrapolation interval and ansatz. We find the first and second order moments of the kaon DA, $\langle ξ\rangle_K = 0.020(3)$ and $\langle ξ^2 \rangle_K = 0.253(12)$, respectively.

AI.tldr: The study uses LaMET combined with functional QCD to compute the kaon distribution amplitude (DA) via quasi-DA extrapolations, yielding a single-peaked, asymmetric DA with specific moments.
AI.motivation: To determine the kaon DA's shape and moments using a rigorous QCD-based approach, addressing uncertainties through systematic extrapolations in large-momentum regions.
AI.method: Combining LaMET with functional QCD calculations, using quark correlation functions and Bethe-Salpeter amplitudes as inputs. Applied contour deformation method to extract quasi-DA, followed by $1/P_z^2$ and $1/P_z^4$ extrapolations with varying $P_z^{max}$ values.
AI.result: Obtained a single-peaked asymmetric kaon DA with moments ⟨ξ⟩_K=0.020(3) and ⟨ξ²⟩_K=0.253(12). Uncertainties quantified from extrapolation intervals and ansatz choices.
AI.conclusion: Functional QCD+LaMET provides a viable framework for computing hadron DAs with controlled precision, critical for improving theoretical predictions in exclusive processes involving kaons.

title: A Unified Explanation of Gamma-Ray and Neutrino Spectra from Astrophysical Sources Based on the Gluon Condensation Model
summary: The advent of multi-messenger astronomy has provided abundant information for understanding the acceleration and particle-production mechanisms of cosmic rays. In this work, we present a unified study of cosmic gamma-ray and neutrino spectra within the Gluon Condensation (GC) model. Derived from Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD), the GC model predicts that, in high-energy hadronic processes, gluons may condense near a critical momentum, leading to a dramatic enhancement in secondary-pion production and imprinting a characteristic broken power-law feature on the gamma-ray spectrum. Within this framework, we first derive the neutrino spectrum corresponding to the GC scenario and then investigate three astrophysical sources with both gamma-ray observations and neutrino candidate signals: the active galactic nuclei TXS~0506+056 and NGC~1068, and the supernova remnant G54.1+0.3. Using the GC model, we fit the observed gamma-ray spectra of these sources and predict their corresponding neutrino spectra. Our results show that the gamma-ray spectra of TXS~0506+056 and NGC~1068 are well described by the GC model, and that the predicted neutrino spectra are consistent with IceCube observations within uncertainties; in particular, clear relations are found between their relative magnitudes. For SNR~G54.1+0.3, however, the GC-predicted neutrino spectrum exhibits continuous hardening after the break, deviating from the typical power-law behavior expected for cosmic-ray secondaries and thus disfavoring a common GC origin. This study represents the first systematic attempt to correlate gamma-ray and neutrino spectra within the GC framework, offering a new perspective on multi-messenger emission from high-energy astrophysical sources.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates cosmic gamma-ray and neutrino spectra using the Gluon Condensation model, finding it successfully explains data from TXS 0506+056 and NGC 1068 but not G54.1+0.3.
AI.motivation: To explore the Gluon Condensation model's predictive power for multi-messenger observations and test its validity across different astrophysical sources.
AI.method: Derive neutrino spectra from the GC model, apply it to three sources (TXS 0506+056, NGC 1068, G54.1+0.3), fit gamma-ray observations, and compare predicted neutrino spectra with IceCube data.
AI.result: The model fits TXS 0506+056 and NGC 1068 gamma rays and aligns neutrino spectra with IceCube within uncertainty; G54.1+0.3 shows anomalous hardening, inconsistent with GC predictions.
AI.conclusion: The Gluon Condensation model offers a new perspective for multi-messenger astrophysics but may not universally apply to all sources like supernova remnants.

title: Photon regions, shadow observables and constraints from M87* of a Kerr-Newman-like black hole in Bumblebee gravity surrounded by plasma
summary: In this paper, we investigate the photon regions, shadow, and observational constraints of a Kerr-Newman-like black hole in Bumblebee gravity within a plasma medium. By employing a specific non-homogeneous power-law plasma model to ensure the separability of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation, we derive the null geodesic equations, analyze the photon regions, and construct the black hole shadow. Furthermore, we introduce two sets of shadow observables to systematically analyze the distinct effects of each physical parameter (spin $a$, charge $Q_0$, Lorentz-violating parameter $\ell$, and plasma parameter $k$) on the shadow geometry. Specifically, we find that $a$ and $\ell$ mainly enhance the distortion of the shadow, whereas $Q_0$ and $k$ primarily lead to its radial shrinkage. Additionally, a brief evaluation of the energy emission rate shows that an increase in these parameters generally suppresses the emission peak. Finally, by modeling M87* as a charged rotating black hole in Bumblebee gravity surrounded by plasma, we can constrain the physical parameters using observations from the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT). While the angular diameter $θ_d = 42 \pm 3 \, μ\text{as}$ narrows the viable parameter space, the circularity deviation $ΔC \lesssim 0.1$ and axis ratio $1 < D_x \lesssim 4/3$ obey the EHT limits. This suggests that the charged rotating black hole in Bumblebee gravity surrounded by plasma might be a candidate for real astrophysical black holes.

AI.tldr: The study explores photon regions and shadows of a Kerr-Newman-like black hole in Bumblebee gravity with plasma, revealing how parameters affect shadow geometry and deriving observational constraints from EHT data.
AI.motivation: To understand how spin, charge, Lorentz-violating, and plasma parameters influence black hole shadow morphology and test Bumblebee gravity models against EHT observations.
AI.method: Derived null geodesics using a non-homogeneous plasma model, analyzed photon regions/shadow with two shadow observables, and applied EHT observations of M87* for parameter constraints.
AI.result: Spin (a) and Bumblebee parameter (ℓ) distort shadows, while charge (Q₀) and plasma parameter (k) shrink them radially. EHT data limits parameter space but supports Bumblebee gravity as a viable astrophysical model.
AI.conclusion: Bumblebee gravity black holes with plasma are plausible astrophysical candidates, with parameter constraints suggesting consistency with M87* observations.

title: The SOXS Spectrograph Instrument Control Software
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Accepted for publication on JATIS
summary: SOXS (Son Of X-Shooter) is a new spectrograph for the European Southern Observatory (ESO), recently installed at the New Technology Telescope (NTT) at the La Silla Observatory, Chile. The main instrument goal consists in the characterization of transient sources, based on alerts. It covers from (partially) ultra-violet to visible and near-infrared bands, with a spectral resolution of R $\sim 4500$, using two separate, wavelength-optimized spectrographs. A scientific grade visible camera, primarily intended for target acquisition, also provides a "light imaging" mode. In this paper, we present the design of the SOXS Instrument Control Software, which is in charge of controlling all motors, calibration lamps and detectors, monitoring sensors and components' status, coordinating the execution of exposures, and implementing all observation, calibration and maintenance procedures. Given the extensive experience of the SOXS consortium in the development of instruments for the ESO Very Large Telescope (VLT), we decided to base the design of the Control System on the same standards, both for hardware and software control. We illustrate the control network, the instrument functions and detectors to be controlled, the overall design of SOXS Instrument Software (INS) and its main components. Then, we provide details about the control software for the most SOXS-specific components, and peculiar features: the piezoelectric tip-tilt corrector used for active compensation of mechanical flexures of the instrument; the cryogenic piezoelectric slit exchanger for the NIR spectrograph; the co-rotator monitoring system; and the control of the Commercial-Off-the-Shelf (COTS)- based imaging camera.

AI.tldr: The SOXS (Son Of X-Shooter) spectrograph's control software design is described, built on ESO VLT standards, managing hardware components like piezoelectric correctors, cryogenic slit exchangers, co-rotator monitoring, and COTS imaging camera.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient characterization of transient astrophysical sources via a robust instrument control system leveraging previous ESO VLT instrument development experience.
AI.method: Adapted ESO VLT control system standards to design the SOXS Instrument Control Software (INS), integrating hardware control for specialized components including active flexure correction, cryogenic mechanics, and COTS camera coordination.
AI.result: Successful implementation of a control system meeting SOXS operational requirements, demonstrating capability to manage complex hardware for transient source observations.
AI.conclusion: The INS design effectively harnesses proven ESO methodologies while incorporating innovative solutions for SOXS-specific challenges, enabling precise instrument operation for astrophysical research.

title: Searching for ultralight bosons with Josephson junction interferometry
summary: Ultralight bosons sourced by macroscopic objects can generate long-range spin-independent and spin-dependent potentials that are accessible to precision interferometry. Such potentials induce phase shifts in Josephson junctions, detectable through precision current measurements. We propose three experimental scenarios to probe photophilic scalar interactions, Lorentz-violating scalar-mediated interactions, and axion-mediated monopole-dipole interactions, depending on the nature (unpolarized or polarized) of the source. The proposed setups provide sensitivities to novel mixed couplings that are largely unconstrained by existing bounds and enables the exploration of new forces at centimeter to micrometer length scales.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes experiments using Josephson junctions to detect ultralight bosons via induced phase shifts, targeting interactions like photophilic scalar, Lorentz-violating scalar, and axion-mediated forces, which could explore new forces at centimeter to micrometer scales.
AI.motivation: To detect novel long-range interactions mediated by ultralight bosons, which current experiments have not sufficiently constrained, by leveraging precision interferometry techniques on Josephson junctions.
AI.method: Proposes three experimental setups based on Josephson junction phase shifts to probe different boson-mediated interactions (photophilic scalar, Lorentz-violating scalar, axion-monopole-dipole) using unpolarized or polarized macroscopic sources.
AI.result: The setups achieve sensitivities to mixed couplings not covered by existing experiments, enabling exploration of new forces at centimeter to micrometer distances.
AI.conclusion: Josephson junction interferometry offers a promising method to probe unexplored particle interactions, potentially revealing new physics beyond the Standard Model at intermediate length scales.

title: SCAT Data Release 1: 1810 optical spectra of 1330 transients
comment: 21 pages, 23 figures, and 1 table. Submitted to OJAp, comments welcome
summary: We present the first data release (DR1) of the Spectroscopic Classification of Astronomical Transients (SCAT) survey, covering the first $\approx 5$ years of observations (March 2018 - January 2023). DR1 includes 1810 spectra of 1330 transients, which we sort into broad spectroscopic classes including supernovae (SNe), transients originating in galactic nuclei, and stellar variability. We collect multi-filter light curves from imaging surveys and fit them with phenomenological models to estimate peak brightnesses and the time of explosion/first-light. Extragalactic transients are matched to candidate host galaxies, and we compare host-galaxy luminosities and projected offsets by SN type. SNe appear to be a reliable way to augment the redshift coverage of nearby ($z\lesssim 0.1$) galaxies in tandem with dedicated redshift surveys. We present new redshifts for roughly half of the SN host galaxies, most of which are low-luminosity dwarfs similar to the Magellanic Clouds ($M_r \gtrsim -18$ mag). This set of transient spectra, light curves, luminosities, redshifts, and host galaxies offers an excellent testbed for real-time photometric/light curve classification pipelines in the modern era of deep and large-area surveys. We conclude with a brief discussion of the provided data products and status of the SCAT survey.

AI.tldr: The SCAT survey's first data release (DR1) includes 1810 spectra of 1330 transients, categorized into supernovae, galactic nucleus transients, and stellar variability. The data, spanning five years, provides light curves, host galaxy information, and redshifts, useful for improving photometric classification in large surveys.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive dataset for improving real-time classification pipelines in modern astronomical surveys, particularly for redshift determination and understanding extragalactic transient properties.
AI.method: Observed 1330 transients over five years, collecting spectra and multi-filter light curves. Spectra were classified into broad groups. Light curves were modeled to estimate peak brightness and explosion time. Host galaxies were matched and analyzed for luminosity and offsets. Redshifts from SNe supplemented those of nearby galaxies.
AI.result: DR1 includes 1810 spectra and new redshifts for ~50% of SN host galaxies, mostly low-luminosity dwarfs. SNe help augment redshift coverage for nearby galaxies. The dataset serves as a testbed for photometric classification.
AI.conclusion: The SCAT survey's data products enhance transient classification and galaxy redshift surveys, particularly for low-luminosity galaxies. The ongoing survey will continue to contribute to modern, large-scale astronomical surveys.

title: On gravitating dyonic configurations in nonlinear electrodynamics
summary: We consider static, spherically symmetric configurations of nonlinear electromagnetic fields with Lagrangians $L(f)$, where $f = F_{μν} F^{μν}$, in general relativity (GR) and other metric theories of gravity. The corresponding exact solutions are well known in the framework of GR in cases where only an electric charge ($q_e$) or a magnetic charge ($q_m$) are present, but only a few solutions in particular examples of $L(f)$ are known for dyonic systems with both nonzero $q_e$ and $q_m$. We study the properties of such systems in the special case of equal electric and magnetic charges and, assuming a correct Maxwell limit of $L(f)$, show that there always exists such a configuration of the electromagnetic field that the invariant $f$ is zero in the whole space. It leads to the existence of the corresponding families of solutions both in GR in the presence of other sources of gravity (like fluids or scalar fields) and in a wide range of extended theories of gravity (e.g., scalar-tensor and $F(R)$ gravity), in which the nonlinear electromagnetic field behaves in an especially simple manner.

AI.tldr: The paper examines dyonic systems with equal electric and magnetic charges in metric theories of gravity, showing that nonlinear electromagnetic fields with $f=0$ everywhere can exist, leading to simple solutions in GR and extended gravity theories.
AI.motivation: To explore exact solutions for dyonic systems in various gravity theories where both charges are present, as existing solutions are limited compared to cases with single charges.
AI.method: Analyzes spherically symmetric configurations of $L(f)$ electromagnetic fields, focusing on equal $q_e$ and $q_m$. Derives conditions for $f=0$ across space, ensuring the Maxwell limit is respected.
AI.result: 发现了在广义相对论和扩展引力理论中，当电荷相等时，电磁场不变量$f$在空间各处为零的解存在，这简化了场方程的行为。
AI.conclusion: Such solutions provide a universal framework for studying dyonic systems across different gravitational theories, highlighting the simplifying role of equal charges in nonlinear electrodynamics.

title: The Microchannel X-ray Telescope on board the SVOM mission: in-flight scientific performance
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 11 pages, 10 figures
summary: The Microchannel X-ray Telescope (MXT) is a compact and lightweight focusing X-ray telescope, which is part of the space payload of the SVOM mission. The main goal of the MXT instrument is to precisely localize and physically characterize the early phases of the X-ray afterglows detected by the SVOM ECLAIRs coded mask telescope after a satellite slew. The MXT is composed by a "Lobster-Eye" type optics, with a 58$\times$58 arcmin$^{2}$ field of view, based on micro-pores of 40 $μ$m side. This innovative type of optics is coupled to an X-ray camera, which implements at its focal plane a low-noise pnCCD. The MXT system is completed by an onboard calculator, able to command the whole telescope and to analyze in real time the MXT data stream and hence to localize the sources within the MXT field of view. In this paper, we present the MXT design and in-flight performance, as measured during the SVOM Commissioning and early science operation phase. In particular, we will focus on the optical and spectral performances, the in flight localization capabilities, and how these compare with the pre-flight ground measurements.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the in-flight performance of the Microchannel X-ray Telescope (MXT) aboard the SVOM mission, focusing on its optical, spectral, and localization capabilities compared to pre-flight expectations.
AI.motivation: To evaluate how well MXT meets its objectives of precisely localizing and characterizing X-ray afterglows post-satellite slew, ensuring effective operation during the SVOM mission.
AI.method: Presentation of MXT design coupled with in-flight performance data collection during SVOM's Commissioning and early science phases. Comparisons between in-flight results and pre-flight ground-based measurements.
AI.result: Confirmation of MXT's performance aligning with design specifications, particularly in optical/spectral quality and source localization accuracy, validating its role in SVOM's observational goals.
AI.conclusion: MXT successfully achieves its mission requirements, providing reliable data for studying X-ray afterglows, with in-flight performance matching or exceeding ground tests, thus confirming its effectiveness in space operations.

title: Probing the electron Yukawa coupling via resonant Higgs boson production at FCC-ee via $e^+e^- \to H \to WW^*$ in lepton-plus-jets final states
summary: We report a detailed simulation study of the search for $s$-channel Higgs boson production in $e^+e^-$ collisions at a center-of-mass (c.m.) energy of $\sqrt{s}=125\,\mathrm{GeV}$ at the CERN Future Circular Collider (FCC-ee), as a means to constrain the electron Yukawa coupling, $y_e$. The process of interest is $e^+e^-\to H\to WW^*\to \ell^\pmν+ jj$ with four different $WW^*$ final states considered, involving both on- and off-shell $W$ bosons decaying either into dileptons ($\ell^\pm = e^\pm$ and $μ^\pm$, including those from $τ^\pm$ decays) or into dijets ($jj$). Signal and background events are discriminated through a multiclass gradient boosted decision tree exploiting a comprehensive set of kinematic and topological variables across the four final-state categories. Assuming a monochromatized c.m. energy spread of 4.1 MeV, yielding a $σ_{e^+e^-\to H} = 280\,\mathrm{ab}$ resonant cross section, and an integrated luminosity of $10\,\mathrm{ab}^{-1}$, the analysis achieves a combined statistical significance of 2.0 standard deviations. This corresponds to an upper limit on the coupling modifier $κ_e = y_e/y_e^{\rm SM} \lesssim 1.35$ at 95\% confidence level, and provides the most stringent constraint on the electron Yukawa coupling achieved in simulation-based studies to date.

AI.tldr: The study proposes a simulation-based approach to constrain the electron Yukawa coupling (κ_e) using s-channel Higgs boson production in e+e− collisions at the FCC-ee collider. With a statistical significance of 2.0σ, it sets an upper limit of κ_e ≈ 1.35 at 95% CL.
AI.motivation: To precisely measure the electron Yukawa coupling, which is crucial for understanding the Higgs mechanism and testing the Standard Model's predictions. Current experiments have not yet constrained this coupling stringently.
AI.method: The analysis uses multiclass gradient boosted decision trees to distinguish signal (e+e−→H→WW*→ℓ±ν +jj) from backgrounds across four WW* decay modes. Input variables include kinematic and topological features, leveraging a 125 GeV monochromatic beam with 280 ab resonant cross-section and 10 ab⁻¹ integrated luminosity.
AI.result: A 2.0σ statistical significance was achieved, yielding κ_e < 1.35 (95% CL). This represents the tightest simulation-based constraint on κ_e to date.
AI.conclusion: The FCC-ee's potential to probe fundamental couplings like κ_e is demonstrated. This method advances Higgs coupling measurements and strengthens tests of the Standard Model's validity.

title: Alfven-winged pulsar
summary: Detecting possible electromagnetic precursors to the gravitational signal from merging compact objects is challenging, but it can reveal intricate physical properties of the merging stars through their gravitational and electromagnetic interactions. We demonstrate, using 3D Particle-In-Cell simulations, that a neutron star moving through the magnetosphere of a merging companion generates a complicated system of dissipative currents, a relativistic analogue of planetary Alfven wings. Generated electric currents carry a large fraction of the electromagnetic power intersected by the neutron star. These currents may lead to the generation of beamed, pulsar-like coherent radio and high-energy emission. Orbital modulation will produce a nearly periodic signal, an Alfven-winged pulsar.

AI.tldr: The study explores electromagnetic precursors to gravitational signals from merging neutron stars using 3D simulations, revealing that a neutron star moving through its companion's magnetosphere creates dissipative currents, leading to potential beamed electromagnetic emissions detectable as an 'Alfven-winged pulsar'.
AI.motivation: To detect electromagnetic precursors of gravitational wave events and understand the physical interactions between merging compact objects through their electromagnetic and gravitational interactions.
AI.method: 3D Particle-In-Cell (PIC) simulations were employed to model the interaction between a neutron star and its companion's magnetosphere, analyzing current systems and resulting electromagnetic emissions.
AI.result: Simulations show that the neutron star generates dissipative currents forming a relativistic analogue of planetary Alfven wings, which channel significant electromagnetic power into beamed radio and high-energy emissions, modulated orbitaly to create a periodic signal.
AI.conclusion: The discovery of these Alfven-winged pulsar signatures could provide observational evidence for electromagnetic interactions during neutron star mergers and offer insights into their physical properties.

title: From Big Bang Nucleosynthesis to Late-Time Acceleration in $f(Q,L_m)$ Gravity
summary: We perform a comprehensive investigation of the early-to-late time cosmic evolution within the framework of $f(Q,L_m)$ gravity, characterized by a non-minimal coupling between non-metricity and matter. The model is further tested against a combined set of observational data, including DESI DR2 BAO, previous BAO measurements, cosmic chronometers (CC), and gravitational-wave (GW) standard sirens, using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach. Further by incorporating the Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) freeze-out constraint, we place stringent limits on the model parameters, ensuring consistency with early-Universe physics. The resulting constraints exhibit strong agreement with observations, with the model successfully describing the transition from decelerated to accelerated expansion. The evolution of the effective equation-of-state parameter, together with statefinder diagnostics and energy conditions, reveals a quintessence-like nature and confirms the physical viability of the model. Overall, the $f(Q,L_m)$ framework emerges as a viable alternative to $Λ$CDM, closely reproducing its predictions while allowing controlled deviations in the expansion history.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates early-to-late cosmic evolution in f(Q,Lm) gravity with non-metricity-matter coupling, testing against observational data and confirming its viability as a ΛCDM alternative.
AI.motivation: To explore an alternative gravity model that can explain cosmic acceleration and transitions without dark energy, while maintaining consistency with early-Universe physics like BBN.
AI.method: Combined observational datasets (DESI DR2 BAO, previous BAO, CC, GW standard sirens) with MCMC analysis, incorporating BBN constraints to limit model parameters.
AI.result: The model aligns well with observations, showing quintessence-like dynamics and viable expansion history. It matches ΛCDM predictions but allows controlled deviations.
AI.conclusion: f(Q,Lm) gravity provides a physically viable alternative to ΛCDM, successfully describing cosmic expansion transitions and passing stringent observational tests.

title: The data analysis pipeline for the Microchannel X-ray Telescope on board the SVOM mission
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 10 pages, 5 figures
summary: The Space-based multi-band astronomical Variable Objects Monitor (SVOM) mission was launched in June 2024. It is a joint Sino-French collaboration designed to detect, localize, and study gammaray bursts (GRBs) and other high-energy transients. Among its onboard instruments, the Microchannel Xray Telescope (MXT) plays a central role by providing follow-up X-ray observations of GRB afterglows and other transient phenomena. To ensure timely and accurate scientific exploitation of MXT observations, a dedicated ground processing pipeline has been developed. This pipeline automatically ingests raw event lists, performs calibration, background and time filtering, corrects instrumental effects, and produces science-ready data products such as images and light curves and spectra of detected sources. In this paper, we describe the architecture and key components of the MXT data analysis pipeline, highlighting its modular design and integration within the broader SVOM ground segment. We also show results from real datasets, demonstrating the pipeline's ability to meet the performance requirements of the mission.

AI.tldr: This paper details the development and validation of the Microchannel X-ray Telescope (MXT) data analysis pipeline for the SVOM mission, emphasizing its modular design, processing capabilities, and successful performance with real data.
AI.motivation: Ensure timely and accurate scientific exploitation of MXT observations by automating data processing tasks such as calibration and filtering.
AI.method: The pipeline ingests raw event lists, applies calibration, background/time filtering, instrumental corrections, and generates science-ready products like images, light curves, and spectra. Real datasets were used to validate performance.
AI.result: Results from real data demonstrate the pipeline meets SVOM's performance requirements, effectively processing MXT observations for GRB afterglow and transient studies.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline successfully fulfills mission goals, enabling efficient scientific analysis of MXT data through its modular architecture integrated into the SVOM ground system.

title: Selected Topics in Quark-Hadron Physics: From Scalar Nonets to Topological Glueballs
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, prepared for the proceedings of the 66th Cracow School of Theoretical Physics "Physics of Strong Interactions under Extreme Conditions"
summary: This contribution reviews recent progress in the low-lying scalar mesons and glueballs. We propose a new classification for the scalar nonet that includes $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$ as the lowest states, while we identify $f_0(1500)$ as a primary glueball candidate. We demonstrate that the production yields of these states in heavy-ion collisions are mutually consistent across statistical, coalescence, and S-matrix frameworks. To investigate their internal structure, we move beyond standard phenomenology by describing glueballs as topological solitons. This approach yields an energy spectrum in excellent agreement with lattice QCD and experimental data, while interpreting $f_0(2470)$ as a tightly bound glueballonium to explain its anomalously long lifetime. This non-perturbative framework provides a predictive basis for the future experimental verification of exotic scalar states.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new classification of scalar mesons and glueballs, identifies possible glueball candidates, and uses a novel topological soliton approach to explain their properties and lifetimes.
AI.motivation: To address the classification of low-lying scalar mesons and glueballs, resolve inconsistencies in production yields across different frameworks, and provide a non-perturbative explanation for their internal structures and experimental observations.
AI.method: The authors propose a new scalar nonet classification with $f_0(980)$, $a_0(980)$, and $f_0(1500)$ as key states. They analyze production yields across statistical, coalescence, and S-matrix models. A topological soliton model is applied to describe glueballs and predict their energy spectra and lifetimes.
AI.result: The proposed classification aligns production data across frameworks. The topological soliton method matches lattice QCD and experimental results, especially interpreting $f_0(2470)$'s long lifetime as a tightly bound glueballonium.
AI.conclusion: This framework offers a predictive tool for future experiments to verify exotic scalar states, bridging theoretical models and experimental data in understanding non-perturbative QCD phenomena.

title: GRRMHD Simulations of State Transitions in Non-Jetted Tidal Disruption Events
summary: Circularization of the stream material into a debris cloud during tidal disruption events (TDEs) was recently demonstrated in one of the most accurate long duration TDE simulations to-date. The cooling envelope model (CEM) provides a description of the circularized debris cloud and its emission over time well beyond circularization across different disruption parameters. In the CEM, sub-Eddington accretion rates occur early in TDEs and the debris has a shallow density profile of roughly $ρ\propto r^{-1}$, with Eddington accretion only being achieved after several months. To explore the late stages of the CEM, we perform general relativistic radiation magnetohydrodynamics (GRRMHD) simulations of magnetized tori adapted from the near Eddington phase of the CEM for a $1M_\odot$ star disrupted around a $10^7 M_\odot$ black hole (BH). We find that the disk becomes thermally unstable within 17.1-46.5 days depending on the spin of the BH. Thermal spectra show a soft X-ray excess prior to collapse, with a nearly two order of magnitude decline in X-ray luminosity upon disk collapse. Furthermore, the evolution of the blackbody radius and temperature of our models are correlated with the spin of the black hole. The spectral properties and soft X-ray luminosity in our models are similar to the TDE AT2021ehb, which is a non-jetted TDE with late X-rays and a state transition after $\approx 271$ days.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the late stages of tidal disruption events (TDEs) using the cooling envelope model (CEM) and GRRMHD simulations, revealing thermal instability in magnetized accretion disks, X-ray luminosity changes, and correlations with black hole spin, with similarities to the TDE AT2021ehb.
AI.motivation: To explore the late stages of TDEs beyond circularization, understand thermal stability in accretion disks, and connect simulation results to observed phenomena like AT2021ehb.
AI.method: Conducted general relativistic radiation magnetohydrodynamics (GRRMHD) simulations of magnetized tori derived from the near Eddington phase of the CEM for a 1M☉ star disrupted around a 10⁷M☉ BH, varying black hole spin parameters.
AI.result: Disks exhibit thermal instability within 17.1-46.5 days post-disruption depending on BH spin; X-ray luminosity drops by two orders of magnitude during collapse, with a soft X-ray excess pre-collapse. Blackbody radius and temperature correlate with BH spin.
AI.conclusion: Thermal instability in accretion disks during TDE late stages explains observed X-ray dimming and spectral changes. The findings align with observations of AT2021ehb, supporting the CEM framework and highlighting BH spin's role in TDE evolution.

title: Relative velocity in special relativity and quantum field theory
summary: A derivation of the relative velocity used in the definition of the relativistic cross-section is given in terms of manifestly Lorentz invariant quantities. Along the way we find that there is a certain arbitrariness in the usual definition of cross-section.

AI.tldr: The paper derives the relativistic cross-section using manifestly Lorentz invariant quantities and identifies an arbitrariness in the conventional cross-section definition.
AI.motivation: To provide a clearer, invariant-based derivation of the relativistic cross-section and address ambiguities in its traditional formulation.
AI.method: The authors express the relative velocity in terms of Lorentz invariant quantities, redefining the cross-section to eliminate arbitrariness from standard approaches.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the conventional cross-section definition contains inherent arbitrariness, offering an invariant-based alternative for more rigorous calculations.
AI.conclusion: A revised framework using invariant quantities improves the theoretical foundation of relativistic cross-sections, resolving ambiguities in earlier methodologies.

title: The SVOM French Science Center Infrastructure
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 13 pages, 13 figures
summary: At the heart of the SVOM French ground segment, the French Science Center is a cloud-based platform which provides services and tools for the management, storage, scientific processing and visualization of SVOM data for the French community. This digital center is a critical node of the SVOM system since it is the single point of access to SVOM scientific data for the French community and the only component of the ground system connected to the SVOM VHF network allowing near-time communication from the satellite. Scientific processing pipelines are fully integrated into its infrastructure, allowing the automated production of high-level scientific data and the generation and broadcast of alerts to the scientific community at large. The software components of the French Science Center are running 24/7 and thus require a high level of automation, which led to the development of dedicated software relying on modern technical solutions such as micro-services, application containerization, infrastructure-as-code and continuous integration and deployment. In this paper, we describe the FSC infrastructure design, technological choices, and the process of SVOM data ingestion, archiving, and automated processing by the FSC scientific pipelines. We present analysis on the FSC performances in terms of availability, amount of data processed as well as processing speed.

AI.tldr: The French Science Center (FSC), a cloud-based platform for the SVOM mission, manages data storage, processing, and dissemination for the French scientific community, using modern tech like microservices and containerization to ensure 24/7 operation.
AI.motivation: To provide an automated, high-availability system for handling SVOM satellite data efficiently, enabling rapid scientific processing and alert distribution while serving as the sole access point for French researchers.
AI.method: The FSC employs microservices, containerization, infrastructure-as-code, and CI/CD pipelines to automate data ingestion, archiving, and processing. It integrates scientific pipelines for high-level data production and alert broadcasting via the SVOM VHF network.
AI.result: The system ensures continuous operation with quantifiable performance metrics on availability, data volume processed, and processing speed, validating its effectiveness for real-time scientific workflows.
AI.conclusion: The FSC's design demonstrates the successful application of modern DevOps practices in managing large-scale astronomical data, setting a model for future space mission infrastructure.

title: How to understand $ρ$ Resonance from the Quark Model and $ππ$ $P$-wave phase shift
summary: As the lightest isovector vector meson, the $ρ$ meson is an important object for investigating the structure of resonant states in strong interactions. Owing to its strong coupling to the $ππ$ channel and its large decay width, the conventional constituent quark model treatment, in which it is simply regarded as a pure $q\bar q$ bound state while the hadronic-channel coupling effects are neglected, is insufficient to fully characterize its physical properties. To this end, in the present work we establish a unified framework for studying the structure and resonant properties of the $ρ$ meson by combining the quark-gluon and hadronic degrees of freedom. At the quark-gluon level, we first determine the parameters of the chiral quark model by refitting a set of narrow mesons for which open OZI-allowed strong-decay channels are absent or strongly suppressed. With these parameters fixed, the bare mass of the $ρ$ meson is obtained and used as the input for the subsequent hadronic-level analysis. At the hadronic level, based on inverse scattering theory, we construct a model including the coupling between the bare state and the $ππ$ continuum, extract the $ρ_0-ππ$ interaction using the $P$-wave $ππ$ scattering phase-shift data, and further calculate the width of the $ρ$ meson as well as the bare-state component in the physical state. The present work also provides a generalizable analytical framework for further studies of other hadronic resonances with significant channel-coupling effects.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified framework combining quark-gluon and hadronic degrees of freedom to study the ρ meson's structure and resonance properties, addressing its strong ππ channel coupling and large decay width, which conventional quark models fail to capture adequately.
AI.motivation: The ρ meson's properties, such as strong ππ coupling and large decay width, cannot be fully explained by traditional constituent quark models that ignore hadronic channel effects. A new approach is needed to accurately characterize its resonant behavior.
AI.method: 1. Use a chiral quark model with refitted parameters (avoiding OZI-allowed decay channels) to determine the bare ρ meson mass. 2. Apply inverse scattering theory at the hadronic level to model ρ-ππ interactions, incorporating ππ scattering phase-shift data. 3. Calculate the ρ meson width and bare-state contribution in the physical state.
AI.result: The framework successfully incorporates both quark-gluon and hadronic components, providing accurate calculations of the ρ meson's width and bare-state contribution. It establishes a generalizable method for studying other hadronic resonances with strong channel coupling.
AI.conclusion: The unified approach effectively addresses the limitations of conventional models by integrating quantum chromodynamics-based quark dynamics with meson-meson interactions. This methodology can be extended to analyze other complex resonant states in strong interactions.

title: SVOM/VT: Instrument Overview, Science Objectives, and First-Year Performance
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 30 pages, 23 figures
summary: The 44-cm Visible Telescope (VT) aboard the Space-based Variable Objects Monitor (SVOM) is a dual-band (400-650 nm and 650-1000 nm) instrument designed to detect and characterize the optical counterparts of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and other high-energy transients. This paper presents the VT's design, scientific objectives, observing strategies, and both space- and ground-based data processing pipelines, along with its first-year in-orbit performance. In-orbit commissioning tests confirm a sensitivity of 22.5 AB mag (300 s exposure), extendable to $\sim\!24$ AB mag through stacking. This performance enables the VT to monitor over 100 GRBs in its first year with an exceptional $\sim\!80\%$ detection rate for \textit{SVOM}/ECLAIRS-triggered bursts and ToO-observed bursts from other missions (e.g., \textit{Swift, Fermi, Einstein Probe (EP)}), outperforming \textit{Swift}/UVOT's $\sim\!40\%$ detection rate. Beyond its exceptional detection efficiency, the VT played a key role in identifying high-redshift GRBs-most notably GRB 250314A (z = 7.3). Its deep upper limits at long wavelengths (up to 1 $μ$m) were pivotal in guiding follow-up observations with large ground-based telescopes, enabling crucial near-infrared (NIR) detections. With its rapid response, deep sensitivity, and real-time processing capabilities, the VT is a key instrument for GRB research in \textit{SVOM}-era, enabling critical studies of GRB optical afterglows, circumburst environments, relativistic jet dynamics, and the origins of optically dark bursts.

AI.tldr: The 44-cm Visible Telescope (VT) on SVOM is a dual-band instrument designed for detecting and analyzing optical counterparts of gamma-ray bursts. It significantly outperforms previous instruments like Swift's UVOT with an 80% detection rate, enabling the study of high-redshift GRBs and guiding follow-up observations.
AI.motivation: To enhance the detection and characterization of gamma-ray burst optical counterparts, providing deeper sensitivity and real-time processing to advance understanding of GRB afterglows, jet dynamics, and the origins of dark bursts.
AI.method: The VT uses dual-band (400-650 nm and 650-1000 nm) imaging, rapid response mechanisms, and optimized observing strategies. It employs space- and ground-based data pipelines, including stacking techniques to achieve extended sensitivity (up to ~24 AB mag).
AI.result: First-year performance showed a detection rate of ~80% for SVOM/ECLAIRS and ToO-observed bursts, exceeding Swift/UVOT's ~40%. It identified high-redshift GRBs like GRB 250314A (z=7.3) and provided critical NIR guidance through deep upper limits at long wavelengths.
AI.conclusion: The VT is a pivotal tool in the SVOM era, advancing GRB research through its unique capabilities. It enables detailed studies of afterglows, jet dynamics, and the origins of optically dark bursts, bridging space-based detection and ground-based follow-up.

title: Primordial black hole production in scalar field inflation within $f(T)$ gravity
summary: We investigate inflation in modified teleparallel gravity within a scalar-tensor framework. We focus on two viable extensions of the Teleparallel Equivalent of General Relativity: a power-law model and an exponential model, which introduce controlled deviations from standard teleparallel gravity through a correction parameter $α$. Inflation is driven by a string-inspired fiber inflation potential that naturally realizes a transient ultra slow-roll (USR) phase. We solve the background equations numerically and compute the evolution of cosmological perturbations within the modified teleparallel framework. We show that both models generate an amplification of the primordial curvature power spectrum on small scales due to the USR phase, while remaining compatible with cosmic microwave background constraints at large scales. The modified gravity sector introduces corrections to the slow-roll parameters, tensor spectral index, and tensor-to-scalar ratio through derivatives of the torsion function, leading to potentially observable signatures distinct from canonical inflation. We further analyze the implications of enhanced scalar perturbations for primordial black hole (PBH) formation and demonstrate that modified teleparallel gravity provides a theoretically consistent and phenomenologically rich framework for producing PBHs during inflation.

AI.tldr: The paper explores inflation in modified teleparallel gravity using scalar-tensor frameworks with power-law and exponential models, introducing a correction parameter α. It analyzes how a USR phase drives enhanced curvature power spectra on small scales while aligning with CMB data. Modified gravity effects alter inflationary parameters, offering unique signatures and potential PBH formation pathways.
AI.motivation: To investigate how modified teleparallel gravity extensions can explain inflationary mechanisms with transient ultra slow-roll phases, addressing limitations in standard models while maintaining consistency with observational data like CMB constraints.
AI.method: Numerical solutions of background equations and analysis of cosmological perturbations in two modified teleparallel models (power-law and exponential). They use a string-inspired fiber inflation potential and compute effects on scalar/tensor perturbations via torsion function derivatives.
AI.result: Both models produce amplified small-scale curvature spectra from USR phases without violating large-scale CMB constraints. Modified gravity introduces measurable differences in slow-roll parameters and tensor modes, enhancing PBH formation potential through magnified scalar perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Modified teleparallel gravity offers a viable and observationally distinct framework for inflation and PBH formation, producing testable signatures in cosmological observables that could distinguish it from standard models.

title: SVOM Science User Support Services at Chinese Science Center
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics, 14 pages, 12 figures
summary: The Chinese-French SVOM (Space-based Multi-band Astronomical Variable Objects Monitor) mission is dedicated to the study of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) from the distant universe. A key component of the SVOM Chinese Ground Segment, the Science User Support Services (SUSS) provides comprehensive support for the mission's scientific operations. SUSS consists of two integral pillars: a suite of specialized software tools that automate key workflows, and a dedicated User Support Team that delivers expert-led, human services. These human-delivered services include operational coordination across telescope networks, direct technical assistance to astronomers, user training, and proactive problem-solving throughout the observation lifecycle. This paper focuses on the organization of SVOM scientific operations and the role of SUSS in facilitating these tasks. We provide a detailed description of the SUSS software architecture and its functionalities, encompassing the General Platform, the Burst Advocate (BA) support tools for GRB counterpart identification, the Target of Opportunity (ToO) support tools, and the General Program (GP) support tools. The structure and services provided by the user support team at the Chinese Science Center (CSC) are also elaborated. Furthermore, we evaluate the performance of SUSS during its first operational year, assessing its effectiveness in fulfilling user requirements. The evaluation offers valuable insights to guide future user support strategies and software enhancements, ultimately enabling better service for the SVOM scientific community.

AI.tldr: The paper describes the Science User Support Services (SUSS) within the SVOM mission, detailing its software tools and user support team, and evaluates its performance in the first operational year.
AI.motivation: To provide comprehensive support for the scientific operations of the SVOM mission dedicated to studying gamma-ray bursts, ensuring effective coordination and service delivery to astronomers.
AI.method: The paper outlines the structure of SUSS, including its software components (General Platform, Burst Advocate tools, Target of Opportunity tools, General Program tools) and the roles of the user support team, followed by an evaluation of its first-year performance.
AI.result: SUSS demonstrated effective support through automated workflows and human services, fulfilling user requirements and providing insights for future improvements.
AI.conclusion: SUSS is critical for SVOM's success, and the evaluation highlights areas for enhancing software and services to better meet the needs of the scientific community.

title: Role of heavy neutral lepton in lepton number violating $B$ meson decays
summary: We study the phenomenology of heavy neutral leptons (HNLs) in $B$-meson decays as probes of physics beyond the Standard Model. Focusing on the leptonic channels $B \to μN$ and $B \to τN$, we constrain the allowed regions in the $M_N$--$|U_{\ell N}|^2$ plane using current experimental data. Using these constraints, we investigate lepton-number violating ($ΔL=2$) processes mediated by on-shell HNLs, including $B_{(c)}^- \to π^+ μ^- μ^-$ and $B_c^- \to J/ψ\, π^+ μ^- μ^-$. For benchmark values $|U_{μN}|^2 = 10^{-6}$ and $M_N = 2$-- $3\,\mathrm{GeV}$, the predicted branching ratios lie in the range $\mathcal{O}(10^{-13})$--$\mathcal{O}(10^{-8})$. Among the channels, $B_c^- \to π^+ μ^- μ^-$ shows the largest enhancement, while $B_c^- \to J/ψ\, π^+ μ^- μ^-$ is strongly suppressed. These results indicate a clear channel dependence, with $B_c$ modes providing enhanced sensitivity to HNL effects and offering promising avenues for future searches of lepton number violation.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates heavy neutral leptons (HNLs) in B-meson decays as probes for physics beyond the Standard Model, focusing on lepton-number violating processes. Constraints from current data and branching ratios for specific decay channels highlight the potential of B_c modes to detect HNL effects.
AI.motivation: To explore physics beyond the Standard Model (BSM) and test lepton number violation (ΔL=2) via HNL-mediated decays. Current experiments lack sensitivity, requiring theoretical constraints and channel-specific predictions.
AI.method: Analyzed B → μN and B → τN decays to constrain HNL parameter space (M_N vs. |U_{ℓN}|²). Calculated branching ratios for B_{(c)}^- → π⁺μ⁻μ⁻ and B_c^- → J/ψπ⁺μ⁻μ⁻ using benchmark parameters |U_{μN}|² = 1e-6 and M_N = 2-3 GeV.
AI.result: Predicted branching ratios span O(1e-13) to O(1e-8). B_c→π⁺μ⁻μ⁻ shows strongest HNL signal, while B_c→J/ψπ⁺μ⁻μ⁻ is suppressed. Sensitivity varies significantly between channels.
AI.conclusion: B_c decay channels offer enhanced potential for detecting lepton number violation via HNLs. Future experiments should prioritize these modes to improve BSM physics discovery prospects.

title: ECLAIRs: the SVOM high-energy transient trigger camera
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 14 pages; 14 figures
summary: The core instrument of the SVOM Gamma-ray burst mission launched in June 2024 is the 4-150 keV 2-D coded mask camera ECLAIRs responsible for the autonomous trigger and localization of transient events within its field of view. The flight model of ECLAIRs has been built by several French labs (IRAP, CEA, APC) under the supervision of the French Space Agency (CNES), while APC, LUPM and IAP built a suite of data reduction and analysis software. This paper outlines the main science goals of ECLAIRs and describes the different instrument sub-systems and their main characteristics. The paper then discusses the instrument configuration and operation as well as the main in-flight measured performances. Finally, the paper summarizes the science performance of ECLAIRs up to March 31, 2025.

AI.tldr: The paper details the ECLAIRs instrument on the SVOM mission, covering its design, in-flight performance, and scientific achievements up to March 2025.
AI.motivation: To present the capabilities and validation of ECLAIRs, ensuring its efficacy in detecting and localizing Gamma-ray bursts and other transient events.
AI.method: Describes ECLAIRs' subsystems, operational configurations, and data analysis software developed by collaborating institutions. Discusses in-flight performance measurements and science outcomes.
AI.result: ECLAIRs successfully meets performance specifications, enabling accurate triggering and localization of transient events. Preliminary scientific results up to early 2025 demonstrate its effectiveness in Gamma-ray astronomy.
AI.conclusion: The instrument fulfills its mission goals, providing critical data for studying Gamma-ray bursts and transients, with ongoing contributions to high-energy astrophysics research.

title: $g_{tt}g_{rr} =-1$ black hole thermodynamics in extended quasi-topological gravity
summary: We present a unified framework for the discussion of black hole thermodynamics of $d$-dimensional static black holes with spherical, toroidal or compact hyperbolic horizon topology satisfying $g_{tt}g_{rr}=-1$ in Schwarzschild gauge. To that end, we consider any such black hole as a solution to an integrable $2$-dimensional effective dilaton theory and thereby as a vacuum solution to an extended notion of $d$-dimensional quasi-topological gravity. We show that the generating function determining $f(r) = -g_{tt} $ in the integrated equation of motion provides the thermodynamic mass in a generalised first law with entropy computed as the Wald entropy. The framework presented here can be applied to singular and regular black holes with flat or anti-de Sitter asymptotics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified framework for analyzing black hole thermodynamics across different horizon topologies and asymptotics using an effective 2D dilaton theory and quasi-topological gravity. It derives thermodynamic mass and entropy via a generating function and Wald entropy, applicable to both singular and regular black holes.
AI.motivation: To address the general thermodynamics of diverse d-dimensional static black holes with spherical, toroidal, or hyperbolic horizons under specific gauge conditions, unifying their analysis through a common theoretical approach.
AI.method: The framework models black holes as solutions to an integrable 2D dilaton theory, treating them as vacuum solutions of extended quasi-topological gravity. The generating function for f(r) = -g_tt is used to derive the thermodynamic mass, with entropy calculated via Wald's formula.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the proposed framework successfully unifies the thermodynamic description, accommodating various horizon topologies and asymptotics (flat/AdS), and applies to both singular and regular black holes.
AI.conclusion: The unified approach provides a versatile tool for studying black hole thermodynamics, emphasizing the role of effective theories and quasi-topological gravity in extending applicability across different spacetime geometries.

title: SVOM/ECLAIRs detection plane: main features and performance
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 8 pages; 8 figures
summary: The detection plane of the high-energy transient camera ECLAIRs onboard SVOM is made of 200 XRDPIX detection modules, each consisting of a matrix of $8\times 4$ Schottky-type CdTe detectors hybridized with the low-noise and low-consumption ASIC IDeF-X.
