The 6400 detectors are operated at -20°C and reverse biased at -300 V, reaching a low energy threshold of 4 keV. The 20 us time resolution readout electronics works in photon counting mode classifying detected events as single or multiple events and measuring the deposited energy in real time. In this paper, we present the detection plane sub-systems and their main features. We also discuss its overall performances as measured both on ground and in-flight, showing compliance with the ECLAIRs science requirements.

AI.tldr: This paper details the design, operation, and performance validation of the ECLAIRs detection plane onboard the SVOM satellite, comprising 200 XRDPIX modules with CdTe detectors and IDeF-X ASICs. It confirms compliance with scientific requirements through ground and in-flight tests.
AI.motivation: To ensure the detection plane meets ECLAIRs' scientific objectives for high-energy transient detection, the authors analyze its subsystems and performance.
AI.method: The study describes the detection modules' architecture, operational parameters (-20°C, -300V bias), and real-time event classification. Performance is evaluated via ground and in-flight measurements.
AI.result: The system achieves a 4 keV energy threshold, 20µs time resolution, and correct classification of single/multiple events, meeting specified requirements.
AI.conclusion: The detection plane's successful in-flight performance validates its readiness for effective high-energy transient observation as part of the SVOM mission.

title: CP violation in $Σ^+\to p\ell^+\ell^-$ within the standard model and beyond
summary: The LHCb collaboration has recently observed the rare hyperon decay $Σ^+\to pμ^+μ^-$. It can also measure the corresponding antihyperon channel with comparable precision and is thus in a position to extract information on $CP$ violation in this mode. Interestingly, the long-distance contributions that dominate it within the standard model provide large absorptive phases that could drive substantial $CP$ violation through interference with potential new-physics contributions. Here we explore this possibility, finding that the decay rate asymmetry is currently allowed to be as high as tens of percent, which can be probed by LHCb in the near future. We additionally consider the same with regard to the dielectron mode $Σ^+\to pe^+e^-$ as well as the related radiative one $Σ^+\to pγ$.

AI.tldr: The LHCb collaboration observed the rare hyperon decay Σ⁺→pμ⁺μ⁻ and can measure the antihyperon channel to study CP violation. The paper explores the potential for large CP violation through interference between standard model contributions and new physics, with decay rate asymmetries potentially reaching tens of percent, detectable by LHCb.
AI.motivation: To investigate CP violation signals in hyperon decays, leveraging the LHCb's ability to measure both particle and antiparticle channels, and explore the possibility of detecting new physics through interference effects with standard model contributions.
AI.method: Analyzing the decay modes Σ⁺→pμ⁺μ⁻, Σ⁺→pe⁺e⁻, and Σ⁺→pγ, considering long-distance standard model contributions with large absorptive phases and their interference with potential new physics contributions to compute CP violation asymmetries.
AI.result: The study finds that CP violation asymmetries in these decays could reach up to tens of percent, within current experimental limits, making them accessible for measurement by LHCb in the near future.
AI.conclusion: These decays provide a sensitive probe for CP violation and new physics, highlighting the importance of forthcoming LHCb measurements to test standard model predictions and uncover potential beyond-standard-model effects.

title: SVOM Science User Support at FSC
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 11 pages, 6 figures
summary: The SVOM mission, a Sino-French collaboration dedicated to Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs) and transient sources, began scientific operations in 2025. This paper describes the ground computing infrastructure and user support tools for SVOM's three observing programs: the Core Program (CP), the General Program (GP), and Targets of Opportunity Program (ToO), the latter two being open to the broader scientific community, provided they collaborate with a mission Co-I. The mission adopts operational roles inspired by Swift, including on-duty scientists such as Burst Advocates (BAs), who validate GRB triggers and coordinate follow-up observations, and Instrument Scientists (IS), who calibrate and validate data for all programs. Users can access observation schedules, public data products, and support tools via the French and Chinese mission centers. The SVOM portal serves as the primary interface for accessing these resources, including a GRB public table, API, and user documentation. This paper serves as a guide for both newcomers and external researchers interested in SVOM scientific operations, focusing on aspects related to the CP.

AI.tldr: This paper outlines the ground computing infrastructure and user support tools for the SVOM mission's three observing programs, emphasizing operational roles like Burst Advocates and access via the SVOM portal, particularly focusing on the Core Program.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive guide for scientific operations and user engagement with the SVOM mission, ensuring efficient data access and collaboration for both the Core Program and open programs requiring collaboration with mission Co-Is.
AI.method: The paper describes the infrastructure and tools developed for SVOM, including operational roles modeled after Swift (BAs and IS), data calibration processes, and user access mechanisms through mission centers and the SVOM portal with APIs and documentation.
AI.result: A structured user support system and operational framework is established, enabling data access and coordination for GRB studies through the SVOM mission's programs.
AI.conclusion: The SVOM infrastructure facilitates effective scientific collaboration and data utilization, serving as a resource for researchers participating in its observing programs, especially the Core Program.

title: Photon Surfaces in Higher-Curvature Gravity: Implications for Quasinormal Modes and Gravitational Lensing
summary:
Effective field theory (EFT) provides a systematic framework to describe possible deviations from general relativity through higher-curvature corrections to the gravitational action, capturing low-energy effects of an underlying fundamental theory.
  In this work, we investigate quasinormal modes (QNMs) and both weak and strong gravitational lensing in static, spherically symmetric spacetimes, focusing on the behavior of null geodesics near the photon sphere. Adopting the strong deflection limit formalism developed by Bozza, we derive the logarithmic divergence structure of the deflection angle and explicitly separate the divergent and regular contributions.
  Within a simplified setup with $2M=1$, we analyze how deviations from general relativity, parametrized in an EFT framework, modify key observables such as the photon sphere radius, the critical impact parameter, and the coefficients governing the strong deflection expansion. We show that these quantities encode direct information about higher-curvature corrections to the gravitational action.
  Our results demonstrate that strong-field observables provide a sensitive probe of EFT corrections, and that precision measurements of gravitational lensing and QNM spectra could place constraints on EFT couplings beyond general relativity, offering a novel observational window into quantum gravity-inspired effects.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how higher-curvature corrections from Effective Field Theory (EFT) modify strong-field gravitational observables like quasinormal modes (QNMs) and gravitational lensing, showing these effects can constrain EFT parameters through precision measurements.
AI.motivation: To probe quantum gravity effects through observable deviations in strong-field gravity using EFT, providing a bridge between fundamental theory and astrophysical observations.
AI.method: Used analytical methods with the strong deflection limit (Bozza's approach) to study lensing in curved spacetimes parametrized by EFT corrections. Focused on null geodesics around the photon sphere, calculating modified photon sphere radius, critical impact parameter, and deflection coefficients.
AI.result: Found that photon sphere properties and strong deflection coefficients encode information about EFT higher-curvature terms. EFT deviations significantly alter lensing observables and QNM spectra, enabling potential constraints on EFT parameters.
AI.conclusion: Precision measurements of lensing and QNM spectra can constrain EFT corrections to GR, offering an observational route to test quantum gravity proposals.

title: SVOM Ground Support System
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics;8 pages,11 figures
summary: The Ground Support System (GSS) is a critical component of the Space-based multi-band astronomical Variable Objects Monitor (SVOM) mission's ground segment. Its main tasks include organizing and implementing the operations and management of the SVOM payloads, receiving scientific data, as well as preprocessing and managing the scientific data. To address the specific requirements of the SVOM mission, including specialized mission planning, data reception, status monitoring, and product processing, a dedicated SVOM Ground Support System has been designed based on the integrated architecture of the Chinese Space Science Satellite Project Ground Support System. This system has successfully supported SVOM's launch and on-orbit operations.

AI.tldr: The paper describes the design and implementation of the Ground Support System (GSS) for the SVOM mission, which supports mission operations, data management, and on-orbit activities based on China's space science satellite project architecture.
AI.motivation: The SVOM mission required a specialized GSS to handle unique tasks such as multi-band astronomical observations, data reception, real-time monitoring, and processing of variable object data, which existing systems couldn't fully address.
AI.method: The GSS was developed using an integrated architecture from China's space science satellite projects, incorporating modules for mission planning, data reception, status monitoring, and product processing tailored to SVOM's needs.
AI.result: The system successfully supported SVOM's launch and ongoing operations, effectively managing scientific data and mission operations according to mission requirements.
AI.conclusion: The developed GSS demonstrates effective support for complex space missions, providing a scalable and adaptable model for future astronomical satellite projects.

title: Same-Sign Tetralepton Signature at $μ$TRISTAN
summary: Naturally tiny neutrino masses can be explained by the low scale seesaw with heavy neutral lepton $N$ coupling to the neutrinophilic Higgs doublet $Φ_ν$, which obtains a much smaller vacuum expectation value than the standard Higgs doublet $Φ$. Within this model, the neutrino masses originate from the new Yukawa interaction $y \overline{L}\tildeΦ_νN$. In this paper, we propose the novel same-sign tetralepton signature at the 2 TeV same-sign muon mode $μ^+μ^+$ of $μ$TRISTAN. We investigate two distinct channels of this signature, which are both generated by the Yukawa interaction $y \overline{L}\tildeΦ_νN$. One is from the pair production of charged Higgs $μ^+μ^+\to H^+ H^+\to μ^+N +μ^+ N\to μ^+ μ^+ jj + μ^+ μ^+ jj\to 4μ^+ + 4j$, and the other one is from the single production of charged Higgs $μ^+μ^+ \to μ^+ N H^+ \to μ^+N +μ^+ N\to μ^+ μ^+ jj + μ^+ μ^+ jj\to 4μ^+ + 4j$. We then perform a detailed simulation of this same-sign tetralepton signature, and obtain the promising region at $μ$TRISTAN.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel same-sign tetralepton signature at the 2 TeV same-sign muon mode of μTRISTAN to probe the low-scale seesaw mechanism with neutrino masses from the Yukawa interaction y ar{L}Φ̃_νN.
AI.motivation: To explain tiny neutrino masses via the low-scale seesaw mechanism involving a heavy neutral lepton N and a neutrinophilic Higgs doublet Φ_ν with a small vacuum expectation value, and to explore experimental signatures at upcoming μTRISTAN collider.
AI.method: Proposes and analytically investigates two production channels (charged Higgs pair production and single production) generating same-sign tetralepton signals (4μ⁺ + 4j). Performed detailed Monte Carlo simulations at μTRISTAN to evaluate signal reachability.
AI.result: Identified promising sensitivity regions at μTRISTAN for detecting the proposed same-sign tetralepton signatures, demonstrating feasibility for exploring low-scale seesaw parameters.
AI.conclusion: Same-sign tetralepton channels at μTRISTAN provide a viable path to probe the low-scale seesaw mechanism, opening new experimental avenues to test neutrino mass generation models.

title: The SVOM / ECLAIRs Scientific Analysis Pipeline
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue of Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics on 25 February 2026; 8 pages, 9 figures
summary: This paper reports on the scientific pipeline for the analysis of the ECLAIRs data of the SVOM mission. We describe the overall procedure, the different steps and the main algorithms of the data analysis for this hard X-ray coded mask instrument. The pipeline runs in automatic mode at the science center generating standard products but can also be used off-line with specific choices of parameters. Generated data products and preliminary performances are illustrated along with perspectives for future improvements.

AI.tldr: This paper details the automated data analysis pipeline for the ECLAIRs hard X-ray instrument on the SVOM mission, describing its procedures, algorithms, and capabilities for generating standard products while allowing offline customization.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to establish a standardized yet adaptable data analysis framework for the ECLAIRs instrument to ensure consistent and high-quality scientific outputs from the SVOM mission.
AI.method: The paper outlines the structured pipeline including automatic processing at the science center, specific data analysis steps, algorithms employed for coded mask imaging, and options for parameter adjustments during offline use.
AI.result: The pipeline successfully generates standard data products, demonstrates preliminary performance metrics, and provides flexibility for tailored analyses through adjustable parameters.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline meets current mission requirements, with future enhancements planned to improve performance and expand analytical capabilities.

title: BMS transformed Quantum String Dynamics near a Black Hole
summary: Asymptotic symmetries are expected to leave subtle but physically meaningful imprints on quantum probes of gravity, yet their manifestation in near-horizon dynamics remains incompletely understood. We examine this question for a closed bosonic string propagating in the near-horizon geometry of a five-dimensional Schwarzschild black hole subjected to a generalized Bondi-van der Burg-Metzner-Sachs (BMS) supertranslation. The extended nature of the string makes it especially sensitive to the resulting anisotropic geometric distortions, and this sensitivity appears most clearly in the angular sector of the worldsheet dynamics. Under the gauge and falloff conditions adopted here, the temporal and radial sectors remain unaffected by the supertranslation, while the angular deformation breaks the original SO(4) symmetry of the background. The radial equation is governed by modified Bessel modes, with a nonvanishing radial conserved current, indicating transport-like propagation. Radial squeezing driven by gravity and anisotropic angular spreading induced by supertranslations provide a dynamical realization of string spreading near the horizon. Thus this analysis demonstrates that probe string dynamics encodes nontrivial signatures of BMS-induced deformations, providing a dynamical probe of symmetry structures in higher-dimensional black hole spacetimes.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how a closed bosonic string probe responds to BMS supertranslations near a five-dimensional Schwarzschild black hole horizon, finding that the string's angular dynamics reveal symmetry-breaking deformations and transport-like behavior.
AI.motivation: To explore how asymptotic symmetries (BMS supertranslations) leave measurable imprints on quantum gravity probes, specifically examining their effects in near-horizon regions of higher-dimensional black holes where understanding is lacking.
AI.method: Analyze the dynamics of a closed bosonic string in the near-horizon 5D Schwarzschild geometry subjected to generalized BMS supertranslations. Derive worldsheet equations of motion, identify symmetry breaking in angular sectors, and examine radial/angular deformation patterns through modified Bessel modes and conserved currents.
AI.result: The supertranslation breaks SO(4) symmetry, affecting angular dynamics with anisotropic spreading, while leaving temporal/radial sectors invariant. Radial squeezing and angular deformation show distinct transport phenomena, dynamically demonstrating symmetry structure probing via string dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Probe string dynamics effectively encode BMS-induced geometric deformations, offering a novel dynamical tool to study symmetry structures in higher-dimensional black hole spacetimes through their influence on extended matter fields.

title: COLIBRI (SVOM/FM-GFT): Instrumentation and Performances on the SVOM Alerts
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary: COLIBRI, the French Mexican Ground Followup Telescope (FM GFT) for SVOM, is a 1.3 meter rapid response optical facility specifically developed for prompt, multiband observations of GRB afterglows and for delivering subarcsecond localisations of optical counterparts for detailed followup studies. The telescope operates through a fully automated system that manages the entire workflow, from alert reception to counterpart identification. Commissioning results confirm that the telescope meets design specifications, and this paper presents a comprehensive performance assessment of the capabilities.

AI.tldr: COLIBRI is a French Mexican telescope designed for rapid optical observations of gamma-ray burst afterglows and precise localization of their counterparts, supported by an automated system. The telescope meets its performance specifications as confirmed by commissioning tests.
AI.motivation: To enable prompt, multiband observations of GRB afterglows and provide accurate subarcsecond localisations for detailed follow-up studies as part of the SVOM mission.
AI.method: COLIBRI uses a 1.3m telescope with an automated system handling alerts, observations, and counterpart identification. It employs multiband optical observations and automated workflows.
AI.result: Commissioning results show the telescope meets design specifications, confirming its capability for rapid response and precise localization.
AI.conclusion: COLIBRI is successfully operational and provides vital capabilities for GRB afterglow studies as part of the SVOM ground follow-up system.

title: Double SM-like Higgs Production at future $ e^+ e^- $ colliders in the 3-Higgs Doublet Model under the $ S_{3} $ symmetry
summary: In this paper, we present the production of double SM-like Higgs $ (h) $ at the future electron positron colliders within the context of $ S_{3} $ model with three Higgs doublets (S3-3H) and no CP violation to describe beyond the Standard Model Higgs Physics. We focus first on the numerically allowed parameter space of the model, taking into account theoretical bounds from perturbative unitarity and vacuum stability, as well as by data at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) and the Tevatron. The double Higgs production in the S3-3H model can deviate from the SM predictions up to a few orders of magnitude in both the $ e^+e^- \rightarrow hhZ $ and the $ e^+ e^- \rightarrow h hν_{e} \barν_{e}$ channels. Thus, our findings indicate that the S3-3H model can lead to measurable deviations from the SM predictions of Higgs production at future $ e^+ e^- $ colliders. These results highlight the importance of studies at such colliders for searching physics beyond the Standard Model.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates double SM-like Higgs production in the S3-3H model at future electron-positron colliders, showing significant deviations from SM predictions, highlighting the model's potential for probing beyond the Standard Model physics.
AI.motivation: To explore beyond Standard Model Higgs physics by examining double Higgs production in the S3-3H model, considering theoretically constrained parameter spaces and experimental data.
AI.method: Analyzed numerically allowed parameter space of the S3-3H model under constraints from perturbative unitarity, vacuum stability, and collider data (LHC/Tevatron). Calculated cross-sections for $e^+e^- ightarrow hhZ$ and $e^+ e^- ightarrow h hν_{e}  arν_{e}$ channels.
AI.result: Significant deviations (up to several orders of magnitude) from SM predictions in double Higgs production cross-sections, indicating measurable differences at future electron-positron colliders.
AI.conclusion: Future $e^+e^-$ colliders can test the S3-3H model's predictions, offering a pathway to discover BSM physics through precise measurements of double Higgs production.

title: Overview of the ECLAIRs Trigger for SVOM gamma-ray burst detection
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 10 pages, 11 figures
summary: The French-Chinese SVOM satellite mission (Space-based multi-band astronomical Variable Objects Monitor) was launched in mid-2024, with science objectives focused on the detection and study of astrophysical transient events, primarily Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs). The onboard trigger of the hard X-ray wide-field coded-mask instrument ECLAIRs autonomously detects and localizes GRBs on SVOM and requests automatic spacecraft slews toward these sources, enabling follow-up observations by the onboard narrow field-of-view instruments MXT and VT. The trigger also transmits real-time alerts via the SVOM VHF network to the ground, allowing rapid follow-up campaigns by the broader community, including multiple space- and ground-based facilities. We present an overview of the ECLAIRs trigger, with emphasis on the two trigger algorithms running simultaneously onboard: the Count-Rate Trigger (CRT) and the Image Trigger (IMT), both of which issue alerts for GRBs localized on the sky. The trigger has already detected several notable events, including both classical GRBs and peculiar X-ray-rich GRBs, enabling numerous redshift measurements, including high-redshift bursts.

AI.tldr: The SVOM satellite's ECLAIRs instrument uses dual onboard trigger algorithms (CRT and IMT) to detect and localize Gamma-Ray Bursts, enabling rapid follow-up observations and contributing to significant astrophysical discoveries.
AI.motivation: To enhance the detection and study of astrophysical transients, particularly GRBs, through real-time alerts and coordinated follow-up观测 by global facilities.
AI.method: The ECLAIRs trigger employs two algorithms: Count-Rate Trigger and Image Trigger operating simultaneously to detect GRBs. These trigger the satellite to slew toward sources, activate narrow-field instruments (MXT, VT), and send real-time alerts via VHF network.
AI.result: Successfully detected multiple GRBs, including rare X-ray-rich types and high-redshift events, facilitating redshift measurements and collaborative observational campaigns.
AI.conclusion: The ECLAIRs trigger system effectively achieves its mission objectives, demonstrating valuable capabilities for transient astronomy and advancing understanding of GRBs across wavelengths.

title: Cone hierarchy and the screening of matter by gravity
summary: In a previous paper by some of the authors (Gen. Rel. Grav. 56, 116, 2024), we introduced a novel paradigm with which to understand gravitational phenomena. We called it the Harmonic Background Paradigm (HBP). In this paradigm, gravity amounts to an effective causality deformation with respect to a more fundamental causality, which always encompasses the former through a causal cone hierarchy. In that paper, the fundamental idea was described in detail but fully elaborated only when restricted to the linear gravitational approximation. In this work, we discuss and conjecture how this idea could be extended to the full non-linear regime. We identify a connection between the cone hierarchy and a property of gravity that can be described as a screening mechanism of negative-energy gravitational clouds surrounding (but never overcoming) positive-energy seeds. We illustrate our ideas by applying them to spherically symmetric matter distributions. The paper concludes with a discussion of some key implications and directions for future research, including some remarks beyond General Relativity.

AI.tldr: The authors extend their Harmonic Background Paradigm (HBP) to the non-linear regime of gravity, proposing a screening mechanism involving negative-energy gravitational clouds around positive-energy seeds. They apply this to spherically symmetric matter distributions and discuss implications beyond General Relativity.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to move beyond the linear approximation introduced in their previous work and explore how the HBP can be applied in the non-linear regime of gravitational phenomena, addressing limitations of current paradigms and exploring novel mechanisms like energy screening.
AI.method: The method involves conjecturing a causal cone hierarchy connection to gravitational screening mechanisms, analyzing spherically symmetric systems as test cases, and extrapolating implications for non-linear gravitational effects beyond standard General Relativity frameworks.
AI.result: Key results include identifying a screening mechanism where negative-energy clouds surround positive-energy matter without dominance, and demonstrating applicability to spherical symmetry. They suggest potential resolutions to issues likedark energy/emergy without explicit modifications to Einstein’s equations.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion highlights HBP’s potential to unify gravitational phenomena through causal deformation, suggests it could offer alternatives to dark sector hypotheses, and calls for further exploration in non-linear scenarios and beyond GR formulations.

title: Overview of Ground-based Wide-Angle Cameras array
comment: Accepted fro publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 14 pages, 11 figures
summary: As one of the key ground-based facilities of the Chinese-French SVOM mission, the main scientific objectives of the Ground-based Wide Angle Camera array (GWAC) are to detect prompt optical emission of gamma-ray bursts or other short duration astronomical transients on a second-scale temporal resolution. GWAC is located at Xinglong observatory, China, and consists of 10 mounts and 40 cameras, providing a joint field of view of about 3600 square degrees.The detection ability is 16 magnitude in 10 seconds of exposure time in the visual band under the condition of the new moon phase. Here, we give an overview of GWAC and introduce the science motivation of the project, as well as the performance of the hardware and the software. The observation strategies and the data processing are briefly presented. The early sciences in the last 5 years since the first light are summarized.

AI.tldr: GWAC, part of the Chinese-French SVOM mission, is a ground-based facility designed to detect short-duration astronomical events like gamma-ray bursts with high temporal resolution. Located in China, it uses 10 mounts and 40 cameras to monitor a wide field of view, achieving 16th magnitude sensitivity in 10-second exposures under dark conditions.
AI.motivation: The primary motivation is to detect prompt optical emissions from gamma-ray bursts and other transient astronomical events requiring second-scale time resolution, enhancing our understanding of these phenomena through coordinated observations with space-based SVOM instruments.
AI.method: GWAC employs an array of 10 mounts and 40 cameras for a 3600 square-degree field of view. It operates with high temporal resolution (second-scale) and uses advanced hardware/software systems for real-time detection, rapid data processing, and precise sky localization.
AI.result: GWAC has demonstrated detection capabilities up to 16th magnitude in 10-second exposures under new moon conditions. Early science results over five years include successful monitoring of astronomical transients and validation of its observational and data processing strategies.
AI.conclusion: GWAC's performance confirms its effectiveness for studying fast-changing astrophysical events. Its early scientific achievements highlight its critical role in the SVOM mission, enabling coordinated multi-wavelength observations and advancing transient astronomy research.

title: Energy spectrum of magnetic fields from electroweak symmetry breaking
summary: We study the magnetic fields produced in the early Universe during the electroweak symmetry breaking by considering random configurations of an inhomogeneous Higgs field. By exploiting the inherent randomness of the initial configurations the spectrum of the produced magnetic field is essentially analytic, which bypasses the need for costly lattice simulations. On the numerical side, we devise a simulation framework which results in continuous fields capable of resolving the small-scale structure of the fields that was inaccessible for the lattice-based calculation. Finally, by revisiting the effects of statistical isotropy and causality on the spectrum, we define general correlation functions that are then fitted to the simulation data and compared to the analytic results.

AI.tldr: The paper explores early Universe magnetic field generation via electroweak symmetry breaking, using random Higgs field configurations to produce an analytic magnetic field spectrum. A new simulation framework resolves small-scale structures better than lattice methods, and correlation functions are developed to compare simulations with analytic results.
AI.motivation: To understand magnetic field formation in the early Universe during electroweak phase transition, avoiding costly lattice simulations by using analytic approaches and improved numerical methods for small-scale resolution.
AI.method: Analytic calculations based on random inhomogeneous Higgs field configurations and a novel simulation framework generating continuous fields to capture small-scale structures. General correlation functions accounting for statistical isotropy and causality are derived and fitted to simulation data.
AI.result: Successful development of an analytic spectrum for magnetic fields, validated against simulations showing improved resolution. Correlation functions align well with both analytic predictions and simulation data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively combines analytic and numerical approaches to study early Universe magnetic fields, offering efficient and accurate models that overcome limitations of traditional lattice-based methods.

title: SVOM/VT: Preliminary Calibration Analysis
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 11 pages, 10 figures
summary: We present the in-orbit calibration of the Visible Telescope (VT), one of the key instruments aboard the Space Variable Objects Monitor (SVOM) mission for gamma-ray burst (GRB) studies. Using Gaia Data Release 3 (DR3) as a reference, the VT achieves an astrometric precision better than 0.03'' for bright stars, degrading to ~0.25'' for faint targets. Shortly after launch, contamination was detected, reducing system transmission by ~40%. An initial bake-out successfully restored performance, but gradual recontamination caused transmission to decline by ~20% over the following 100 days before stabilizing. Despite this effect, routine standard star observations maintain precise zero-point calibration, ensuring a photometric stability of 0.02 mag. Using synthetic stellar spectra, we derived photometric transformations to the Gaia, SDSS, and Johnson-Cousins systems with typical residuals of 0.03 mag. These results demonstrate the VT system's capability and reliability in calibrating GRBs and other transients.

AI.tldr: The paper details the in-orbit calibration of SVOM's Visible Telescope (VT), demonstrating its astrometric and photometric capabilities despite contamiationException effects, ensuring reliable GRB calibration.
AI.motivation: To calibrate and validate the VT instrument aboard SVOM for accurate gamma-ray burst studies, addressing contamination issues post-launch to ensure long-term performance stability.
AI.method: Calibration using Gaia DR3 reference data, contamination monitoring, bake-out restoration, photometric transformations derived via synthetic stellar spectra to Gaia/SDSS/Johnson-Cousins systems.
AI.result: VT achieved 0.03'' astrometry for bright stars, 0.02 mag photometric stability despite 40% initial contamination followed by 20% recontamination. Residuals <0.03 mag in cross-system transformations.
AI.conclusion: VT maintains reliable calibration capabilities for GRBs and transients despite challenges, validating its readiness for scientific operations.

title: Pre-localization of Massive Black Hole Binaries in the Millihertz Band
summary: The space-borne gravitational-wave (GW) detectors will open a new mass and redshift regime, allowing us to observe massive black hole binaries (MBHBs) throughout the Universe. A subset of these systems is expected to produce electromagnetic (EM) counterparts, offering a unique opportunity to follow the continuous evolution of massive black holes through joint GW and EM observations. Realizing this potential, however, requires low-latency, high-throughput data-analysis pipelines that can extract reliable source parameters and sky localizations from space-borne data streams fast enough to trigger EM follow-up. In this work we develop a fast, normalising flow-based inference pipeline designed for early-warning analysis of MBHB signals in a TianQin-like configuration. Our method combines a learned embedding of the detector time series with a neural spline flow (NSF) to perform amortized Bayesian inference, producing posterior samples for the main source parameters in roughly one minute per event. For a representative MBHB whose merger occurs $\sim 15$ minutes after the end of the analyzed GW observation, the pipeline achieves pre-merger sky localizations of order $\sim 20~\mathrm{deg}^2$, recovers the same number of sky modes as a reference parallel-tempered Markov chain Monte Carlo (PTMCMC) analysis, and yields parameter uncertainties of comparable scale, while still operating within a practically useful pre-merger warning window. These results demonstrate that NSF-based inference can deliver accurate, near-real-time parameter estimation for space-borne MBHB GW signals, and that the resulting early-warning localizations are sufficiently precise to make rapid EM follow-up.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a fast, normalizing flow-based inference pipeline for early-warning analysis of massive black hole binary signals in space-borne detectors like TianQin, enabling timely EM follow-up observations with pre-merger sky localization accuracy.
AI.motivation: Space-based GW detectors can observe MBHBs across the universe, but require efficient data analysis to rapidly determine parameters and sky locations for timely EM follow-up. Existing methods are too slow for real-time use.
AI.method: The method uses a learned embedding of detector time series combined with neural spline flows (NSF) for amortized Bayesian inference, enabling posterior sampling in ~1 minute per event.
AI.result: For a test case, the pipeline achieved ~20 deg² sky localization 15 mins pre-merger, matching PTMCMC's mode recovery and uncertainty estimates while operating within usable预警 windows.
AI.conclusion: NSF-based inference provides accurate, near-real-time MBHB parameter estimation for space-based GW data, enabling effective early warnings to facilitate rapid EM observations.

title: The VHF alert network of the SVOM mission
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 18 pages, 14 figures
summary: The scientific success of the SVOM mission will rely on the rapid transmission of alert messages from the satellite to the scientific community, and in particular to the ground-based instruments supporting the mission. In this paper, we present the alert system developed for SVOM which relies on the rapid transmission of alert messages through the transfer of data packets from an onboard VHF-band radio transmitter to a network of radio receivers deployed along the satellite ground track. We will successively detail the antenna design, radio performance, network deployment, its integration within the French data center, as well as the performance achieved after one year of operation in terms of availability and latency.

AI.tldr: The paper describes SVOM's alert system, which uses VHF radio to quickly send alerts via an on-board transmitter and ground receivers, ensuring rapid data transmission to ground instruments. It details the system's design, network deployment, integration into the French data center, and its successful performance metrics after a year.
AI.motivation: Enable rapid delivery of alert messages from SVOM satellite to ground-based instruments for effective scientific response.
AI.method: Development of a VHF-based alert system with onboard transmitter and ground receiver network; detailed design of antennas, radio performance optimization, and integration into the data center infrastructure.
AI.result: After one year of operation, the system demonstrated high availability and low latency in delivering alerts, fulfilling mission requirements.
AI.conclusion: The SVOM alert system successfully achieves its goal of timely data transmission, ensuring effective coordination between satellite and ground instruments for mission success.

title: Phenomenology of Vector Dark Matter produced by a First Order Phase Transition
summary: Both scalar and vector dark matter can be produced during a cosmological first order phase transition if the dark matter is coupled to the field undergoing the transition. Both kinds of particle are also produced by the plasma through the normal freeze out scenario. For different dark matter masses, we identify the regions of parameter space where there are significant deviations from the normal freeze out scenario and discover there are some rather general predictions. For dark matter particles in the traditional thermal relic GeV-TeV window, dark sector phase transitions around a GeV affect scalar dark matter and dark sector phase transitions around 10 MeV affect vector dark matter abundances (and therefore should take place in a dark sector). When the phase transitions are in the interesting temperature range, the normal range of dark matter masses are different to those predicted by thermal freeze out. We calculate the expected gravitational wave signal of these phase transitions.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how cosmological phase transitions influence the production of scalar and vector dark matter, finding that phase transitions at specific temperatures significantly alter their abundances compared to standard freeze-out scenarios and predicting observable gravitational wave signals.
AI.motivation: Understand deviations from thermal freeze-out in dark matter abundance, particularly when coupled to cosmological phase transitions, to explore new parameter spaces and testable predictions.
AI.method: Analyzing scalar/vector dark matter production via phase transitions and freeze-out for different masses, calculating gravitational wave signals from such transitions.
AI.result: Phase transitions around 1 GeV affect scalar DM; 10 MeV affect vector DM. These shift preferred mass ranges beyond thermal relics. Gravitational wave signals provide observational probes.
AI.conclusion: Dark matter abundance and mass predictions are modified by phase transition coupling, offering combined probes via gravitational waves and direct detection experiments.

title: The SVOM mission, its profile and its system
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 13 pages, 8 figures
summary: The SVOM (Space-based Variable Objects Monitor) mission, launched into low Earth orbit on 22 June 2024, is a French-Chinese multi-wavelength observatory dedicated to the study of the transient sky. Inspired by the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory, it consists of an autonomous rapid-slewing satellite, linked in real time to several ground-based telescopes. The space segment comprises two X-ray/gamma-ray wide-field instruments (ECLAIRs and GRM) with real-time triggering capabilities combined with two narrow-field telescopes in X-ray (MXT) and in visible (VT). In addition, the SVOM collaboration has also developed a unique visible and NIR ground-based follow-up system to promptly respond to the gamma-ray transients detected on board. The core program of SVOM will provide new insights into the Gamma-Ray Burst physics by providing a homogeneous dataset covering both the prompt and afterglow emissions, as well as better studying the low luminosity and soft Gamma-Ray Burst populations. As a versatile satellite platform with fast slewing capabilities, SVOM also comprises a Target of Opportunity program and a General Program consisting in pointed observations scheduled over the year that will both significantly contribute to the multi-messenger and time-domain astronomy.

AI.tldr: The SVOM mission, launched in June 2024, is a French-Chinese observatory designed to study transient celestial events. It uses X-ray/gamma-ray and visible/NIR instruments for real-time detection and follow-up of gamma-ray bursts, with a program aimed at advancing GRB physics and multi-messenger astronomy.
AI.motivation: To study Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs) and their afterglows comprehensively, improve understanding of low luminosity and soft GRB populations, and contribute to multi-messenger/time-domain astronomy via rapid follow-up observations.
AI.method: SVOM employs autonomous satellites with wide/narrow-field telescopes (ECLAIRs, GRM, MXT, VT) for real-time triggering and a ground-based follow-up system. It combines prompt/afterglow emission data collection with Target of Opportunity and General Programs for scheduled observations.
AI.result: Will provide a homogeneous GRB dataset, enhance detection capabilities for under-studied GRB types, and support advancements in multi-messenger astronomy through coordinated space-ground observations.
AI.conclusion: SVOM represents a significant step in transient sky monitoring, integrating Swift-like capabilities with expanded spectral coverage and collaborative observation strategies to push GRB research and time-domain astrophysics forward.

title: Scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac perturbations of a regular black hole supported by primordial dark matter
summary: We study massless scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac perturbations of the exact asymptotically flat regular black hole supported by a phantom Dirac--Born--Infeld scalar. Using the Padé-improved WKB method, with a time-domain Prony check for the scalar fundamental mode, we compute representative quasinormal frequencies and find that larger regularity shifts the spectrum toward smaller oscillation frequencies and damping rates, whereas the quality factor changes only weakly. The spectral shifts remain well above the estimated numerical uncertainty, demonstrating that the DBI regularity scale leaves a robust spin-dependent imprint on ringdown.

AI.tldr: The paper examines perturbations of a phantom Dirac-Born-Infeld scalar-supported regular black hole using the Padé-improved WKB and Prony analysis. It finds that regularity shifts influence quasinormal mode spectra, especially affecting oscillation frequencies and damping rates, while the quality factor remains stable.
AI.motivation: To investigate how the regularity of a phantom DBI scalar affects black hole perturbations and their quasinormal mode spectra, ensuring results are numerically robust.
AI.method: Analysis of scalar, electromagnetic, and Dirac perturbations via Padé-improved WKB method, validated by time-domain Prony analysis for scalar fundamental modes.
AI.result: Larger regularity shifts decrease oscillation frequencies and damping rates, with minimal changes in quality factor. Spectral shifts outperform numerical uncertainty thresholds.
AI.conclusion: The DBI regularity scale leaves a detectable, spin-dependent imprint on black hole ringdown signals, significant for gravitational wave astronomy.

title: Alert Chain and Observation Planning for Ground Wide Angle Camera Network
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics, 11 pages, 6 figures
summary: The Ground Wide Angle Camera Network (GWAC-N) is a robotic telescope network. It consists of ten wide-field core telescopes (GWAC-A) and two 60cm narrow-field rapid follow-up telescopes (GWAC-F60A/B). The primary scientific goal of GWAC-N is to detect optical counterparts of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) discovered by the SVOM satellite. This is achieved through synchronized monitoring with the GWAC-A array. Upon receiving a GRB trigger alert, the network conducts rapid, multi-band follow-up observations using the GWAC-F60A/B telescopes. The two-stage observation process involves many telescopes, making manual control impractical. Automated operations are therefore essential. They are realized through an integrated alert processing chain and an automated observation scheduling and dispatching mechanism. To enable this, we employ the SVOM Follow-up Observation Coordinating Service (FOCS) and GWAC-N's Automatic Observation Management (AOM) system. This paper presents the integrated alert processing workflow. It also describes the formulation of observation strategies, and the scheduling and execution of observations enabled by FOCS and AOM.

AI.tldr: The GWAC-N telescope network uses automated systems (FOCS and AOM) to detect GRB optical counterparts and manage rapid follow-up observations in coordination with the SVOM satellite.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to efficiently detect optical counterparts of GRBs discovered by SVOM and handle the complex, multi-telescope observation process through automation.
AI.method: The method involves a two-stage observation with GWAC-A for monitoring and GWAC-F60A/B for rapid follow-up, managed by FOCS for alert processing and AOM for automated scheduling.
AI.result: The system enables coordinated, automated GRB follow-up observations, overcoming the impracticality of manual control with multiple telescopes.
AI.conclusion: The integrated FOCS and AOM systems successfully automate the detection and observation process, fulfilling GWAC-N's scientific goals for GRB studies.

title: Non-perturbative heavy quark diffusion coefficients in arbitrarily magnetized quark-gluon plasma
summary: Heavy quark (HQ) momentum ($κ$) and spatial diffusion ($D_s$) coefficients are computed in a non-perturbative thermal QCD medium in the presence of a background magnetic field of arbitrary strength. Both perturbative and non-perturbative effects are incorporated via the in-medium HQ potential, obtained from the resummed gluon propagator. We find that the momentum diffusion coefficients become anisotropic even in the static heavy quark limit, with the magnetic field direction defining the axis of anisotropy. This anisotropy originates from restrictions on longitudinal momentum diffusion in the gluon spectral function, and naturally leads to two spatial diffusion coefficients ($D_s^L$, $D_s^T$). Non-perturbative effects are found to be dominant at low temperatures. These results provide a more consistent input for Langevin based calculations of the heavy quark directed flow at RHIC and LHC energies.

AI.tldr: The paper calculates heavy quark momentum and spatial diffusion coefficients in a thermal QCD medium with a magnetic field, revealing anisotropy in momentum diffusion and two spatial diffusion coefficients, emphasizing non-perturbative effects at low temperatures for Langevin flow simulations.
AI.motivation: To understand the impact of magnetic fields on heavy quark dynamics in QCD plasmas and provide accurate inputs for heavy quark directed flow calculations at colliders like RHIC and LHC.
AI.method: Using a non-perturbative in-medium HQ potential derived from a resummed gluon propagator, incorporating both perturbative and non-perturbative effects, the authors compute diffusion coefficients in a magnetized thermal QCD medium.
AI.result: Momentum diffusion shows magnetic field-induced anisotropy even for static heavy quarks; two distinct spatial diffusion coefficients (longitudinal and transverse) emerge. Non-perturbative effects dominate at low temperatures.
AI.conclusion: The findings advance the modeling of heavy quark dynamics in magnetized QCD plasmas and offer improved parameters for Langevin simulations of heavy ion collision experiments.

title: The coded mask of the ECLAIRs telescope onboard the SVOM space mission
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 7 pages, 14 figures
summary: ECLAIRs is a hard X-ray coded-mask telescope onboard the SVOM space mission, designed to detect and localize high-energy transients, in particular gamma-ray bursts. Operating over the 4-150 keV energy range, ECLAIRs extends coded-mask imaging to an unusually low-energy threshold. Achieving sensitivity down to 4 keV while maintaining performance up to 150 keV motivated the development of a novel self-supporting coded mask. This design addresses both scientific and mechanical challenges through dedicated pattern-generation algorithms and an innovative stiffened sandwich structure. We present the rationale, development, and final implementation of the ECLAIRs coded mask.

AI.tldr: The paper describes the development of a novel self-supporting coded mask for the ECLAIRs telescope on the SVOM mission, enabling low-energy threshold X-ray imaging for detecting high-energy transients like gamma-ray bursts.
AI.motivation: To extend coded-mask imaging to lower energy thresholds (4 keV) while maintaining sensitivity up to 150 keV, addressing the need for detecting and localizing high-energy transients effectively.
AI.method: The team developed a self-supporting coded mask using dedicated pattern-generation algorithms and a stiffened sandwich structure to meet both scientific performance and mechanical stability requirements.
AI.result: The final implementation successfully achieves the desired energy range sensitivity and structural integrity, overcoming challenges in designing a coded mask for extreme environments.
AI.conclusion: The innovative design demonstrates effectiveness in balancing scientific objectives with engineering constraints, providing a robust solution for future space-based X-ray observations.

title: Gravitational waves of extreme-mass-ratio inspirals in a rotating black hole with Dehnen dark matter halo
summary: Extreme Mass Ratio Inspirals (EMRIs) are among the key targe sources for the space-based gravitational wave (GW) detectors. The waveforms of the EMRIs are highly sensitive to the types of the central supermassive black hole (SBH) and can serve as a novel sensitive tool to probe the background spacetime. In this work, we compute GWs radiated from EMRIs in the backgrounds of Kerr black hole and rotating black hole with Dehnen-type dark matter halo (DMBH). Following the Teukolsky prescription, we obtain the perturbed equations for curvature tensor from the Newman-Penrose (NP) equations, and for the DMBH we obtain the radial and angular equations through separation of variables. To solve the equations with numerical method we apply the Sasaki-Nakamura (SN) transformation to convert the Teykolsky-type equation into the SN equation. We study the radiation reaction of GWs by computing the energy flux and angular momentum flux at infinity and at the horizon. The orbital evolution is then derived from the total fluxes. We extract the two polarizations of GWs by solving the equation numerically. By comparing the waveforms of Kerr and DMBH, it is found that the DM halo induces noticeable changes in both the amplitude and phase of GWs. We compute the strain of GW detector with the response function and evaluate the mismatch between the waveforms of Kerr and DMBH. The results show that the mismatch increases with the mass parameter of DM halo and the spin of the SBH.

AI.tldr: The study investigates how dark matter halos around supermassive black holes affect gravitational wave signals from extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs), revealing noticeable changes in waveform amplitude and phase, which could be detectable by space-based GW detectors.
AI.motivation: To probe the spacetime structure around supermassive black holes and detect deviations caused by dark matter halos using EMRI gravitational wave signals.
AI.method: Computed GWs from EMRIs in Kerr black hole and DMBH (Dehnen-type dark matter halo) backgrounds. Used Teukolsky formalism and Newman-Penrose equations, solved with Sasaki-Nakamura transformation. Calculated energy/angular momentum fluxes, orbital evolution, and GW polarizations.
AI.result: DM halos induce measurable changes in GW waveform amplitude/phase. Mismatch between Kerr and DMBH waveforms increases with DM mass parameter and SBH spin.
AI.conclusion: GW detectors like LISA can use EMRI signals to test alternative gravity theories and map dark matter distributions around SBHs.

title: SVOM Real-time Response and Collaboration System
comment: Accepter for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 6 pages,4 figures
summary:
The SVOM mission (Space-based multi-band astronomical Variable Objects Monitor) is a Franco-Chinese mission dedicated to the study of the most distant explosions of stars, the gamma-ray bursts. Here, we introduce the real-time response and collaboration system of SVOM, with the adoption of the BeiDou-3 short message communication service. We present the SVOM on-board and on-ground system designs and data flow, together with the collaboration mechanism with other missions. In the first year of the in-flight operation, SVOM has detected 172 gamma-ray bursts, including 147 by the GRM instrument and 62 by the ECLAIRs instrument.
  At the same time, SVOM has performed 1040 observations, including 122 ToO-EX(Target of Opportunity-Exceptional) observations, 48 ToO-MM(Target of Opportunity-Multi-messenger) observations and 870 ToO-NOM(Target of Opportunity-Nominal) observations. All these have increased the scientific output of the mission.

AI.tldr: The SVOM mission, a Franco-Chinese collaboration, uses real-time response systems including BeiDou-3 for gamma-ray burst detection and collaboration with other missions. In its first year, it detected 172 bursts and performed 1040 observations, enhancing mission science output.
AI.motivation: To study distant gamma-ray bursts effectively through advanced on-board systems, real-time collaboration, and multi-messenger/multi-band observations.
AI.method: Implements the BeiDou-3 short message service for real-time data transmission, integrates on-board and ground systems, and establishes collaboration protocols with other missions. Uses GRM and ECLAIRs instruments for burst detection.
AI.result: In the first operational year, SVOM detected 172 gamma-ray bursts (147 via GRM, 62 via ECLAIRs) and executed 1040 observations, including ToO-EX, ToO-MM, and ToO-NOM types, significantly boosting scientific results.
AI.conclusion: The system design and collaboration mechanisms successfully enhance SVOM's observational capabilities and scientific productivity, establishing its role in advancing gamma-ray burst research.

title: Mass spectra of charged mesons and the quenching of vector meson condensation via exact phase-space diagonalization
summary: We investigate the dynamics and mass spectra of charged pseudoscalar ($π^+$) and vector ($ρ^+$) mesons in a background magnetic field at finite temperature using the two-flavor Nambu-Jona--Lasinio (NJL) model. By employing a quark propagator that isolates the Schwinger phase from its Landau level expansion, we formulate an exact non-commutative phase-space framework utilizing the Wigner-Weyl transform and the Moyal star product. This approach enables the algebraic diagonalization of the Bethe-Salpeter equations for composite states with asymmetric fractional constituent charges. For the pseudoscalar channel, we analytically verify the exact cancellation between the dynamical random phase approximation spatial sum rules and the vacuum gap equation. This identity preserves the generalized Goldstone theorem, causing the $π^+$ pole mass to strictly track the kinematic zero-point energy drift at order of $eB$. In the vector channel, our full phase-space evaluation reveals that the Zeeman spin-splitting emerges dynamically from microscopic threshold truncations governed by the chiral Dirac algebra. Notably, we find that the tachyonic instability of the spin-aligned $ρ^+$ state is quenched. The magnetic catalysis of the chiral condensate drives the continuum threshold ($2M$) upwards, overtaking the Zeeman attraction and preventing vector meson condensation within this mean-field framework. Furthermore, finite-temperature evaluations show a monotonic thermal suppression of the meson masses driven by Pauli blocking, yet all modes remain bound without undergoing Mott dissociation prior to chiral symmetry restoration.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the behavior of charged mesons in strong magnetic fields and at finite temperature using the NJL model, revealing unique mass shifts and stability through quantum field theory methods.
AI.motivation: To understand how charged pseudoscalar and vector mesons' properties are affected by magnetic fields and temperature, and to validate the generalized Goldstone theorem under these conditions.
AI.method: Used a two-flavor NJL model with a quark propagator incorporating Schwinger phases, applied non-commutative phase-space techniques via Wigner-Weyl transforms and Moyal products, solved Bethe-Salpeter equations for states with fractional charges.
AI.result: Pseudoscalar π + mass precisely follows magnetic field-induced zero-point energy shifts. Vector ρ +'s Zeeman splitting arises from microscopic thresholds, with its tachyonic instability suppressed by magnetic catalysis. Thermal effects reduce meson masses via Pauli blocking without Mott dissociation.
AI.conclusion: The framework confirms the robustness of Goldstone's theorem in magnetic fields and shows magnetic catalysis and thermal effects stabilize mesons without condensation, offering insights for high-density QCD scenarios like neutron stars or heavy-ion collisions.

title: In-flight performance of the MXT Camera
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 11 pages, 12 figures
summary: On-board the SVOM mission, the Microchannel X-ray Telescope observes the soft X-ray band of the gamma-ray bursts afterglows. The so-called lobster-eye optics focuses X-rays to the camera subsystem that performs imaging and spectroscopy of a region of the sky 58x58 arcmin2 wide centered on the burst detected by the ECLAIRs instrument. The recorded photon positions are used by the on-board scientific software to rapidly localize the source, whereas spectral information is used on ground to model the properties of the gamma-ray bursts. The first months in orbit were intensively used to tune the parameter settings of the detector and the calibration method to provide high availability of the camera and accurate spectroscopy to the users. The paper presents the design of the camera validated by on-ground testing, the tuning phase in flight and the performance of the camera at the beginning of the mission. Perspectives are given concerning the evolution of the spectral response during the mission.

AI.tldr: The Microchannel X-ray Telescope (MXT) onboard SVOM is designed to study gamma-ray burst afterglows, focusing X-rays with lobster-eye optics to a 58x58 arcmin² camera. Onboard software localizes sources rapidly, while ground analysis models burst properties. The paper details the camera design, mission calibration, and initial performance, with future focus on spectral response changes during the mission.
AI.motivation: To enable precise localization and spectral analysis of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) for better understanding their afterglows and improving mission efficiency. The MXT addresses the need for rapid, accurate GRB detection and calibration to ensure high camera availability and reliable spectroscopy.
AI.method: The MXT uses lobster-eye optics to focus X-rays to a detector array in the camera. On-ground testing validated the design. Post-launch, the first months were used to tune detector parameters and calibration methods. Spectral and positional data are analyzed both onboard (for localization) and on ground (for spectral modeling).
AI.result: Successful validation of the camera's design through pre-launch tests. Initial mission phases demonstrated effective parameter tuning and calibration, ensuring high operational availability and accurate spectroscopy. The system achieves its goal of rapid source localization and preliminary spectral analysis.
AI.conclusion: The MXT camera performs well early in the mission, with ongoing monitoring to track spectral response changes. This paves the way for continuous, accurate GRB afterglow studies, enhancing astrophysical insights and mission capabilities over time.

title: Geometry of transient gravitational waves and estimation of efficiencies of different detector configurations
comment: 14 pages. Accepted for publication in Int. J. Geom. Meth. Mod. Phys
summary: This work introduces a geometrical method for analyzing transient gravitational waves recorded at interferometric observatories. This approach is intended to aid in assessing the performance and sensitivity of next-generation detector configurations, such as Cosmic Explorer, Einstein Telescope, and the South American Gravitational-wave Observatory.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a geometric method for analyzing transient gravitational waves to evaluate the performance and sensitivity of next-generation detectors like Cosmic Explorer, Einstein Telescope, and the South American Gravitational-wave Observatory.
AI.motivation: To assess the capabilities of upcoming gravitational wave observatories and optimize their configurations for improved detection.
AI.method: Development of a geometrical analysis method for transient gravitational wave data from interferometric detectors.
AI.result: This method provides a tool for evaluating detector performance and sensitivity, aiding in the design of next-generation facilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is essential for optimizing future gravitational wave observatories to enhance their detection sensitivity and overall effectiveness.

title: GRM Scientific Pipeline
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics
summary: The Gamma-Ray Monitor (GRM) is a key payload of the Space-based multiband astronomical Variable Objects Monitor (SVOM) mission, which is designed to detect gamma ray bursts (GRBs) within the energy range of 15 keV to 5 MeV. The GRM Instrument Center (GRM\_IC) features real-time data processing through the X-band, enabling rapid response of high-energy GRB events. The system employs an event-driven architecture and distributed design, achieving efficient processing and real-time monitoring of massive observational data. Through comprehensive data production processes and scientific data product management, the system achieves efficient production of scientific data products of the L1B / C level through the submission of jobs to the task scheduling system. Through modular architecture design and automated processing workflow, the GRM data processing system realizes precise conversion and scientific analysis of GRB detection data, providing robust technical support for future system upgrades and cross-platform collaboration.

AI.tldr: The GRM, a key instrument on the SVOM mission, detects gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and uses real-time data processing via X-band to enable rapid response. Its event-driven architecture and distributed design efficiently handle massive data, producing L1B/C level scientific products and supporting future upgrades and collaborations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enable rapid detection and response to high-energy GRB events by processing observational data in real-time, while providing a scalable system for future upgrades and cross-platform work.
AI.method: The system uses an event-driven architecture, distributed design, modular structure, and automated workflows. It submits jobs to a task scheduler for data processing, converting raw data into scientific products (L1B/C levels) through comprehensive production processes.
AI.result: Efficient real-time monitoring and processing of GRB data is achieved, with precise conversion and analysis enabling robust technical support for future system upgrades and collaborations.
AI.conclusion: The GRM_IC's design successfully meets the requirements for handling massive GRB data in real-time, offering a reliable platform for current and future astronomical studies through its flexible architecture.

title: Nucleon decays into one lepton plus two non-strange mesons
summary: Nucleon decays into a lepton and two pseudoscalar mesons represent key channels for probing baryon number violation, complementing conventional two-body modes. In this Letter, we model-independently correlate two- and three-body processes within the framework of low-energy effective field theory, performing a global analysis that avoids single-operator-dominance assumption. We derive significantly improved bounds on 15 three-body modes with a lepton ($e^+,\,μ^+,\hatν=ν/\barν$) and two non-strange mesons ($π,η$). For charged-lepton modes, our lower limits on partial lifetimes ($Γ^{-1}$) surpass current Particle Data Group (PDG) values by more than three orders of magnitude. For 5 (anti)neutrino modes, we establish for the first time $Γ^{-1}\gtrsim 10^{34}\,\rm yr$. Additionally, our analysis improves constraints on two-body processes $n\to e^+π^-$, $n\to μ^+π^-$, and $p\to \hatνπ^+$ by approximately a factor of 2 compared to the PDG limits. These results highlight the importance of leveraging correlations among different processes to better probe new physics, enabling more stringent constraints on experimentally challenging processes from well-measured ones.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a model-independent analysis of nucleon decay modes using effective field theory, significantly improving lower limits on partial lifetimes for both charged-lepton and (anti)neutrino three-body decay channels, and enhancing constraints on two-body modes by correlating them with three-body processes.
AI.motivation: To explore baryon number violation beyond conventional two-body decay channels by analyzing three-body nucleon decays and improving experimental constraints through correlation between decay modes.
AI.method: Model-independent, low-energy effective field theory framework correlating two- and three-body decay processes globally without assuming single-operator dominance.
AI.result: Derived improved bounds: three-body charged-lepton modes' partial lifetimes are 3+ orders of magnitude higher than PDG benchmarks; 5 (anti)neutrino modes now have lower limits ~1e34 yr. Two-body processes (e.g., n→e+π-, p→νπ+) are constrained 2x tighter than PDG data.
AI.conclusion: Correlating decay channels via effective field theory provides a powerful method to constrain baryon number violation, offering stricter limits on less observable processes using measured decay data.

title: SVOM/VT: Overview of data processing and GRB identifications with X-band data
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 15 pages, 11 figures
summary: VT (the Visible Telescope) is an optical telescope onboard the SVOM (Space-based Multi-band Astronomical Variable Objects Monitor) mission, specifically designed to detect optical counterparts of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs), study their afterglows, and select high-redshift candidates. It performs rapid follow-up observations simultaneously in two channels either via autonomous platform slewing or Target of Opportunity (ToO) observations. The science images acquired by VT and transmitted via the X-band downlink system are designated as VT X-band data. This paper provides an overview of GRB optical afterglow identifications with VT and describes the ground-based processing pipeline for VT X-band data, including preprocessing, astrometric calibration, and photometry. Up to 2025 December 3, VT has followed up 111 GRBs triggered by SVOM or external missions. The overall detection rate of optical counterparts is approximately 75%. Specifically, for bursts detected by SVOM/ECLAIRs, the detection rate is 77% when observed by VT within 30 minutes after the burst. A slightly higher detection rate of 81% is achieved for GRBs triggered by external missions through rapid ToO observations with a mid-time of less than 3 hours.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses the Visible Telescope (VT) onboard the SVOM mission, detailing its role in detecting optical counterparts of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and processing X-band data. It highlights GRB afterglow identifications and a ground-based processing pipeline, reporting a 75% overall detection rate with higher efficacy for timely observations.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind the paper is to provide an overview of VT's capabilities in identifying GRB optical afterglows and to describe the ground-based processing pipeline for VT X-band data, ensuring efficient analysis of observational data for astrophysical studies.
AI.method: The method involves using VT's two-channel observations, either through autonomous platform slewing or Target of Opportunity (ToO) observations. The ground processing pipeline includes preprocessing, astrometric calibration, and photometry to analyze the science images transmitted via X-band.
AI.result: Up to December 3, 2025, VT has followed up 111 GRBs, achieving a 75% overall detection rate of optical counterparts. SVOM/ECLAIRs-detected GRBs observed within 30 minutes had a 77% detection rate, while external missions with ToO observations under 3 hours achieved 81%.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion underscores VT's effectiveness in rapidly detecting GRB optical counterparts, demonstrating the success of its observational strategies and processing pipeline in advancing the study of GRBs and high-redshift candidates.

title: Tests of scalar polarizations with multi-messenger events
summary: Gravitational wave (GW) observations provide a unique opportunity to test Einstein's General Relativity (GR) in the strong-field regime. While GR predicts only two tensor polarization modes, generic metric theories allow up to six independent modes. We perform a parameterized test of GR using the parameterized post-Einsteinian (PPE) framework applied to GW170817, incorporating for the first time the polarization angle constraints from the gamma-ray burst afterglow alongside other electromagnetic (EM) counterpart information. We extend the GR waveform by adding a scalar breathing mode and modifications to the tensor modes, introducing three non-GR parameters. We perform Bayesian inference for both quadrupole $\ell = |m|= 2$ and dipole $\ell = |m|= 1$ angular harmonics, with two frequency evolution models. For $\ell = |m|= 2$ , we find mild preference for a scalar mode (scalar amplitude deviates from zero at $\sim 2 σ$), while for the $\ell = |m|= 1$, we find no preference for a scalar mode. The EM constraint on the polarization angle places very tight bounds on non-GR parameters; for instance, in the case $\ell = |m| = 2$, the bound on the scalar (tensor) amplitude modification parameter improves by roughly $60\%$ $(30\%)$, highlighting the impact that long-term follow up of GW events can have on tests of gravity.

AI.tldr: The study uses GW170817 data and electromagnetic observations to test General Relativity's predictions on gravitational wave polarizations, finding mild evidence for a scalar mode in the quadrupole harmonic but not in the dipole, with EM constraints significantly enhancing parameter bounds.
AI.motivation: To test Einstein's General Relativity in strong-field regimes by analyzing gravitational wave polarizations, leveraging the unique multimessenger observation of GW170817 and its electromagnetic counterparts.
AI.method: Applied the parameterized post-Einsteinian (PPE) framework to GW170817, incorporating gamma-ray burst afterglow polarization angle constraints and other EM data. Extended GR waveforms with three non-GR parameters (scalar breathing mode and tensor modifications), performing Bayesian inference for quadrupole and dipole harmonics under two frequency models.
AI.result: Mild preference (≈2σ) for a scalar mode in the quadrupole (ℓ=2) but not dipole (ℓ=1). EM constraints tightened non-GR parameter bounds by ~60% (scalar) and ~30% (tensor) for ℓ=2, demonstrating the importance of long-term EM follow-up for gravity tests.
AI.conclusion: Multimessenger data significantly strengthens GR tests. While no strong violations found, the method shows promise for future observations to more rigorously probe gravity theories in extreme conditions.

title: SVOM/VT: Flight Model Verification and Pre-launch Testing
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Reaearch in Astronomy and Astrophysics: 16pages, 14 figures, 3 tables
summary: This paper presents pre-launch testing and calibration results for the SVOM/VT (Space-based Variable Objects Monitor, Visible Telescope) Flight Model (FM), validating its performance under simulated space conditions through thermal vacuum cycling, energy concentration analysis, stray light suppression, and CCD/electronics calibrations (gain, noise, quantum efficiency). The results confirm full compliance with design requirements: stray light suppression achieves point-source transmittance $<10^{-7}$ at $30^\circ$ off-axis, thermal control maintains stable CCD temperatures ($-75^\circ$C for the red channel, $-65^\circ$C for the blue channel), and detection sensitivity meets the limiting magnitude of 22.50 (SNR $>$ 3 with 300 seconds exposure). Early in-orbit tests further validate performance, yielding limiting magnitudes of 22.70 (V-band, red) and 22.78 (blue), consistent with pre-launch specifications.

AI.tldr: The paper details successful pre-launch tests and calibrations for the SVOM/VT Visible Telescope Flight Model, confirming compliance with design specifications through thermal vacuum cycling, stray light suppression, and CCD performance evaluations.
AI.motivation: To validate the SVOM/VT FM's readiness for space operations by confirming its performance under simulated space conditions and ensuring it meets mission requirements for sensitivity, thermal stability, and stray light control.
AI.method: The study involved thermal vacuum cycling, energy concentration analysis, stray light suppression testing (measuring point-source transmittance), and CCD/electronics calibrations (gain, noise, quantum efficiency). In-orbit tests were also conducted post-launch.
AI.result: Stray light suppression achieved <10^-7 transmittance at 30° off-axis. Thermal control maintained CCD temperatures at -75°C (red channel) and -65°C (blue channel). Detection sensitivity met a limiting magnitude of 22.50 (SNR>3 with 300s exposure). Early in-orbit results showed even better performance (22.70-22.78 magnitudes).
AI.conclusion: Pre-launch and in-orbit testing confirmed SVOM/VT FM fully complies with design requirements, ensuring reliable scientific operations post-launch.

title: On the Two $R$-Factors in the Small-$x$ Shockwave Formalism
summary:
There are two $R$-factors frequently used in the phenomenology of exclusive processes at small values of the Bjorken $x$ variable. One $R$-factor takes into account the effects of non-zero longitudinal momentum transfer, which is assumed to be zero in the dipole scattering amplitude. Another $R$-factor accounts for the real part of the elastic scattering amplitude which is often neglected, with the standard dipole scattering amplitude giving only the imaginary part of the elastic amplitude.
  In this work we present two new theoretical developments aimed at eliminating the need for the two $R$-factors. We argue that the $R$-factors can be replaced by (i) modifying the argument of the dipole scattering amplitude and by (ii) augmenting the initial conditions for its non-linear small-$x$ evolution. Specifically, we show that to account for the effects of non-zero skewness $ξ$, one has to replace the rapidity argument $Y = \ln (1/x)$ of the eikonal dipole amplitude $N$ and the odderon dipole amplitude $\cal O$ by $Y = \ln \min \left\{ 1/|x|, 1/|ξ|\right\}$. The prescription applies to the elastic scattering cross sections, as well as for calculations of the Generalized Parton Distributions and Generalized Transverse Momentum Dependent parton distributions at small $x$ and at small but non-zero skewness $ξ$. We also show that the real part of the scattering amplitude, proportional to Im~$N$, which is intimately connected to the signature factor of the amplitude, can be accounted for by a more careful evaluation of the initial condition for the evolution and by writing the non-linear evolution equation in an integral form. One can similarly construct Im~$\cal O$ for the odd-signature odderon amplitude. We hope that future implementation of our prescriptions presented here will eliminate the need for both phenomenological $R$-factors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two theoretical modifications to the dipole scattering amplitude framework to eliminate the need for two phenomenological $R$-factors commonly used in exclusive processes at small $x$. The first modification adjusts the rapidity argument to account for non-zero skewness $\xi$, and the second revises the initial conditions and uses an integral form of the non-linear evolution equation to incorporate the real part of the scattering amplitude.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of the standard dipole scattering amplitude, which assumes zero longitudinal momentum transfer and neglects the real part of the elastic scattering amplitude, captured by the two $R$-factors in phenomenology.
