AI.method: 1. Replace the rapidity $Y = \ln(1/x)$ with $Y = \ln \min\{1/|x|, 1/|\xi|\}$ to account for non-zero skewness $\xi$. 2. Modify the initial conditions and use an integral form of the non-linear evolution equation to include the real part of the amplitude, eliminating the need for $R$-factors.
AI.result: The proposed framework unifies the treatment of skewness and real parts without empirical $R$-parameters, applicable to elastic scattering cross sections and generalized parton distributions at small $x$ and $\xi$. Future implementation aims to remove reliance on both $R$-factors.
AI.conclusion: The new prescriptions provide a theoretically consistent approach to eliminate phenomenological $R$-factors, offering a more accurate description of small-$x$ processes by inherently accounting for skewness and real parts through modified evolution equations and initial conditions.

title: The GRB joint scientific analysis pipeline of the ECLAIRs and GRM instruments on board SVOM
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 10 pages, 9 figures
summary: The study of the prompt high-energy emission of Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs) with SVOM relies on the observations performed by ECLAIRs (4-150 keV) and the Gamma-Ray Monitor (GRM, 0.015-5 MeV), the two wide field-of-view instruments on board the satellite. In this article, we introduce the eclgrm pipelines running at the French Science Center of SVOM, which combine the ECLAIRs and GRM data to generate scientific data products describing the GRB broad-band temporal and spectral properties. The architecture of the pipelines is presented, as well as their activation following each onboard trigger, and their workflow. The statistical data analysis methods employed by the pipelines are described, along with the scientific data products that are created in real time or from the full event data. We also present the eclgrm-ui user interface which allows the scientists on shift to monitor the automated data processings in the pipelines, and to optimize the analysis results interactively.

AI.tldr: The article describes the eclgrm pipelines used by SVOM's ECLAIRs and GRM instruments to analyze GRB emissions, detailing their architecture, real-time data processing, and user interface for scientists.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive analysis of GRB prompt emissions using combined ECLAIRs and GRM data, enabling accurate temporal and spectral property determination.
AI.method: The eclgrm pipelines integrate data from both instruments, employ statistical methods for real-time and full-event analysis, and utilize the eclgrm-ui for interactive optimization by scientists.
AI.result: Generates scientific data products that describe GRB broadband characteristics, enhancing understanding of high-energy emissions through integrated analysis.
AI.conclusion: The pipelines and interface effectively support real-time GRB studies, offering scientists tools to refine analyses and respond promptly to observational triggers.

title: Pair-Dependent Drift of Kerr Neighboring-Overtone Gap Minima
summary: Quasinormal modes (QNMs) underpin black-hole ringdown modeling and spectroscopy, where higher overtones can contribute at early times and neighboring modes can become locally organized in the complex-frequency plane. Motivated by this, we continuously track Kerr overtones along a spin scan and study a raw neighboring-overtone quantity: the complex-frequency gap between adjacent overtones. We find robust interior minima whose spin locations drift from pair to pair, even within the same \((s,\ell,m)\) sector. We explain this by reformulating minimum setting as a local zero-setting problem for the complex separation between the two modes. Differentiating the squared gap yields a denominator-free real-projection diagnostic (the real part of the product between the complex separation, with conjugation, and its spin derivative). The sampled minimum is governed by an approximate local zero of this diagnostic, so minimum drift becomes the drift of its dominant zero crossing. This also yields a geometric picture: the minimum is a local radial turning event of the separation vector, while angular motion in the complex plane may persist. Finally, an expanded but deliberately restricted representative set (including same-family continuations, external positive transfers, and smooth no-trigger controls) supports this local picture for the triggered cases examined here. At the same time, the detailed local crossing environment remains intrinsically pair dependent.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the complex-frequency gap between neighboring quasinormal modes (QNMs) of Kerr black holes under spin variation, revealing drifting minima explained by a geometric separation diagnostic.
AI.motivation: To understand how higher QNM overtones contribute to black-hole ringdown and how neighboring modes organize in the complex-frequency plane during spin scans.
AI.method: Continuous tracking of Kerr overtones' spin scans, analyzing the complex-frequency gap between adjacent overtones. Reformulated minimum detection as a zero-setting problem for the complex separation, deriving a real-projection diagnostic involving separation and its spin derivative.
AI.result: Discovered robust interior minima in the gap with drifting spin locations between mode pairs. The minima correspond to local 'turning points' in the separation vector's radial motion, influenced by an approximate zero crossing of the diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: The drifting minima arise from local geometric properties of mode separations. While a unified framework explains the mechanism, specific behaviors depend on mode pair characteristics. Supports a local geometric interpretation for these QNM interactions.

title: SVOM/VT: Real-Time Onboard Data Processing
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics: 15 pages, 6 figures
summary: The SVOM Visible Telescope (VT) is critical for the rapid identification of gamma-ray burst (GRB) optical counterparts, particularly for high-redshift candidates that require immediate infrared spectroscopic follow-up. To address the stringent bandwidth constraints of the VHF downlink while ensuring real-time data availability, we developed the VT Onboard Data Processing Pipeline (VOPP).This paper details the software architecture, algorithms, and hardware implementation of VOPP using an FPGA and a CPU. The pipeline performs essential real-time tasks, including image quality assessment, dark and flat-field correction, and optimized image stacking to mitigate cosmic ray contamination and variable background noise. Furthermore, it generates compact source catalogs and highly compressed 1-bit images to facilitate rapid downlink.In-flight performance analysis confirms the pipeline's robustness, demonstrating the availability of VT VHF data for 78 percent of promptly slewed SVOM GRBs, with 56 percent leading to the identification of optical counterparts, typically within 18 minutes post-trigger.

AI.tldr: VOPP is an onboard data processing pipeline designed for the SVOM Visible Telescope to enable rapid identification of GRB optical counterparts by optimizing data reduction, compression, and real-time downlink under bandwidth constraints.
AI.motivation: To address bandwidth limitations of the VHF downlink and ensure real-time availability of data for immediate infrared spectroscopic follow-up of high-redshift GRB optical counterparts.
AI.method: The VOPP pipeline uses FPGA and CPU hardware with algorithms for image quality assessment, dark/flat-field correction, cosmic ray mitigation, optimized image stacking, and generates compressed 1-bit images and source catalogs.
AI.result: 78% data availability for promptly slewed GRBs, with 56% leading to optical counterpart identification within 18 minutes post-trigger.
AI.conclusion: VOPP successfully provides essential real-time data processing capabilities critical for timely follow-up observations of GRBs, demonstrating its effectiveness inMeeting demanding observational requirements.

title: Accurate distances of the Galactic spiral arms from dust-scattered X-ray emission of gamma-ray bursts
summary: The details of the spiral structure of the Milky Way are still debated due to large uncertainties in the distance estimates obtained through the most common tracers. X-ray dust scattering rings produced by short extragalactic X-ray transients provide instead a direct method to measure the 3D distribution of interstellar clouds up to the edges of our Galaxy with a few percent precision. We report on the analysis of all the available XMM-Newton and Chandra follow-up observations of three low-latitude gamma-ray bursts: GRB 031203 ($l \sim 255°$, $b \sim -5°$), GRB 160623A ($l \sim 84°$, $b \sim -3°$), and GRB 221009A ($l \sim 53°$, $b \sim 4°$). The previous detection of X-ray rings in these observations, produced by dust clouds located beyond 5 kpc, can be associated with dust in the Perseus, Outer, and Outer Scutum-Centaurus arms, thus providing direct distance measurements to these structures along three distinct lines of sight. We have identified two additional rings in the direction of GRB 160623A, produced by dusty clouds at $6.91\, \pm\,0.06$ kpc and $9.9\,\pm\,0.6$ kpc, and confirmed -- through a second XMM-Newton observation -- the presence of one cloud at $9.7\,\pm\,0.4$ kpc toward GRB 031203. We also accurately measured the distance of dusty clouds up to $19.0\,\pm\,0.2$ kpc owing to the analysis of one Chandra and four XMM-Newton observations of GRB 221009A. The small statistical and systematic uncertainties of these measurements place tight constraints on the geometry of the outer Milky Way and reveal significant deviations from current models, which critically depend on spectroscopy-based Galactic rotation curves at large distances.

AI.tldr: This paper uses X-ray dust scattering rings from gamma-ray bursts observed by XMM-Newton and Chandra to precisely determine distances to interstellar clouds in Milky Way spiral arms (Perseus, Outer, Outer Scutum-Centaurus), revealing discrepancies with existing galactic models.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainties in Milky Way spiral structure due to imprecise distance measures from traditional tracers, leveraging X-ray rings' superior accuracy for direct 3D cloud positioning.
AI.method: Analyzed X-ray observations of three low-latitude GRBs (031203, 160623A, 221009A) to identify dust scattering rings, determine cloud distances, and compare with galactic models.
AI.result: Measured distances up to ~19 kpc with <2% precision; found two new rings at ~6.9-10 kpc for GRB 160623A and confirmed a 9.7 kpc cloud for GRB 031203; GRB 221009A showed the farthest cloud at 19 kpc.
AI.conclusion: Results challenge existing Milky Way models reliant on spectroscopic rotation curves, indicating need for revised structures incorporating these precise distance constraints.

title: Hawking Temperature, Sparsity and Energy Emission Rate of Dark Matter Halo Regular Black Holes
summary: In this paper, we investigate the thermodynamic and radiative properties of a regular black hole sourced by a dark matter halo described by the Einasto density profile. The closed-form expressions for the Hawking temperature, specific heat capacity, sparsity parameter of Hawking flux, and the spectral energy emission rate were obtained. All these are examined as a function of the characteristic scale parameter $α$ of the dark matter distribution and compared with the standard Schwarzschild results. We show that the presence of a dark matter halo suppresses both the Hawking temperature and energy emission rate relative to the standard black hole result. Crucially, the specific heat capacity can be positive for a finite range of horizon radii, signaling a thermodynamically stable phase; the boundary of this stable region defines a Davies-type phase transition. The sparsity parameter is higher than in the standard black hole, indicating that the dark matter environment makes the Hawking flux even more intermittent.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how dark matter halos with an Einasto profile affect thermodynamic and radiative properties of regular black holes, finding suppression of Hawking temperature and emission rate, a potential thermally stable phase, and increased Hawking flux intermittency.
AI.motivation: To understand the impact of dark matter distributions on black hole thermodynamics and radiation, going beyond the standard Schwarzschild model.
AI.method: Deriving closed-form expressions for thermodynamic (Hawking temperature, specific heat) and radiative (sparsity parameter, spectral energy emission rate) properties as functions of the Einasto profile's α parameter, then comparing with Schwarzschild results.
AI.result: Dark matter halos reduce Hawking temperature and emission rate; specific heat capacity allows a stable phase with Davies-phase transitions; Hawking flux intermittency increases (higher sparsity parameter).
AI.conclusion: Dark matter significantly alters black hole thermodynamic behavior, introducing stability regions and modifying radiation characteristics compared to vacuum Schwarzschild solutions.

title: SVOM/VT: On-ground processing of VT-VHF data
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 11 pages, 4 figures
summary: The VT--VHF data comprise three types of onboard-processed data results generated from four sequential observational sequences and transmitted to the ground via a Very High Frequency (VHF) downlink. On the ground, these data are processed by three successive pipelines: the pre-processing pipeline, the VT--VHF data processing pipeline (VVPP), and the VT afterglow candidate pipeline (VTAC). These pipelines perform packet decoding, astrometric and photometric calibration, and afterglow candidate identification, respectively. This paper describes the architecture and operational implementation of the VT--VHF ground processing system and assesses its end-to-end performance using the first year of SVOM operations. These data enable rapid identification of GRB optical counterparts. Early detections, while the source is still optically bright, facilitate spectroscopic redshift measurements. Dual-band colors provide preliminary redshift constraints and help identify high-redshift candidates, whereas non-detections in both bands may indicate very high redshift, significant extinction, or intrinsically dark bursts. In-orbit operations show that the VT--VHF ground processing system successfully identifies optical afterglow candidates for a significant fraction of ECLAIRs triggers with available VT--VHF data, demonstrating its robustness and readiness.

AI.tldr: This paper details the design, implementation, and performance evaluation of the VT-VHF ground processing system for SVOM mission data. It demonstrates the system's effectiveness in rapidly identifying GRB optical counterparts using dual-band observations to constrain redshift and classify high-redshift candidates.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enable rapid detection of gamma-ray burst (GRB) optical counterparts for timely spectroscopic redshift measurements. Early detection allows for capturing the bright phase of the source, which is crucial for determining cosmological parameters and understanding burst mechanisms.
AI.method: The study employs three ground processing pipelines: pre-processing for packet decoding, VVPP for astrometric/photometric calibration, and VTAC for afterglow candidate identification. The system's architecture and operation are analyzed using the first year of SVOM mission data, assessing end-to-end performance metrics.
AI.result: The system successfully identifies optical afterglow candidates for a significant fraction of ECLAIRs triggers with available data. Dual-band color analysis provides preliminary redshift constraints, distinguishing high-redshift candidates, while non-detections suggest extreme redshift, extinction, or intrinsically dim bursts. In-orbit results confirm the system's robustness and reliability.
AI.conclusion: VT-VHF ground processing meets mission requirements by enabling timely GRB optical afterglow identification. Its performance validation establishes it as a reliable tool for prompt follow-up observations, enhancing the scientific return of SVOM through effective candidate prioritization and characterization.

title: Spectral Evidence of Heavy Nuclei from the Neutron Star Crust in Magnetar Bursts
summary: The crust of a neutron star (NS) provides a unique laboratory for studying matter under extreme density and magnetic field conditions that cannot be realized in terrestrial experiments. However, direct observational constraints on its composition have remained very limited. Magnetar bursts provide a promising means to probe the nuclear composition of the outer crust, as their energy release may be associated with stress-driven yielding of the crustal Coulomb lattice (including plastic deformation) and magnetic reconnection in the surrounding magnetosphere. We develop a general-purpose radiative transfer framework for a strongly magnetized electron--ion thermal plasma (MEITP) and apply it to the observed X-ray burst spectra. The spectral fits disfavor light-ion compositions and instead favor plasmas characterized by effective charge numbers around $Z \sim 37$. These results provide spectral evidence for the participation of heavy nuclei in magnetar bursts, offer new observational constraints on the baryonic content and the location of the emitting fireballs, and further imply a crustal origin of the heavy ions.

AI.tldr: The study uses X-ray burst spectra from magnetars to determine that the outer crust of neutron stars likely contains heavy nuclei with Z ~37, challenging previous light-ion assumptions and providing new constraints on crustal composition and fireball location.
AI.motivation: To probe the nuclear composition of neutron star crusts under extreme conditions where terrestrial experiments are impossible, addressing the lack of direct observational constraints.
AI.method: Developed a radiative transfer framework for magnetar outer crust plasma (MEITP) and applied it to observed X-ray burst spectra to analyze spectral features.
AI.result: Spectral fits disfavor light ions and indicate plasma with effective charge Z ~37, suggesting heavy nuclei participation in magnetar bursts.
AI.conclusion: Confirms heavy ion presence in neutron star crusts, constrains baryonic content and fireball emission location, supporting crustal origin of heavy ions.

title: Semiclassical phases of charged spin-$1/2$ matter-wave interferometers in gravitational wave backgrounds
summary: A matter wave propagating through curved spacetime accumulates phase that encodes both geometry and gauge structure. We develop a semiclassical framework for charged spin-$1/2$ matter-wave interferometers based on a WKB expansion of the covariant Dirac equation, in which the phase decomposes into dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic Aharonov-Bohm (AB) contributions. In a freely falling detector frame, all three channels are governed by local tidal fields. In a weak gravitational-wave (GW) background, the dynamical and spin phases probe the gravitoelectric and gravitomagnetic sectors of curvature, while the AB phase arises from curvature-induced electromagnetic fields obtained from Maxwell's equations in curved spacetime. For a Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI), all three responses are determined by the same tidal scale, $\ddot{h}_A \sim Ω^2_{gw}h_0$, and filtered by a common geometric kernel, while entering through distinct physical couplings. In particular, the AB contribution depends not only on the enclosed flux but also on spatial variations of the induced fields and exhibits an intrinsic frequency dependence set by the traversal time. These results provide a unified description of matter-wave interferometric phases in time-dependent GW backgrounds and identify complementary dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic pathways through which spacetime curvature imprints itself on quantum interference.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a semiclassical framework using a WKB approximation of the covariant Dirac equation to analyze phase contributions in charged spin-1/2 matter-wave interferometers in curved spacetime. It identifies three phase components—dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic Aharonov-Bohm (AB)—which respond to gravitational waves via distinct couplings but share a common tidal scaling. The framework unifies phase descriptions in time-dependent gravitational backgrounds.
AI.motivation: To understand how spacetime curvature and gauge fields imprint phases on matter waves, and to explore how gravitational waves affect charged spin-1/2 particles in interferometric setups through complementary dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic pathways.
AI.method: A semiclassical approach based on the WKB expansion of the covariant Dirac equation is developed. The phase decomposition into dynamical, spin, and AB contributions is analyzed in freely falling frames and weak gravitational-wave backgrounds. The methodology includes solving Maxwell's equations in curved spacetime to account for curvature-induced electromagnetic fields.
AI.result: The three phase contributions (dynamical, spin, AB) are shown to depend on the same tidal scale $\ddot{h}_A \sim \Omega^2_{gw}h_0$ and a common geometric kernel, but couple differently to gravitational waves. The AB phase uniquely depends on field variations and traversal time, revealing frequency dependence. This unifies the description of matter-wave interferometric phases in gravitational-wave backgrounds.
AI.conclusion: Matter-wave interferometers provide a multifaceted probe of spacetime curvature through distinct dynamical, spin, and electromagnetic channels. The overlap in tidal scaling but divergence in coupling mechanisms highlights the necessity of considering all three pathways for precise quantum measurements in gravitational physics.

title: SVOM/C-GFT: Instrumentation and Performances on the SVOM Alerts
comment: Accepted for publication in the SVOM special issue in Research in Astronomy and Astrophysics; 20 pages, 16 figures
summary: The Chinese Ground Follow-up Telescope (C-GFT) is an optical facility upgraded to support the Space Variable Objects Monitor mission (\textit{SVOM}). Located at the Jilin Observation Station, it is capable of rapidly identifying and monitoring the optical counterparts of Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs). The 1.2-m telescope is equipped with two switchable focal-plane instruments: the prime-focus wide-field LATIOS camera and the Cassegrain-focus three-channel CATCH camera. In this paper, we present a system overview, including the observatory, the telescope, the instrumentation, the automated operational framework managed by the Operations Center, and the data processing pipelines. We also report the performance results obtained during over one year of \textit{SVOM}'s post-launch operations. The results demonstrate that the system meets its design specifications and delivers robust observational and operational performance.

AI.tldr: The C-GFT telescope system, supporting the SVOM mission, successfully meets design specifications with its 1.2-m telescope and dual-camera setup, achieving robust performance in detecting GRB optical counterparts.
AI.motivation: To support the SVOM mission by rapidly identifying and monitoring optical counterparts of GRBs through an upgraded optical facility.
AI.method: The C-GFT uses a 1.2-m telescope with two instruments (LATIOS and CATCH cameras) and automated systems for operations and data processing.
AI.result: Post-launch operations over a year show the system meets design specs and performs reliably in GRB optical counterpart detection.
AI.conclusion: The C-GFT system is validated as effective and operational for its intended SVOM support role.

title: pyTANSPEC v1.0 and HxRGproc: Updated packages to Clean and Reduce TANSPEC data
comment: 15 pages, 12 figures, accepted for publication in Journal of Astrophysics and Astronomy on 27 April 2026
summary:
TIFR-ARIES Near-Infrared Spectrometer (TANSPEC) is a spectrograph-cum-imager operating over the wavelength range $0.55 - 2.5~μ$m. The instrument is mounted on the 3.6-m Devasthal Optical Telescope (3.6-m DOT). It offers two resolution modes: Low Resolution (LR) with $R\sim100-350$ and Cross-Dispersed (XD) via various slits of different widths (0.5", 0.75", 1.0", 1.5", 2.0" and 4.0"). The LR mode provides a resolving power ($R$) of $\sim 100-350$, while the XD mode achieves $R\sim2500$ using the 0.5" slit. The previous version of the data reduction pipeline supported only wavelength-calibrated XD mode spectra and was limited to two slits (S-0.5 and S-1.0). In this work, we present an upgraded version of pyTANSPEC. The upgraded pipeline not only improves the data extraction algorithm but also introduces several new features for users. It now enables the reduction of spectra from all available slits for both LR and XD modes. The upgraded version also implements a template-matching method for more precise wavelength calibration. Additionally, a step for flux calibration is also included.
  Alongside pyTANSPEC, we upgraded HxRGproc, a Python package for cleaning and generating slope images from Non-Destructive Readout (NDR) frames taken with H1RG and H2RG detectors. The package performs non-linearity correction, flags saturated pixels, removes pink noise, and eliminates cosmic ray events. HxRGproc is updated to work for the H2RG detector of TANSPEC and is set up on the TANSPEC server, ensuring users receive data that are pre-cleaned and non-linearity corrected.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an upgraded pyTANSPEC data reduction pipeline and an enhanced HxRGproc package for the TANSPEC instrument, improving data extraction, expanding slit support, adding flux calibration, and enabling precise wavelength calibration through template matching.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of the previous pipeline that only supported specific slits and lacked features for LR mode and flux calibration, as well as to ensure better processing of H2RG detector data for improved data quality.
AI.method: The upgraded pyTANSPEC includes improved data extraction algorithms, template-matching for wavelength calibration, flux calibration steps, and support for all slits in LR and XD modes. HxRGproc is updated to handle H2RG detectors, correct non-linearity, flag saturated pixels, remove pink noise, and eliminate cosmic rays.
AI.result: The new pipeline enables full support for all TANSPEC slits and modes, provides more accurate wavelength calibration, and flux-calibrated spectra. HxRGproc now effectively processes NDR frames, delivering pre-cleaned data to users.
AI.conclusion: The upgraded tools significantly enhance TANSPEC's data processing capabilities, ensuring reliable and efficient reduction of spectral and imaging data for astronomical research.

title: Robust Angles-Only Initial Relative Orbit Determination Using Polynomial Optimization
summary: This paper develops a robust angles-only IROD method based on polynomial optimization for arbitrary nonlinear dynamics. First, the relative motion is approximated by high-order Taylor polynomials within the differential algebra framework, and the resulting cross-product-residual minimization problem is solved through a recursive polynomial optimization procedure. Second, a reduced-order weighting strategy is introduced by projecting the residual onto the two-dimensional tangent subspace of the line of sight, thereby structurally removing the intrinsic singularity of conventional three-dimensional weighting. Third, a zero-solution-avoidance constraint together with an adaptive threshold-selection mechanism is developed to improve robustness against poor initialization, strong measurement noise, and unfavorable observation geometries. Numerical simulations show that the proposed method improves IROD accuracy by about three orders of magnitude relative to the baseline methods, while also reducing the downstream orbit-refinement burden. The reduced-order weighting strategy further improves accuracy by about 43% in the nominal case and remains stable under large-noise conditions, outperforming the conventional three-dimensional weighting by about 81%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a robust angles-only Inter-Orbit Ranging and Determination (IROD) method using polynomial optimization for nonlinear dynamics, addressing singularities and improving accuracy under noisy conditions.
AI.motivation: Conventional IROD methods suffer from singularities due to three-dimensional weighting, poor initialization, and sensitivity to noise. The need for higher accuracy and stability, especially in challenging observational scenarios, drives the development of this improved method.
AI.method: The method involves three key steps: (1) approximating relative motion with high-order Taylor polynomials in a differential algebra framework, (2) applying a reduced-order weighting strategy by projecting residuals onto a two-dimensional tangent subspace to eliminate singularities, and (3) incorporating a zero-solution-avoidance constraint with an adaptive threshold to enhance robustness against noise and poor initializations. These steps form a recursive polynomial optimization procedure.
AI.result: Numerical simulations demonstrate a 1,000x accuracy improvement over baseline methods. The reduced-order weighting strategy boosts accuracy by 43% in nominal cases and maintains stability under high noise, outperforming conventional 3D weighting by 81%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses limitations of existing IROD techniques, delivering significantly higher precision and robustness, particularly in noisy environments and unfavorable geometries, while reducing the need for subsequent orbit refinement.

title: An archive of reduced and telluric-corrected CRIRES+ L- and M-band spectra with slit-tilt and wavelength calibrations
summary: The high-resolution near-infrared spectrograph CRIRES+ at ESO VLT covers the Y, J, H, K, L and M bands. The U-Ne and Fabry-Perot calibration light sources, however, only work up to the K-band, leaving the bands L and M without wavelength calibration, and without a way to measure the inclination of the long slit relative to the detector frame. To remedy this, we present here a uniformly reprocessed archive of all public CRIRES+ L/M science observations obtained between September 2021 and March 2025, totalling 11 131 raw frames. We use the telluric modelling tool viper that fits a model to the plethora of atmospheric absorption features that exist around these wavelengths. We calibrate the slit tilt from the wavelength solutions for the nodding A and B frames that have the target in the lower and upper half of the slit, respectively. We then update the static inputs to the data reduction system with the slit tilt information and reduce the data with the standard pipeline recipes. Subsequently, we derive new wavelength scales for each observation from telluric fits on the spectra themselves, additionally interpolating the solutions for spectra that have no tellurics from the ones that have. The resulting 5649 extracted, calibrated and telluric-fitted AB nod-pair spectra, spanning 156 unique targets from 68 ESO programmes, are served through an interactive web archive at https://www.astro.uu.se/crires-lm that offers data downloads and figures for all datasets that allow an initial judgement of the data quality.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the lack of wavelength calibration and slit tilt measurement for CRIRES+ L/M bands by creating a reprocessed archive of public observations using telluric modeling and updated reduction techniques, providing a valuable resource accessible via a web interface.
AI.motivation: The U-Ne and Fabry-Perot calibration sources for CRIRES+ only work up to the K-band, leaving L and M bands without proper calibration and slit tilt measurement, which are essential for accurate data reduction.
AI.method: The authors reprocessed 11,131 raw L/M band frames using the viper telluric modeling tool to derive wavelength solutions and slit tilt from nodding A/B frames. They updated the data reduction pipeline with this information, applied standard recipes, and interpolated telluric fits for spectra lacking absorption features. The final 5649 spectra are organized in an interactive web archive.
AI.result: Produced a comprehensive archive of calibrated CRIRES+ L/M band spectra with accurate wavelength and slit tilt corrections, covering 156 targets from 68 programs. The web platform enables easy access and quality assessment of the data.
AI.conclusion: The created archive significantly improves data quality and usability for L/M band observations, filling a calibration gap and providing a community resource for astronomical research requiring high-resolution near-infrared spectroscopy.
